reason: Within an 80+ cohort, residual age variation may weakly track frailty/renal reserve. Likely adds limited unique signal once comorbidities and periop physiology are available, so low sparse-selection priority.

Sex relates to baseline muscle mass/creatinine generation and AKI risk differences. However, the outcome is a creatinine ratio (partly normalizing baseline), so incremental predictive value is modest.

Race category is a coarse demographic proxy and not a direct perioperative renal insult marker. In sparse modeling it is typically dominated by baseline creatinine/comorbidities and adds limited unique value.

Race may correlate with baseline kidney disease risk and creatinine generation, but this is indirect and often confounded. For a creatinine ratio target, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus physiologic/renal features.
Broad race grouping is an indirect proxy for risk and body composition rather than a near-term AKI signal. Low expected unique contribution in a sparse linear model.
Demographic category with limited direct mechanistic link to postoperative renal trajectory. Likely low prevalence and weak incremental value, making it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Coarse demographic proxy and likely sparse/low-frequency category. Unlikely to add stable, unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Heterogeneous catch-all category that is hard to interpret physiologically. Typically noisy and dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.
Ethnicity is an indirect sociodemographic proxy and not a perioperative renal injury marker. Expected to be redundant with other clinical variables and low priority for sparse selection.
Height mainly reflects body size and is only indirectly related to creatinine kinetics. With weight available, it is largely redundant and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Weight captures body size/volume of distribution and may relate to dosing, hemodilution, and renal reserve. Still indirect for AKI and often redundant with more proximal physiologic signals, so modest priority.
Represents long-term genetic cardiovascular risk rather than acute postoperative renal hypoperfusion/toxicity. Very unlikely to contribute unique predictive information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Diabetes is a strong predisposing factor for CKD and postoperative AKI via microvascular disease and reduced renal reserve. It is a stable, non-redundant baseline risk factor likely to survive sparse screening.
Diabetes control status is a coarse proxy for chronic glycemic burden and may be inconsistently defined. Adds some nuance beyond diabetes but is often noisy/redundant, so lower sparse-model priority.

Dyslipidemia reflects chronic atherosclerotic risk but is not a direct driver of short-term postoperative creatinine rise. Likely redundant with other comorbidity/severity markers and low value for sparse selection.

Hypertension is associated with chronic nephrosclerosis and reduced renal reserve, increasing AKI susceptibility. Useful baseline risk information, though somewhat correlated with age/diabetes and thus moderate priority.

History of infective endocarditis implies prior severe infection/valve pathology and possible baseline frailty. It is an indirect, low-prevalence signal and likely dominated by perioperative hemodynamics/labs for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Chronic lung disease reflects limited pulmonary reserve and higher risk of hypoxemia/ventilator dependence. It is a nonspecific severity marker and usually adds little unique renal-prediction value beyond closer physiologic variables.

Home oxygen use is a proxy for advanced chronic lung/cardiopulmonary disease. It may correlate with postoperative instability but is indirect and typically redundant with more proximal ICU oxygenation/hemodynamic features.
Bronchodilator therapy mainly indicates obstructive lung disease treatment patterns. It is a weak clinician-behavior/comorbidity proxy with minimal unique linkage to AKI trajectory at 60 hours.

Sleep apnea is a chronic comorbidity associated with cardiometabolic risk but not a direct perioperative renal insult marker. In sparse selection it is usually dominated by baseline creatinine and postoperative perfusion/vasopressor signals.

Pneumonia indicates active infection/inflammation and potential hypoxemia/sepsis physiology that can precipitate AKI. It can add some unique risk information, though still indirect compared with hemodynamics, lactate, and renal labs.

IV drug abuse is a social/behavioral history that may correlate with endocarditis or chronic infections. It is low-prevalence and indirect, unlikely to survive sparse screening for creatinine ratio prediction.
Depression is a chronic psychosocial comorbidity with limited mechanistic connection to acute postoperative renal dysfunction. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.
Alcohol use can reflect liver disease, malnutrition, and perioperative vulnerability. It is a broad proxy and typically redundant with direct hepatic/lab and hemodynamic indicators relevant to AKI.

Liver disease captures impaired perfusion reserve, inflammation, and altered drug handling, all associated with higher AKI risk. It is still indirect but can provide unique baseline risk not fully captured by pulmonary comorbidities.
Immunosuppression indicates infection risk and chronic illness burden. For Hour-60 creatinine ratio it is mostly an indirect risk marker and often dominated by perioperative physiologic derangements.

Prior mediastinal radiation reflects complex cardiac surgical history and tissue fragility. It has weak direct linkage to postoperative renal function and is unlikely to be selected over physiologic predictors.

Cancer is a general comorbidity that may correlate with frailty, inflammation, or prior nephrotoxic exposures. It is nonspecific and usually provides limited incremental value for AKI prediction versus ICU course variables.

Peripheral artery disease is a marker of systemic atherosclerosis and renal microvascular disease, increasing susceptibility to hypoperfusion-related AKI. It is a relatively specific baseline risk factor that can add unique value in sparse selection.

Thoracic aortic disease implies complex surgery (often longer bypass/circulatory arrest) and embolic/hypoperfusion risk. It can be informative but may be partially redundant with operative time/hemodynamic instability features if those are available.
Syncope is a symptom history with heterogeneous causes and limited specificity for renal vulnerability. It is unlikely to contribute unique predictive signal for postoperative creatinine ratio.

History of unresponsive state is a nonspecific neurologic comorbidity marker. It is largely an indirect frailty/illness-severity proxy and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio versus hemodynamic/renal-specific signals.

Cerebrovascular disease reflects systemic atherosclerosis and baseline vascular risk. It may weakly correlate with renal vulnerability but is indirect and typically dominated by more proximal perfusion/renal markers in a sparse model.

Baseline hemoglobin captures anemia and oxygen delivery reserve, which can influence renal ischemic risk during/after surgery. Moderately useful but indirect and often redundant with transfusion/bleeding/hemodynamic variables not shown in this batch.

Pre-op albumin is a marker of nutrition/inflammation and frailty, associated with postoperative complications including AKI. It can add some baseline risk stratification but remains a nonspecific severity proxy rather than a direct renal signal.

Baseline bilirubin reflects hepatic dysfunction/cholestasis and overall organ reserve. It is an indirect predictor of AKI risk and is likely redundant with stronger global severity indices (e.g., MELD) within this batch.

HbA1c reflects chronic glycemic control and diabetic microvascular disease, which increases susceptibility to AKI. Useful baseline risk information, but still indirect and may be partially captured by other comorbidity/renal baseline variables outside this batch.

HIT antibodies indicate a specific anticoagulation complication risk rather than renal physiology. Any association with creatinine change is likely rare, mediated by downstream events, and not a priority for sparse selection.

MELD score (including creatinine component) summarizes baseline hepatorenal dysfunction and physiologic reserve. It is a strong pre-op risk stratifier for postoperative AKI and likely provides more unique signal than bilirubin or albumin alone.

BNP reflects cardiac filling pressures and heart failure severity, which relates to renal congestion and low forward flow after cardiac surgery. It is a strong, relatively unique baseline predictor of postoperative renal dysfunction in this age group.

5-meter walk test is a frailty/functional status measure associated with postoperative complications. It is indirect for kidney injury and often redundant with other severity/frailty markers, so lower sparse-model priority.
History of prior cardiovascular intervention is a broad marker of coronary/vascular disease burden. It is nonspecific and likely redundant with more granular prior CABG/PCI variables in this batch.

Prior CABG indicates advanced coronary disease and potentially higher surgical complexity. It may weakly relate to AKI risk but is indirect and overlaps with other prior cardiac surgery/intervention indicators.

Prior cardiac surgery suggests reoperative status and higher operative complexity, which can increase AKI risk via longer bypass/bleeding/hemodynamic instability. More informative than generic intervention history, but still a proxy rather than a direct renal marker.
Prior PCI reflects coronary disease burden and prior contrast exposure history. It is indirect for postoperative creatinine trajectory and overlaps with other PCI-specific features here.

PCI during the same episode implies recent contrast load and acute coronary instability, which can contribute to early renal injury risk. More temporally relevant than remote PCI, though still indirect without actual contrast dose/renal labs.

Presence of a stent is a subtype of prior PCI and mainly reflects coronary disease history. It is largely redundant with the broader PCI variables and offers limited unique signal for creatinine ratio prediction.

Recency of prior cardiac procedures reflects baseline cardiovascular complexity and possible cumulative contrast/surgical burden. It is an indirect, pre-op proxy for risk and likely dominated by nearer-term hemodynamics/renal signals for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Count/history of prior cardiac interventions captures chronic disease severity and reoperative status. Useful context but nonspecific for AKI and typically redundant with more proximal perioperative instability and perfusion markers in a sparse model.

Recent ACE inhibitor exposure can predispose to perioperative hypotension and reduced renal autoregulation. It can add some unique pre-op susceptibility signal, but remains indirect and often outweighed by post-op perfusion/vasopressor features.

Recent P2Y12 inhibitor use mainly reflects antiplatelet management and bleeding risk. Any association with creatinine rise is indirect (transfusion/bleeding pathways) and usually weak for sparse selection.

Amiodarone use is a marker of arrhythmia burden and cardiac disease severity. It is not a direct renal-risk driver and is likely redundant with hemodynamic instability variables captured postoperatively.

Pre-op anticoagulation indicates AF/VTE/mechanical valve and may relate to bleeding/transfusion risk. This is an indirect pathway to AKI and typically a weaker, behavior-driven proxy compared with direct perfusion and renal markers.
Aspirin use is common and mostly reflects baseline CAD prevention rather than renal physiology. It is unlikely to provide unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Beta-blocker use reflects chronic cardiac management and may influence perioperative heart rate/cardiac output. The renal signal is indirect and often redundant with measured post-op hemodynamics and vasoactive support.

Duration of beta-blocker therapy is largely a refinement of the beta-blocker exposure signal. It adds little incremental information beyond the simpler pre-op beta-blocker indicator and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
CCB therapy duration is a chronic medication marker with weak mechanistic linkage to postoperative AKI. It is low-yield and likely redundant with broader comorbidity/hemodynamic features.

GPIIb/IIIa inhibitor use is uncommon and mainly indicates acute coronary syndrome/PCI-related management. Any renal association is indirect and confounded by acuity/bleeding, making it a weak sparse-model candidate.

Need for inotropes preoperatively signals low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock physiology and high risk of renal hypoperfusion. This is a strong pre-op severity marker with relatively unique predictive value for subsequent creatinine rise.
Statin/lipid-lowering therapy is prevalent and primarily reflects chronic preventive care. It has minimal direct short-term renal predictive signal and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Chronic nitrate therapy indicates angina/CAD symptom management. It is an indirect comorbidity marker with little unique information for predicting postoperative creatinine ratio.

IV nitrates suggest active ischemia/heart failure management and potentially tenuous hemodynamics. Still indirect for AKI and often redundant with direct measures of perfusion/vasopressor/inotrope requirements.
Other antianginal therapy duration is a broad, nonspecific chronic treatment marker. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond core cardiac severity and post-op physiologic variables.

Pre-op steroid exposure may reflect chronic inflammatory disease and can affect glucose/infection risk. It is an indirect, low-specificity AKI signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics/renal markers.

Recent direct thrombin inhibitor use mainly reflects anticoagulation management and bleeding risk. It has little direct mechanistic link to postoperative creatinine rise and is mostly a care-pattern proxy.

Warfarin use indicates chronic anticoagulation (e.g., AF/mechanical valve) and periop reversal/bleeding considerations. It is not a direct renal perfusion/toxicity marker and is likely weak and redundant with broader comorbidity/severity features.
Factor Xa inhibitor use is primarily a medication-history/bleeding-risk indicator. It provides minimal unique information about evolving AKI compared with physiologic and renal-function variables.

Thrombin inhibitor use within 5 days is another anticoagulation-history feature. It is largely redundant with the 24-hour version and remains an indirect proxy with low expected predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Statin use is a marker of chronic atherosclerotic disease and may correlate with baseline cardiovascular risk. Any renal-protective/renal-risk association is modest and confounded, so it is a low-priority sparse-model feature.

Pre-op catheterization can imply recent contrast exposure and higher coronary disease burden, both linked to AKI risk. It may add some unique signal (contrast/complexity) but is still indirect versus periop hemodynamics and renal labs.

Known coronary anatomy disease indicates underlying CAD burden. It is a broad comorbidity marker and likely redundant with more granular CAD measures (e.g., number of diseased vessels) and periop instability variables.

Extent of CAD reflects surgical complexity and risk of low cardiac output/hypoperfusion post-op. It can contribute unique baseline risk stratification, but remains indirect compared with direct perfusion/renal trajectory features.

Aortic valve disease (often AS) is associated with periop hemodynamic vulnerability and potential renal hypoperfusion. It may add some unique baseline physiologic risk, though much of its effect is mediated through post-op hemodynamics.

Mitral valve disease relates to chronic heart failure/pulmonary hypertension and periop complexity. Its renal predictive value is mostly indirect and likely captured better by post-op hemodynamics/vasopressor needs.

Tricuspid disease can indicate right-sided failure and venous congestion, which is relevant to cardiorenal syndrome. It may provide some unique congestion-related risk not fully captured by CAD counts, but still indirect.
Pulmonic valve disease is uncommon and typically not a primary driver of postoperative AKI in this context. It is likely sparse/noisy and low-yield for selection.

Prior cardiac surgeries signal redo sternotomy/adhesions and higher operative complexity, which can increase AKI risk via longer bypass/bleeding/instability. Useful baseline risk information, but still a proxy compared with direct periop physiologic data.

A generic status variable likely encodes administrative/clinical category (e.g., elective/urgent) and overall acuity. It can correlate with AKI risk but is nonspecific and often redundant with more concrete periop features.
Case cancellation is an operational outcome and not a physiologic marker of renal injury. It is unlikely to generalize or add meaningful predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine_ratio.

Conversion (e.g., minimally invasive/robotic to open) is a marker of intraop complexity/complications that can increase AKI risk. Useful as a relatively unique procedural-severity signal, but still indirect compared with hemodynamics/renal markers.

Robotic approach mainly reflects case selection and operative technique rather than renal physiology. Likely weak/indirect for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and partly redundant with other procedure-type indicators.

CABG status captures a major procedure category associated with CPB exposure, hemodynamic swings, and AKI risk. Moderately useful for baseline risk stratification but indirect and often dominated by more granular perfusion/hemodynamic features.

Valve surgery indicates a different operative phenotype (often longer CPB/cross-clamp, older/frailer physiology) linked to AKI risk. Adds some unique procedural context but remains an indirect proxy versus post-op renal perfusion signals.

Additional cardiac procedures suggest increased operative complexity and duration, which can worsen renal insult. Potentially additive as a complexity marker, but heterogeneous and less specific than direct perfusion/temperature/anemia variables.
AF ablation/maze procedures add time and inflammation but are not strongly kidney-specific. Likely modest incremental value and often redundant with broader “procedure complexity/duration” signals.

Aortic procedures (especially arch/descending) are strongly associated with prolonged CPB, hypothermia, embolic risk, and renal hypoperfusion. This is a high-yield procedural risk marker likely to survive sparse selection within this batch.
Non-cardiac concomitant procedures are a nonspecific marker of complexity and comorbidity burden. Heterogeneous and indirect, so lower priority for a sparse model.

Appropriate prophylactic antibiotic choice is primarily a quality/process metric rather than a renal physiology signal. Any association with creatinine ratio is likely weak and confounded by documentation and care pathways.
Antibiotic timing is a periop process-of-care indicator with minimal direct linkage to AKI at 60 hours. Low unique predictive value for sparse selection.
Discontinuation ordering reflects protocol adherence and clinician behavior more than kidney injury biology. Unlikely to add meaningful independent signal for creatinine ratio.

An extra prophylactic dose may proxy longer operative duration or blood loss, which can relate to AKI risk. Still indirect and likely dominated by more direct duration/complexity or anemia/temperature signals.

Lowest core temperature reflects hypothermia strategy and often correlates with CPB complexity/arch work and systemic stress that can impact renal perfusion. Stronger, more specific physiologic/procedural severity signal than most process metrics in this batch.

Nadir intraop hemoglobin captures hemodilution/bleeding and reduced oxygen delivery, a plausible contributor to renal ischemic injury. High-value physiologic insult marker with relatively direct linkage to AKI risk.

Intraoperative hyperglycemia is a general stress/diabetes control marker and is not kidney-specific. Likely modest incremental value and often redundant with overall illness severity and comorbidity features.

Use of cardiopulmonary/coronary perfusion indicates CPB-related technique and myocardial protection strategy, which can correlate with systemic perfusion and AKI risk. Moderately informative but less directly interpretable than nadir temperature/hemoglobin or specific high-risk procedure types (e.g., aortic).

Aortic arterial cannulation is a common CPB configuration and mainly reflects operative approach rather than renal physiology. Likely adds little unique predictive value beyond bypass time/arrest variables and is often the reference/most prevalent category.

Femoral arterial cannulation often indicates peripheral cannulation, vascular disease, or emergent/redo contexts that can increase embolic/low-flow risk. It may add some unique risk signal for AKI beyond CPB time, but is still an indirect procedural proxy.

Axillary cannulation is used in complex aortic surgery and may correlate with circulatory arrest strategies and altered perfusion patterns. Provides some incremental context for renal risk, but is partly redundant with arrest/DHCA and bypass duration.

Innominate cannulation is typically tied to arch/aortic procedures and selective perfusion strategies, which can co-occur with higher AKI risk. Moderately useful as a marker of case complexity but largely overlaps with circulatory arrest/DHCA variables.

Other arterial cannulation sites are heterogeneous and often encode idiosyncratic surgical choices or documentation noise. Weak, non-specific proxy that is unlikely to survive sparse selection when more direct perfusion-duration features exist.

Femoral venous cannulation can reflect peripheral cannulation/ECMO-like setups or access limitations, but is an indirect marker of complexity. Likely redundant with arterial site and bypass/arrest duration for predicting creatinine rise.

Jugular venous cannulation is uncommon and usually reflects special access needs or complex support. It is a weak, indirect proxy for severity and unlikely to add stable unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Right atrial venous cannulation is standard for many CPB cases and mostly indicates routine technique. Minimal incremental information for AKI risk beyond CPB time and arrest variables.

Left atrial venous cannulation is procedure-specific (e.g., certain valve/redo contexts) but not directly tied to renal hypoperfusion. Any association with AKI is indirect and likely dominated by bypass/arrest duration.
Pulmonary venous cannulation is rare and heterogeneous, often reflecting unusual operative circumstances. Too sparse/noisy and indirect to be a good sparse-model keeper for creatinine ratio.

Bicaval cannulation reflects specific intracardiac procedures and may correlate with longer/complex operations. Still an indirect procedural marker and largely redundant with cardiopulmonary bypass time.
Other venous cannulation sites are heterogeneous and likely capture documentation variability more than physiology. Very weak candidate compared with direct perfusion-duration and arrest features.

Total CPB time captures duration of non-physiologic perfusion, inflammation/hemolysis, and low-flow exposure—strong drivers of postoperative AKI. High unique predictive value and a classic top sparse-model feature for creatinine rise.

Use of circulatory arrest indicates profound low/no-flow periods and high-complexity aortic surgery, both strongly linked to AKI risk. Adds distinct information beyond CPB time (binary exposure to extreme perfusion insult).

DHCA time quantifies the duration of extreme systemic hypoperfusion/ischemia, a near-direct precursor to renal injury. Likely one of the strongest unique predictors in this batch, especially beyond the binary arrest indicator.

Cerebral perfusion use marks arch strategy and may correlate with longer arrest/complexity and altered systemic flow management. Moderately informative but largely overlaps with circulatory arrest and DHCA time, so less priority than duration measures.

Duration of selective cerebral perfusion is a marker of complex arch surgery and prolonged nonphysiologic perfusion. It is an indirect severity/procedure proxy for AKI risk and likely redundant with circulatory arrest and cross-clamp times in a sparse model.

Minutes of circulatory arrest reflect profound systemic hypoperfusion/ischemia during surgery, a strong precursor to postoperative AKI. Adds relatively unique signal about global ischemic burden beyond routine case duration.

Binary indicator of aortic occlusion/cross-clamping is common in many cardiac cases and mainly reflects procedure type. Provides limited incremental information compared with continuous cross-clamp time and arrest time.

Cross-clamp duration captures ischemic time and correlates with CPB complexity, inflammation, and hemodynamic stress that predispose to AKI. Strong, interpretable intraop exposure likely to survive sparse selection, though partially correlated with other duration metrics.

Use of cerebral oximetry is largely a monitoring/clinician-practice indicator rather than a physiologic renal signal. Likely confounded by institutional protocols and case type, offering weak unique predictive value.

Diffuse aortic calcification reflects systemic atherosclerosis and vascular disease burden, which can associate with renal vulnerability. However it is indirect and often overlaps with comorbidity/complexity signals, so lower priority for sparsity.

Whether the ascending aorta/arch was assessed is primarily a documentation/procedural workflow marker. It is a weak, non-physiologic proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Plaque assessment is mainly a screening/monitoring behavior variable tied to surgical planning. It is indirect and likely redundant with more direct complexity measures (cross-clamp/arrest), so low sparse-model priority.

Indicates intraoperative change in plan due to aortic pathology, signaling higher procedural complexity and potential embolic/ischemic risk. Still an indirect proxy and likely captured by arrest/cross-clamp time and transfusion needs.
Refusal of blood products reflects patient preference/consent rather than physiologic insult. It is not a direct renal risk marker and is unlikely to generalize as a predictor.

Overall blood product exposure is a marker of bleeding, hemodilution, inflammation, and transfusion-associated renal risk. Useful but somewhat redundant with specific component units (especially RBCs) for sparse selection.

RBC transfusion volume is a strong proxy for bleeding/hemolysis and oxygen delivery issues, and is consistently associated with postoperative AKI risk. Likely the most informative transfusion component and a good sparse-model keeper.
FFP units reflect coagulopathy/bleeding severity and large-volume transfusion. Adds some signal but is typically secondary/redundant once RBC transfusion and overall product exposure are included.

Platelet transfusion indicates bleeding or platelet dysfunction (often CPB-related) and correlates with case complexity. Predictive value is plausible but usually overlaps with RBC/total transfusion signals.

Cryoprecipitate use suggests severe bleeding/hypofibrinogenemia and high transfusion burden. It is relatively infrequent and largely redundant with broader bleeding/transfusion markers in a sparse model.

Clotting factor administration (e.g., PCC/factor concentrates) reflects significant coagulopathy/bleeding management and may track severity. However it is a treatment-choice proxy and overlaps with transfusion/complexity measures, limiting unique value.

Use of aminocaproic acid is mainly a bleeding-management practice pattern rather than a direct renal physiology marker. Likely redundant with stronger downstream signals (hemodynamics, transfusion/bleeding severity) and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Tranexamic acid administration reflects antifibrinolytic strategy and bleeding risk, not kidney injury directly. Any association with AKI is indirect and typically dominated by hypotension/vasopressors, lactate, and fluid balance features.
TEE performed is largely a procedural/monitoring indicator and proxy for case complexity. It provides little unique quantitative signal for Hour-60 creatinine beyond other physiologic variables.

Residual aortic insufficiency can worsen forward flow and contribute to low effective perfusion, which can predispose to AKI. Predictive value is indirect and likely weaker than direct hemodynamic/vasopressor measures, but may add some unique cardiac-performance signal.

Residual mitral regurgitation can drive pulmonary congestion and low cardiac output physiology affecting renal perfusion. Useful but indirect, and often partially captured by EF and postoperative hemodynamics, so moderate sparse-model priority.

Residual tricuspid regurgitation is closely tied to right-sided failure and venous congestion, a strong mechanism for postoperative AKI (congestive nephropathy). This can add unique predictive value beyond generic hypotension metrics, so a strong sparse candidate.

Indicator of valve surgery reflects procedure type/complexity and correlates with bypass time and hemodynamic perturbations. It is a coarse proxy and likely redundant with more proximal physiologic features, so lower priority.

Atrial fibrillation procedure (e.g., MAZE/ablation) is mainly a marker of operative complexity and baseline atrial disease. Any renal signal is indirect and typically dominated by postoperative perfusion/volume status variables.
General anesthesia is near-universal in cardiac surgery and offers minimal discrimination. It adds little unique information for predicting creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

PCC use suggests significant bleeding/coagulopathy and may correlate with shock/transfusion burden that can affect kidneys. However it is a clinician-action proxy and usually redundant with direct hemodynamic and lab severity signals.

Post-repair aortic gradient reflects valve hemodynamics and potential residual obstruction affecting cardiac output. It is indirect for renal outcomes and often less informative than direct measures of perfusion/vasopressor need, so modest priority.

Paravalvular leak can cause hemolysis and/or reduced effective forward flow, both potentially impacting renal function. Still relatively uncommon and indirect, but may contribute unique signal not fully captured by EF alone.

Mitral gradient indicates residual stenosis/obstruction and can affect filling and output, indirectly influencing renal perfusion. Typically a weaker and more redundant signal compared with global hemodynamics and EF.
Mitral PVL can drive hemolysis and heart failure physiology, which can contribute to AKI risk. Predictive value is indirect but potentially unique versus simple MR severity, so moderate priority.

This appears to represent residual valvular leak status but overlaps conceptually with the separate MR/TR insufficiency variables. Likely redundant/ambiguous compared with the more specific insufficiency measures, lowering sparse-model priority.

Post-procedure EF summarizes left ventricular systolic function and is a key determinant of renal perfusion and low-cardiac-output AKI risk. It provides a compact, relatively unique cardiac performance signal that can complement hemodynamic time-series features in a sparse model.

Indicates planned PCI (care pathway/strategy) rather than a physiologic renal insult. Likely a weak, indirect proxy for coronary complexity and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Use of an intra-aortic balloon pump signals significant low-output/cardiogenic shock physiology and need for mechanical support. This is a strong, relatively unique marker of renal hypoperfusion risk and often survives sparse selection.

Baseline cath-lab assist device use reflects preop hemodynamic instability/complexity. It is an indirect severity proxy and is likely dominated by more proximal postoperative support markers (e.g., IABP/ECMO) for predicting AKI trajectory.

ECMO use indicates profound cardiopulmonary failure with high risk of renal hypoperfusion, inflammation, hemolysis, and fluid overload. It is a top-tier, high-signal feature for subsequent creatinine rise and likely highly selected in a sparse model.

Blood product transfusion volume reflects bleeding/CPB-related inflammation and can contribute to AKI risk via hemodilution, hypotension, and transfusion-related effects. Useful but partly a general severity/operative complexity proxy and may be redundant with postop anemia measures.

Extubation in the OR is a marker of uncomplicated course and better immediate physiology. It is an indirect, clinician-pathway proxy and less directly tied to renal injury than hemodynamic support variables.

Re-intubation suggests respiratory failure or clinical deterioration, often correlating with shock, fluid overload, or sepsis-like physiology that can worsen kidney function. Moderately informative but nonspecific and may be redundant with direct hemodynamic support markers.

ICU visit/transfer is largely a workflow/triage variable with limited physiologic specificity in a post-cardiac surgery cohort. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio beyond actual support and lab variables.

Postoperative hemoglobin captures anemia/hemodilution and bleeding burden, which can contribute to renal hypoxia and AKI risk. Moderately useful, but overlaps strongly with hematocrit and transfusion variables, limiting sparse-model uniqueness.
Postoperative hematocrit is another anemia/hemodilution measure. It is largely redundant with postoperative hemoglobin (typically the stronger/cleaner signal), so lower priority for sparse selection.

Postoperative intubation status reflects immediate postoperative severity and sedation/ventilation needs. It is a broad illness-severity proxy and generally less specific for renal trajectory than mechanical circulatory support variables.

History of prior MI reflects baseline cardiac reserve and vascular disease burden. It may weakly relate to AKI risk via chronic low-output propensity but is indirect and often dominated by perioperative hemodynamic support indicators.

Timing of prior MI is a nuanced historical detail with uncertain incremental value beyond the presence of prior MI itself. Likely low signal-to-noise and not a priority feature for sparse prediction of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Symptom timing at admission is a preop presentation descriptor and mainly a proxy for urgency/trajectory. It is distant from the postoperative renal injury window and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Symptom timing at surgery similarly reflects urgency and preop course rather than postoperative renal physiology. Low direct linkage to AKI evolution compared with postoperative support and perfusion markers.

Angina class measures symptom severity of coronary disease but not postoperative perfusion or renal insult directly. In a sparse model it is likely dominated by objective postoperative instability markers (ECMO/IABP, transfusion, anemia).

NYHA functional class reflects baseline heart failure symptom burden and chronic congestion risk. It is an indirect, preop severity marker and likely dominated by more proximal hemodynamic/renal signals for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Cardiogenic shock indicates profound low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk. It is a strong upstream driver of AKI and can add unique predictive value beyond generic comorbidity flags.

Resuscitation suggests perioperative instability (hypotension/arrest) with potential ischemic kidney insult. Useful but partly a coarse proxy for severity and may be redundant with shock/vasopressor/hemodynamic features not shown in this batch.

A broad arrhythmia indicator captures baseline rhythm issues but is nonspecific. It is likely redundant with the more specific arrhythmia subtype variables in this batch and only indirectly linked to AKI.

Recent VT/VF implies severe cardiac instability and potential low-flow episodes that can precipitate AKI. Predictive but relatively infrequent and often mediated through shock/resuscitation pathways, limiting unique value in a sparse model.
Sick sinus syndrome mainly reflects conduction system disease and pacing need rather than renal perfusion. Weak and indirect for AKI prediction and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Atrial flutter reflects atrial arrhythmia burden and may relate to hemodynamic compromise or anticoagulation complexity. Generally indirect for renal injury and often redundant with AF/overall arrhythmia indicators.

Second-degree AV block is primarily a conduction abnormality and usually not a direct driver of renal hypoperfusion. Low unique predictive value for creatinine rise compared with shock/heart failure markers.
Third-degree AV block can cause bradycardia and low output if not paced, potentially affecting renal perfusion. Still an indirect and often treated/mitigated signal, so moderate-low priority.

Pacemaker dependence indicates underlying conduction disease and may correlate with cardiac comorbidity. It is a weak proxy for renal risk and typically less informative than direct hemodynamic instability features.

Heart failure captures baseline low cardiac reserve and venous congestion, both strong contributors to postoperative AKI risk. High-priority comorbidity likely to add unique signal when proximal renal labs/urine output are not in this batch.

Recent atrial fibrillation is common and can worsen cardiac output and reflect systemic illness burden. Moderately predictive but partly redundant with heart failure and generic arrhythmia indicators.
Preoperative pain score is largely unrelated to renal physiology and more reflective of subjective symptoms/analgesic needs. Very low expected unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Cell saver volume is a proxy for surgical blood loss/transfusion avoidance and case complexity. It may weakly relate to hypotension/inflammation but is indirect and likely dominated by direct hemodynamic/lab features in a sparse model.
Heparin dose before bypass mainly reflects protocolized anticoagulation and patient size rather than renal injury. Minimal unique predictive value for postoperative creatinine trajectory.

Protamine dose reflects heparin reversal and can be associated with hemodynamic reactions, but is mostly protocol/size-driven. At best a weak proxy for intraop complexity and unlikely to be a top sparse-model keeper.

Intraoperative viscoelastic testing (e.g., TEG/ROTEM) reflects coagulopathy/bleeding risk and transfusion propensity. It is at best an indirect severity/procedure-complexity proxy for AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics/renal markers.

Indicator/amount of any volatile anesthetic use is primarily an anesthesia practice marker. It has weak, nonspecific linkage to postoperative creatinine trajectory and is dominated by more direct perfusion/renal-injury signals.

Isoflurane exposure mainly captures anesthetic choice rather than renal physiology. Any association with AKI is indirect and likely redundant with overall case complexity and hemodynamic variables not in this batch.

Sevoflurane exposure is largely clinician-choice/practice pattern; modern use has minimal direct nephrotoxicity signal in this context. Low unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
Desflurane exposure reflects anesthetic selection and fast emergence strategy more than kidney risk. It is unlikely to add unique predictive information beyond general intraop course variables.
Other volatile agent use is a heterogeneous category and mostly a documentation/practice proxy. Heterogeneity and indirectness make it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Volatile use pre-bypass is timing of anesthetic delivery rather than a renal insult marker. It is unlikely to predict later creatinine ratio once more direct perfusion/AKI precursors are considered.

Volatile use during bypass may weakly correlate with bypass management strategy and case complexity. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct renal hypoperfusion/toxin signal, so low priority.

Post-bypass volatile use can correlate with immediate post-CPB instability and sedation needs. However it remains indirect and likely redundant with hemodynamic/vasopressor data, so only weak-to-moderate value.
Maintenance volatile use is a broad anesthesia practice indicator. It provides little unique information about evolving AKI compared with physiologic and renal biomarkers.

Dexmedetomidine infusion reflects sedation strategy and may correlate with hemodynamic profile (bradycardia/hypotension) and extubation plans. Any renal signal is indirect and likely redundant with direct perfusion measures.

Propofol infusion is primarily an anesthetic management variable; it can influence blood pressure but is not a direct kidney injury marker. Limited unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in sparse selection.
Midazolam infusion mainly captures sedation choice and longer/complex cases. It is a weak, indirect proxy for illness severity and unlikely to add unique AKI prediction signal.

Total intraop insulin reflects hyperglycemia/insulin resistance and diabetes severity, which are associated with AKI risk. It may add some unique metabolic-stress signal, though still partly a treatment/behavior proxy.

Pre-anesthesia systolic BP reflects baseline hemodynamic reserve and chronic hypertension/hypotension risk, which can modify renal perfusion vulnerability. Useful baseline context but indirect and less predictive than post-op hypotension extremes.

Pre-anesthesia diastolic BP is a baseline vascular tone/perfusion marker but is noisier and less directly tied to renal outcomes than systolic/MAP-related measures. Likely redundant with other hemodynamic context, so lower priority.

Pre-induction heart rate reflects baseline sympathetic tone/fitness and may correlate with frailty or hemodynamic reserve. It is an indirect, nonspecific severity marker and is usually dominated by postoperative hemodynamics for predicting AKI trajectory.

Use of a PA catheter is largely a clinician-choice proxy for perceived cardiac complexity/instability. It may weakly tag higher-risk patients but is indirect and confounded by practice patterns, so low priority in a sparse model.

Maximum core temperature is a nonspecific perioperative physiologic variable and often reflects warming strategy or transient inflammation. It has weak, indirect linkage to later creatinine rise and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Inhaled nitric oxide use suggests pulmonary hypertension/RV strain and higher cardiopulmonary complexity. This is an indirect proxy for risk of low cardiac output/venous congestion affecting kidneys, but is treatment-pattern dependent and not a direct renal signal.

Total intraoperative crystalloid reflects volume loading/hemodilution and can contribute to renal congestion or signal hemodynamic instability/bleeding. It can add some unique perioperative context, but is partly redundant with later fluid balance and severity markers.

Synthetic colloid volume captures exposure to fluids that may be used in higher-risk cases and (depending on agent) can be associated with renal injury. It may carry unique signal beyond crystalloid, but is still confounded by indication and practice variation.

Albumin administration reflects intravascular volume strategy and often higher acuity/third spacing. It may modestly predict later creatinine via congestion/illness severity, but is an indirect treatment proxy and likely redundant with overall fluid/vasopressor signals.

Lowest intraoperative glucose can reflect metabolic stress, insulin use, or hypoglycemia episodes. It is not a direct kidney injury marker and is unlikely to survive sparse selection compared with hemodynamic/renal-specific predictors.

Intraoperative vasodilator use indicates hypertension management or afterload reduction and can relate to hemodynamic lability. As a medication-choice proxy it is indirect and typically less informative than direct blood pressure/vasopressor requirement measures.

Intra-procedural EEG monitoring mainly reflects anesthesia/neurologic monitoring intensity and case type. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with minimal direct mechanistic link to AKI, so very low sparse-model priority.

Pre-incision TEE use signals anticipated complexity and need for detailed cardiac assessment. It is largely a practice/acuity proxy and is dominated by the actual cardiac function findings (e.g., EF/RV function) for prediction.

Pre-incision LVEF summarizes baseline LV systolic function and risk of low cardiac output, a key driver of renal hypoperfusion after cardiac surgery. It is a relatively direct physiologic risk factor and likely to add unique value among pre-op/intra-op features.

Baseline RV dysfunction predicts venous congestion and impaired forward flow, both strongly linked to postoperative AKI risk. This provides distinct information from LVEF and is a strong sparse-model candidate.

Mitral regurgitation severity reflects chronic volume overload and can correlate with heart failure physiology. It is a weaker, more indirect predictor than direct LV/RV function measures and may be redundant with them.

Mitral stenosis indicates elevated left atrial pressures/pulmonary hypertension risk, which can contribute to RV strain and congestion. However it is relatively uncommon and its predictive signal is typically captured better by RV function and hemodynamic variables.

Aortic regurgitation reflects chronic LV volume load and may relate to perioperative hemodynamic instability. It is indirect for AKI and often redundant with LVEF and overall cardiac function assessments.

Pre-incision aortic valve disease severity can correlate with baseline hemodynamics and operative complexity. It is an indirect, largely redundant proxy for downstream perfusion/vasopressor/AKI signals that will be better captured by post-op physiology features.

Indicator that aortic valve area was assessed (measurement/availability flag rather than physiology). Mostly a documentation/monitoring proxy with little unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Preoperative tricuspid regurgitation reflects right-sided dysfunction/venous congestion risk, which can contribute to AKI. Still indirect and likely dominated by post-op hemodynamics/fluid balance features in a sparse model.
Presence of a patent foramen ovale is generally unrelated to renal perfusion or intrinsic kidney injury. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine trajectory.
Flag that the ascending aorta was assessed (process/measurement indicator). Adds minimal physiologic information and is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

Ascending aortic diameter reflects aortopathy/surgical planning rather than renal risk directly. Any association with AKI is weak and mediated through procedure complexity, making it a low-priority sparse feature.

Aortic atherosclerosis burden can proxy embolic risk and overall vascular disease, which may correlate with postoperative organ dysfunction. However it is indirect for AKI and likely weaker than direct perfusion/CPB-related variables.

Mobile atheroma suggests higher embolic potential and complex aortic manipulation, which can relate to postoperative complications. For creatinine ratio it remains an indirect signal and typically secondary to CPB/hemodynamic/volume features.
Whether the aortic arch was visualized is primarily a procedural/assessment indicator. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with little direct renal predictive content.

Maximum arch atheroma severity reflects systemic atherosclerotic burden and potential embolic risk. Still an indirect predictor of AKI and likely redundant with stronger perioperative insult markers (e.g., CPB use, hypotension, lactate, urine output) not in this batch.
Mobile arch atheroma indicates higher embolic/complexity risk. For sparse prediction of creatinine ratio it is a weaker, indirect proxy compared with direct renal perfusion and exposure variables.

Use of cardiopulmonary bypass is a major mechanistic exposure for postoperative AKI via inflammation, hemolysis, and nonpulsatile flow. It is a high-yield, nonredundant perioperative predictor likely to survive sparse selection.

Autologous priming/retrograde autologous priming reflects perfusion strategy affecting hemodilution and transfusion needs. It may relate to AKI risk but is a technique proxy and typically weaker than CPB use and direct post-op physiologic signals.

Perfusion-team crystalloid volume captures intraoperative fluid/hemodilution exposure that can influence renal perfusion, congestion, and AKI risk. Within this batch it provides more direct, quantitative exposure information than most echo/atheroma descriptors.

Synthetic colloid exposure can affect renal function (agent-dependent) and reflects intravascular volume strategy. Useful but likely less common and potentially correlated with other perfusion fluid totals, reducing unique sparse-model value.

Albumin volume reflects intraoperative volume resuscitation strategy and oncotic support, which may correlate with hemodynamics and AKI risk. Moderately informative but partially redundant with overall fluid exposure and illness/complexity.

Use of hemofiltration/perfusion ultrafiltration reflects significant fluid overload, hemodynamic instability, or early renal support needs. It is a high-specificity, clinician-initiated marker tightly linked to evolving AKI risk and likely adds unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Need to wean inotropes off CPB is a proxy for myocardial dysfunction and low cardiac output physiology. It can predict renal hypoperfusion but is indirect and partly redundant with more direct perfusion markers (e.g., lactate, vasopressor dependence).

Difficulty weaning vasopressors off CPB indicates vasoplegia/hypotension risk and impaired renal perfusion. Compared with inotrope weaning, it more directly captures systemic perfusion pressure support and is a stronger sparse-model candidate.

Post-CPB/intra-op TEE presence is largely a monitoring/procedure indicator rather than a physiologic measurement. It may correlate with case complexity but is a weak, behavior-driven proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

SAM indicates dynamic LVOT obstruction that can cause episodic hypotension. It is relatively rare and its renal impact is mediated through hemodynamics already captured by broader shock/perfusion variables, so it is a low-priority sparse feature.

Return to CPB/echo-related event suggests intraoperative instability or technical issues. It is an indirect, low-frequency event marker and likely redundant with downstream perfusion markers (lactate, vasopressor needs, hemofiltration).

Baseline LV systolic function influences postoperative cardiac output and renal perfusion reserve. Useful context in older patients, but it is upstream/indirect and often dominated by immediate postoperative perfusion indicators.

Postoperative RV dysfunction can drive low forward flow, venous congestion, and renal dysfunction (cardiorenal physiology). It provides relatively unique information not fully captured by LV EF or generic severity markers, making it a strong sparse candidate.

Intraoperative cardiac arrhythmias can cause transient low output and hemodynamic instability. Predictive signal is plausible but heterogeneous and typically mediated through perfusion/vasopressor/lactate pathways, limiting unique value.

Core temperature reflects rewarming/hypothermia and overall postoperative physiology. It is a nonspecific severity marker with weak direct linkage to AKI compared with perfusion and renal-support variables.

INR reflects coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction and transfusion burden, which can correlate with complexity. It is indirect for kidney injury and likely redundant with stronger shock/perfusion markers in sparse selection.
WBC is a nonspecific inflammatory/stress marker in the immediate postoperative period. It is noisy and weakly specific for AKI risk at 60h, so low priority for a sparse linear model.
Platelet count can reflect CPB-related consumption, inflammation, and bleeding/transfusion exposure. It is indirect and often redundant with other complexity markers, so only a weak candidate.

Hematocrit reflects anemia/hemodilution and transfusion strategy, which can affect renal oxygen delivery. It can add some predictive value but is still indirect and confounded by fluid balance and transfusions.

Fibrinogen reflects inflammatory/consumptive coagulopathy and bleeding risk. It is a nonspecific perioperative severity marker and unlikely to add unique AKI prediction beyond perfusion and renal-support features.

Postoperative lactate is a strong marker of global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output and correlates closely with subsequent organ dysfunction including AKI. It is a high-yield physiologic signal likely to survive sparse screening as a top predictor.

Likely reflects postoperative propofol exposure/sedation intensity, which is more a care-pattern/illness-severity proxy than a renal mechanism. May correlate with instability/ventilation needs but is indirect and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics/renal markers.

Pain score on POD3 is temporally misaligned with the 36h feature window and is not a direct precursor of AKI. Mostly reflects analgesia/sedation practices and patient reporting, adding little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Current smoking captures baseline vascular/pulmonary risk that can modestly increase perioperative complications. However it is a distal, nonspecific risk factor and usually dominated by immediate postoperative physiology for predicting Hour-60 creatinine changes.

Former smoking is an even weaker baseline risk marker than current smoking and is highly nonspecific for acute postoperative AKI trajectory. Unlikely to add unique signal in a sparse model given stronger perioperative predictors.

Paroxysmal AF indicates baseline cardiac disease and risk for postoperative low cardiac output/embolism that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Moderately useful baseline context but indirect and often redundant with direct hemodynamic/vasopressor signals.

Persistent AF suggests more advanced atrial/cardiac disease and potentially worse perioperative hemodynamics affecting renal perfusion. Still an indirect baseline marker, so a moderate priority compared with direct post-op perfusion/renal measures.

Antegrade cardioplegia delivery is primarily a surgical technique variable with weak direct linkage to postoperative renal function. Any association with AKI is likely confounded by procedure type/complexity and not a high-value sparse feature.

Retrograde cardioplegia is another technique/complexity marker rather than a direct renal insult measure. Likely redundant with other operative complexity variables and low yield for sparse prediction of creatinine_ratio.
Blood cardioplegia type reflects operative preference and case characteristics more than kidney-specific physiology. Expected to add minimal unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Crystalloid cardioplegia is similarly a procedural choice and not a direct driver of postoperative AKI signal. Low priority for sparse selection versus physiologic/hemodynamic features.

History of acute heart failure indicates recent decompensation and higher risk of perioperative low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Strong baseline risk factor that can add unique signal even when early post-op noise is present.

Chronic heart failure reflects reduced renal perfusion reserve and susceptibility to congestion/low output after cardiac surgery. A strong, relatively nonredundant baseline predictor of AKI risk in older adults.

Systolic HF (reduced EF phenotype) is closely tied to low cardiac output risk, a key mechanism for postoperative AKI. High-value baseline feature likely to remain predictive even with limited feature budget.

Diastolic HF (HFpEF) relates to congestion and sensitivity to fluid shifts, which can contribute to renal dysfunction. Predictive but typically less directly tied to low-output AKI than systolic HF, so slightly lower sparse priority.

Mean SpO2 in the immediate 0–12h window is often influenced by ventilation/FiO2 and early postoperative noise. As an averaged measure it can miss hypoxemic extremes and is a weak, indirect proxy for renal risk.

Mean SpO2 at 13–24h is more temporally informative than 0–12h and may reflect ongoing respiratory failure affecting renal perfusion/inflammation. Still indirect and mean-aggregated (extremes would be better), so modest priority.

Average SpO2 in the 25–36h window reflects overall oxygenation but smooths over brief hypoxemic insults. Likely redundant with SpO2 minima/maxima and generally an indirect AKI signal, so low sparse-selection priority.

Peak SpO2 in the immediate 0–12h post-op period mostly reflects supplemental oxygen/ventilator settings rather than renal perfusion. Weak and non-specific for AKI risk and dominated by more informative extremes (especially minima) later in time.

Peak SpO2 at 13–24h is largely a care/FiO2 artifact and rarely captures kidney-relevant physiology. Adds little unique value versus SpO2 minima or hemodynamic variables, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Peak SpO2 closest to the target window still mainly reflects oxygen delivery settings and ceiling effects (often near 100%). Minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio compared with hypoxemia measures, so very low priority.

Lowest SpO2 in 0–12h can indicate early hypoxemia, which may contribute to organ injury, but this period is noisy post-bypass/anesthesia. Some potential signal, yet indirect and less reliable than later-window minima.
Lowest SpO2 in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful hypoxemic episodes after initial post-op noise settles. Still an indirect AKI predictor, but more likely to add value than SpO2 means/maxima.

Lowest SpO2 in 25–36h is the closest window and best captures late hypoxemic insults that can worsen renal oxygen delivery. Moderate priority as an indirect but plausible contributor; not as direct as renal/hemodynamic markers.
Count/frequency of SpO2 measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/ICU workflow proxy. High redundancy with routine continuous monitoring and weak physiologic specificity for AKI.

SpO2 measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects clinician attention and device availability more than oxygenation physiology. Generally a proxy feature with low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
SpO2 measurement frequency at 25–36h remains a monitoring/acuity proxy rather than a biomarker. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given weak mechanistic linkage and redundancy.

Mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) average in 0–12h reflects RV afterload/volume status but is early and confounded by immediate post-op physiology. Indirect for AKI and likely dominated by later-window values and more direct perfusion markers.

Average mPAP at 13–24h can indicate persistent pulmonary hypertension/RV dysfunction affecting cardiac output and renal perfusion. Moderately useful but still indirect and potentially redundant with other hemodynamic summaries.

Average mPAP at 25–36h is temporally closest and may capture sustained RV strain/venous congestion relevant to AKI risk. Moderate sparse-model candidate, though less informative than capturing extremes (max) within the same family.

Peak mPAP in 0–12h captures acute RV afterload spikes but this window is noisy and influenced by transient perioperative factors. Some signal, yet less reliable for Hour-60 creatinine than later peaks.

Peak mPAP at 13–24h better reflects clinically significant pulmonary hypertension/RV dysfunction episodes that can reduce renal perfusion. Useful but still indirect and may compete with other hemodynamic predictors.

Peak mPAP at 25–36h captures late, potentially pathologic RV afterload/venous congestion episodes closest to the prediction horizon. Within this batch it is the strongest PA-pressure aggregation (extreme + closest timing) and more likely to add unique predictive value.

Minimum mean PAP in the immediate post-op window reflects early RV/pulmonary vascular load and congestion. It is an indirect renal-perfusion signal and early (0–12h) values are noisy; likely dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Minimum mean PAP during 13–24h may reflect evolving RV failure/venous congestion that can contribute to AKI. Still indirect and typically redundant with CVP-based congestion measures, so low sparse-model priority.

Minimum mean PAP closest to the outcome window can capture persistent pulmonary hypertension/RV dysfunction contributing to renal venous congestion. More temporally relevant than earlier epochs, but remains an indirect proxy and overlaps with CVP signals.
Count/frequency of PAP measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/illness-complexity proxy. It provides little direct physiologic information and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of PAP in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior (PA catheter use/attention) more than renal pathophysiology. Generally weak and confounded, so minimal value for a sparse linear model.

PAP measurement frequency in 25–36h is a proxy for ongoing invasive monitoring and perceived instability. Not a direct renal signal and likely redundant with actual hemodynamic values, so very low priority.

Frequency of continuous cardiac index device readings in 0–12h reflects device presence/monitoring rather than cardiac output itself. As a clinician-behavior proxy and early window, it is unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency of continuous cardiac index in 13–24h is primarily a proxy for severity and monitoring strategy. It is not a physiologic exposure and is weak for sparse selection.

Continuous cardiac index measurement frequency in 25–36h indicates ongoing advanced monitoring rather than low output per se. Confounded and redundant with actual hemodynamic variables, so low utility.

Mean CVP in 0–12h reflects venous congestion/right-sided filling pressures that can impair renal perfusion. Early post-op CVP is heavily influenced by fluids/ventilation and is less reliable for Hour-60 AKI prediction than later/extreme CVP.

Mean CVP in 13–24h captures sustained venous congestion, a plausible driver of postoperative AKI. Moderately useful, but likely dominated by the corresponding max CVP (extremes) and by the later 25–36h window.

Mean CVP in 25–36h is temporally closest and reflects ongoing venous congestion, which is mechanistically linked to worsening creatinine. Strong sparse-model candidate among this batch, though still somewhat redundant with CVP max.
Peak CVP in 0–12h captures episodes of marked venous congestion that can precipitate renal dysfunction. Useful extreme aggregation, but early post-op transients reduce reliability versus later peaks.
Peak CVP in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful congestion beyond immediate bypass/anesthesia effects. As an extreme measure it often outperforms means and is a strong candidate within this set.

Peak CVP in 25–36h is the closest and most pathophysiologically informative congestion signal in this batch, strongly linked to renal venous hypertension and AKI trajectory. Likely to provide unique predictive value and survive sparse selection.

Minimum CVP in 0–12h may reflect hypovolemia/low preload but is highly confounded by ventilation, sedation, and measurement artifacts early post-op. It is less informative than CVP max/mean for renal congestion and is low priority.

Minimum CVP reflects lowest venous congestion/volume status in the 13–24h window, which can relate to renal perfusion and right-sided filling pressures. Moderately useful but indirect and likely weaker than later (25–36h) congestion signals for predicting Hour-60 creatinine.

Minimum CVP in the 25–36h window is a closer-to-target marker of hemodynamic state and venous congestion/underfilling that can influence AKI risk. Stronger sparse-model candidate than earlier CVP summaries due to temporal proximity and capturing an extreme.

Count/frequency of CVP measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy rather than a physiologic renal signal. Early post-op measurement frequency is especially noisy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
CVP measurement frequency reflects clinician behavior and illness complexity more than kidney pathophysiology. Adds little unique predictive value compared with actual CVP levels/extremes.

Later CVP measurement frequency may weakly proxy ongoing hemodynamic concern, which can correlate with evolving AKI risk. Still largely a behavior/monitoring feature and dominated by CVP value summaries.

Mean FiO2 in the first 12h is a nonspecific marker of respiratory support and immediate post-bypass physiology. It is indirect for renal outcomes and likely redundant with later oxygenation/support signals.

Mean FiO2 at 13–24h reflects ongoing respiratory support/illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it is indirect and typically weaker than extreme (max) requirements and later windows for sparse selection.

Mean FiO2 at 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and indicates sustained respiratory support needs. Still a general severity proxy and usually not a top sparse predictor of creatinine ratio compared with hemodynamic/renal-specific features.
Peak FiO2 in 0–12h captures early respiratory instability but is often driven by transient post-op factors. Some severity signal, but timing is early and indirect for kidney injury.
Peak FiO2 in 13–24h captures episodes of significant oxygenation/ventilatory support need, a stronger severity signal than mean/min. Moderately useful but still indirect for renal dysfunction.

Peak FiO2 in 25–36h is the most temporally proximate extreme respiratory-support marker in this batch and may track ongoing shock/inflammation affecting kidneys. Useful as a severity covariate, though not kidney-specific.
Minimum FiO2 early post-op is often near protocolized settings and provides little information about physiologic stress. Likely redundant and weak for predicting later creatinine changes.
Minimum FiO2 in 13–24h mainly reflects ability to wean but is less informative than peak requirements. Low unique value for sparse selection compared with FiO2_max in the same epoch.

Minimum FiO2 at 25–36h can indicate successful weaning vs persistent support, which may correlate with overall recovery trajectory. Still indirect and generally dominated by FiO2_max for capturing acute severity.
FiO2 measurement frequency is a documentation/monitoring proxy rather than a physiologic biomarker. Early post-op frequency is routine and unlikely to add predictive value.
FiO2 measurement frequency reflects care processes and ventilator charting patterns more than renal risk. Adds minimal unique information beyond FiO2 level summaries.

Count/frequency of FiO2 documentation in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilator management rather than renal physiology. Weak, proxy-heavy signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine ratio.

Average set ventilator respiratory rate in the immediate 0–12h post-op period, largely driven by sedation/vent strategy. Indirect and early/noisy for AKI prediction, and dominated by later-window or extreme physiology if present.

Average set respiratory rate at 13–24h, reflecting ongoing ventilatory support and illness severity. Still an indirect proxy for renal risk and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal markers, but later timing makes it slightly more informative than 0–12h.

Peak set respiratory rate in 0–12h may capture early respiratory distress/acidosis prompting higher minute ventilation. Extremes can matter, but this is early and largely clinician-driven, so it’s a weak-to-modest sparse-model candidate.

Peak set respiratory rate in 13–24h can reflect evolving metabolic acidosis/sepsis/respiratory failure, which correlates with AKI risk. Among RR summaries, the max is the most plausible to retain signal, though still indirect.

Minimum set respiratory rate in 0–12h mostly reflects sedation/paralysis and ventilator weaning choices. Low specificity for renal hypoperfusion/AKI and early post-op noise makes it a poor sparse-selection feature.

Minimum set respiratory rate in 13–24h is typically a weaning/sedation artifact rather than a pathophysiologic insult. Redundant and weakly linked to creatinine rise compared with max or other organ-failure markers.
Measurement frequency of set RR in 0–12h is a documentation/monitoring proxy. Adds little unique physiologic information and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model.
Measurement frequency of set RR in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than renal function. Proxy-heavy and dominated by actual RR values or more direct AKI predictors.

Measurement frequency of set RR in 25–36h is still primarily clinician behavior/monitoring intensity. Even with closer timing, it remains an indirect proxy with low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Average set tidal volume in 0–12h reflects ventilator strategy (often weight-based) and sedation rather than renal perfusion. Early and largely protocol-driven, so low priority for sparse selection.
Average set tidal volume in 13–24h remains mostly management-driven and relatively stable across patients. Weak, indirect association with AKI and likely redundant with other severity markers.

Average set tidal volume in 25–36h may reflect ongoing lung mechanics/ARDS management, which can correlate with multi-organ dysfunction. Still indirect and often protocolized, but closer timing gives modest potential value.

Maximum set tidal volume in 0–12h is typically determined by initial ventilator settings and body size rather than acute physiology. Early and clinician-driven, offering little unique signal for later creatinine rise.
Maximum set tidal volume in 13–24h is still largely a ventilator-management artifact with limited variability. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI beyond other covariates.

Maximum set tidal volume in 25–36h could indicate reduced lung-protective ventilation needs or clinician response to respiratory mechanics, loosely tracking severity. Indirect and redundant, but the later window makes it a modest candidate within this batch.

Minimum set tidal volume in the immediate post-op window mainly reflects ventilator strategy/body size rather than renal physiology. Weak, indirect signal for AKI and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers not in this batch.

Minimum set tidal volume from 13–24h is still primarily a ventilation management parameter with limited direct linkage to creatinine rise. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given indirectness and redundancy with overall illness severity.

Minimum set tidal volume at 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory failure/ARDS and associated multi-organ dysfunction closer to the outcome. Still indirect and clinician-driven, so low priority in a sparse model.

Count/frequency of tidal volume set measurements is a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than a physiologic renal signal. Early post-op documentation frequency is noisy and not a strong sparse-model candidate.

Measurement frequency of set tidal volume reflects charting/vent management intensity more than kidney risk. Adds little unique predictive value and is dominated by actual physiologic extremes if available.
Measurement frequency at 25–36h remains a clinician-behavior proxy (ventilator attention, instability) rather than a direct AKI precursor. Typically low-yield for sparse linear selection.

Mean PEEP in 0–12h reflects early oxygenation strategy and can affect venous return/renal perfusion, but this window is confounded by immediate post-bypass physiology. Mean is also less sensitive than max for capturing harmful exposure.

Mean PEEP at 13–24h may indicate sustained respiratory failure and higher intrathoracic pressures that can reduce renal perfusion. Still indirect and likely redundant with PEEP max as a stronger insult marker.

Mean PEEP at 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may capture sustained high intrathoracic pressure/ongoing lung injury associated with AKI risk. Moderately useful, but still partly redundant with PEEP max for sparse selection.
Peak PEEP in 0–12h can reflect acute severity and potential hemodynamic compromise, but early post-op settings are often transient and protocol-driven. Some signal, but not a top sparse feature.

Peak PEEP at 13–24h better captures episodes of high intrathoracic pressure and severe respiratory failure that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. More informative than mean/min, though still an indirect AKI predictor.

Peak PEEP at 25–36h is the strongest version here for capturing severe ongoing respiratory failure and potential hemodynamic/venous congestion effects near the prediction horizon. Likely to add the most unique predictive value among ventilator features in this batch.
Minimum PEEP in 0–12h mostly reflects baseline ventilator settings and weaning rather than renal insult. Highly redundant and unlikely to predict creatinine ratio meaningfully.
Minimum PEEP at 13–24h is more about ventilator management/weaning than kidney risk. Provides little unique information compared with PEEP max/mean.
Minimum PEEP at 25–36h may weakly indicate readiness to wean vs persistent respiratory failure, indirectly relating to overall recovery. Still a weak and redundant signal relative to PEEP max/mean.
PEEP measurement frequency is primarily a documentation/monitoring intensity proxy in the immediate post-op period. Not a direct physiologic marker and low priority for sparse modeling.

Count of PEEP recordings in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ventilation-management proxy. It is indirect for AKI risk and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus physiologic/hemodynamic or renal markers.

Frequency of PEEP documentation in 25–36h reflects clinician behavior and respiratory complexity more than renal physiology. Despite being closer to Hour 60, it adds little unique signal for creatinine_ratio.

Average plateau pressure early post-op reflects lung mechanics/ventilator strategy and can correlate with severity and venous congestion. However it is indirect for kidney injury and early (0–12h) values are noisy and often transient.

Mean plateau pressure in 13–24h captures sustained ventilatory load but is still a nonspecific severity marker for AKI. Likely redundant with more informative extremes (max) and with other illness-severity variables outside this batch.

Peak plateau pressure in 0–12h may capture acute high intrathoracic pressure episodes that can reduce renal perfusion/raise venous pressure. As an extreme it is more informative than the mean, but early timing limits reliability for Hour 60 creatinine.

Maximum plateau pressure in 13–24h better reflects clinically meaningful respiratory severity and potential hemodynamic compromise than mean/min. Still indirect for AKI and may be redundant with other severity/hemodynamic features, so moderate priority.

Minimum plateau pressure mostly reflects periods of low support or measurement conditions rather than injurious exposure. It is dominated by max/mean for capturing clinically relevant extremes and is early/noisy.
Lowest plateau pressure in 13–24h is not a plausible driver of renal injury and is largely uninformative compared with peak pressures. Low unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Number of plateau pressure measurements in 0–12h is a documentation/monitoring proxy (e.g., ventilator adjustments, clinician concern). It is not a direct physiologic renal signal and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Measurement frequency of plateau pressure in 13–24h reflects care intensity and respiratory management rather than kidney pathophysiology. Adds little unique information beyond actual pressure values.
Plateau pressure measurement count in 25–36h is still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy despite temporal proximity. Weak and indirect for predicting Hour 60 creatinine_ratio.

Mean minute ventilation in 0–12h reflects ventilatory demand and acid-base management, which can correlate with overall illness severity. Early post-op values are heavily influenced by anesthesia/vent settings and are indirect for AKI.
Average minute ventilation in 13–24h may reflect sustained metabolic/respiratory stress but is nonspecific for renal injury. Likely redundant with max minute ventilation and other severity markers.

Peak minute ventilation early may indicate episodes of acidosis, agitation, or high CO2 production, serving as a general severity proxy. As an extreme it is preferable to the mean, but early timing and indirectness limit sparse-model priority.

Maximum minute ventilation in 13–24h captures more clinically meaningful sustained stress/acid-base burden than early values and may track evolving shock/sepsis physiology. Still indirect for AKI, but among minute-ventilation features it is the most selection-worthy.

Minimum minute ventilation is often driven by sedation, ventilator mode changes, or brief measurement artifacts rather than pathologic exposure. It is dominated by max/mean for severity signal and is weak for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Minimum minute ventilation in 13–24h reflects ventilatory support/respiratory drive and overall illness severity. It is an indirect proxy for AKI risk and likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/renal features, so low sparse-selection priority.

Count/frequency of minute ventilation documentation in 0–12h is mainly monitoring intensity and workflow. This is a clinician-behavior proxy with high noise early post-op and little unique renal signal.

Measurement frequency of minute ventilation in 13–24h primarily reflects monitoring intensity/ventilator management rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Measurement frequency of minute ventilation in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing mechanical ventilation/ICU complexity closer to the outcome window. Still largely clinician-behavior and dominated by actual physiologic variables, so only modest value.

Average oxygen device level in 0–12h reflects early respiratory support needs after surgery. It is an indirect severity marker and early post-op practices vary, limiting unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine.

Mean oxygen device level in 13–24h captures sustained respiratory support requirement. Still an indirect illness-severity proxy and generally less informative than extremes or closer-window measures for sparse selection.
