Mean oxygen device level in 25–36h reflects ongoing respiratory failure/need for support closer to Hour 60. As a nearer-term severity marker it may add some predictive value, but remains indirect for renal dysfunction.

Maximum oxygen device level in 0–12h captures the highest early respiratory support requirement. Extremes are preferable to means, but this very early window is noisy post-bypass and only indirectly related to AKI.

Maximum oxygen device level in 13–24h captures episodes of higher respiratory support need, a marker of cardiopulmonary instability. This extreme may carry more signal than mean/min, but remains a non-specific proxy for renal injury.

Maximum oxygen device level in 25–36h reflects the worst respiratory support requirement in the closest available window. It can proxy ongoing shock/low cardiac output states that predispose to AKI, but is still indirect and potentially redundant with hemodynamics.

Minimum oxygen device level in 0–12h largely reflects best-case support (often room air after extubation) and is less informative than maxima for acute insults. Early post-op variability makes it a weak sparse-model candidate.

Minimum oxygen device level in 13–24h reflects periods of low support and is generally less predictive than maxima for instability. Likely redundant with mean/max and provides little unique renal-risk information.

Minimum oxygen device level in 25–36h may indicate recovery/extubation status closer to the outcome window. It can weakly stratify overall trajectory but is dominated by max/mean and remains indirect for AKI.

Frequency of oxygen device documentation in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/workflow proxy. Early post-op charting intensity is variable and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of oxygen device in 13–24h reflects clinician attention and respiratory therapy workflow more than physiology. Low priority for sparse selection given indirectness and redundancy.

Measurement frequency of oxygen device in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing respiratory support needs and ICU complexity near the prediction horizon. Still largely a behavior proxy and inferior to actual support level (max/mean).

Mean right chest tube output in the immediate post-op window reflects bleeding/effusions and fluid shifts. It is an indirect, early signal for AKI risk and is likely dominated by later windows and/or extreme outputs for sparse selection.

Average chest tube drainage from 13–24h may reflect ongoing bleeding/inflammation affecting hemodynamics. Still indirect for renal injury and largely redundant with max output and the closer 25–36h window.

Average chest tube output at 25–36h captures ongoing losses closer to the creatinine outcome and may relate to hypoperfusion/transfusion needs. Among means, this is the most temporally relevant, but remains an indirect proxy compared with direct renal/hemodynamic markers.

Peak early chest tube output captures acute bleeding that can drive hypotension/transfusion and renal hypoperfusion. Max is more informative than mean/min, but the 0–12h timing is noisy and less predictive than later peaks.

Peak chest tube output in 13–24h reflects clinically significant ongoing bleeding after initial stabilization. Useful as an extreme-value insult marker, but still indirect and somewhat redundant with the closer 25–36h peak.

Peak chest tube output at 25–36h is a near-term marker of ongoing bleeding/instability that can precipitate renal hypoperfusion and AKI. This extreme and closest epoch is the strongest chest-tube-derived candidate for a sparse model.

Minimum chest tube output in 0–12h is usually near zero and reflects drain patency/measurement artifacts more than physiology. It adds little unique predictive value and is dominated by max/mean outputs.

Minimum chest tube output from 13–24h is typically uninformative (often zero) and can reflect intermittent recording rather than clinical state. Low priority and redundant with more meaningful extremes (max) and averages.
Minimum chest tube output at 25–36h is not a reliable marker of bleeding burden or hemodynamic stress. It is unlikely to survive sparse selection given stronger summaries like max output.

Count/frequency of chest tube output measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/ICU workflow proxy. It is not a direct physiologic renal signal and is unlikely to add unique value beyond actual output amounts.
Measurement frequency of chest tube output in 13–24h reflects clinician attention and documentation patterns. As a behavior proxy, it is weak and redundant with the recorded output values themselves.

Chest tube output measurement frequency at 25–36h is primarily a proxy for monitoring intensity rather than bleeding severity. Low sparse-model priority compared with max/mean output in the same epoch.

Mean systolic pulmonary artery pressure (PAP) in 0–12h reflects RV afterload/volume status and can relate to low cardiac output states affecting renal perfusion. Early post-op values are noisy and mean is less sensitive than extremes, so it is a weaker sparse candidate.

Mean systolic PAP in 13–24h may capture sustained pulmonary hypertension/RV strain that can impair forward flow and renal perfusion. Moderately useful as a hemodynamic context variable, but indirect and potentially redundant with more direct perfusion markers.

Mean systolic PAP at 25–36h is the closest sustained RV afterload signal to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. It can add some unique hemodynamic information, though it remains indirect and may be outperformed by extreme PAP or systemic perfusion variables.

Maximum systolic PAP in 0–12h captures acute RV afterload spikes that may contribute to low output and renal hypoperfusion. Max is preferable to mean for insult detection, but early timing limits reliability for Hour-60 prediction.

Peak systolic pulmonary artery pressure in 13–24h reflects RV afterload/volume status and cardiopulmonary stress. Indirect for AKI and largely redundant with later (25–36h) extremes, so low sparse-selection priority.

Peak systolic PAP in 25–36h may capture ongoing RV failure/pulmonary hypertension contributing to low forward flow and renal hypoperfusion. Still an indirect renal signal, but the closest window and an extreme makes it the best candidate among systolic PAP summaries.

Minimum systolic PAP in the immediate 0–12h post-op period is heavily influenced by anesthesia, ventilation, and transient hemodynamic swings. Weak and noisy for Hour-60 creatinine trajectory and dominated by later windows.

Low systolic PAP in 13–24h can reflect low pulmonary flow/low cardiac output states that may relate to renal hypoperfusion. Indirect and likely redundant with other hemodynamic markers and the later 25–36h window.

Low systolic PAP in 25–36h may indicate persistently low flow/RV dysfunction, which can precede worsening renal function. Indirect but temporally closest and an extreme, making it a moderate sparse-model candidate within this batch.
Count/frequency of systolic PAP measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/line-presence proxy. Not a physiologic renal signal and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Measurement frequency of systolic PAP in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and device availability more than kidney physiology. Typically redundant with severity-of-illness proxies and low value for sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of systolic PAP in 25–36h is a proxy for ongoing invasive monitoring/concern. Not a direct renal precursor and generally not worth a sparse-model slot.
Mean diastolic PAP in 0–12h reflects average pulmonary vascular/volume status but is smoothed and early post-op. Likely noisy and dominated by later extremes, so minimal sparse-model utility.
Average diastolic PAP in 13–24h may correlate with RV filling pressures and congestion that can contribute to AKI. Mean aggregation blunts insults and is indirect, so only weak-to-moderate priority.

Average diastolic PAP in 25–36h captures sustained pulmonary pressure/volume status closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and likely less informative than max/min extremes, so not a top sparse feature.

Peak diastolic PAP in 0–12h can reflect acute pulmonary hypertension/volume overload but is confounded by immediate post-bypass physiology. Extreme helps, yet early timing limits reliability for Hour-60 creatinine.

Peak diastolic PAP in 13–24h may indicate evolving RV strain/congestion and elevated venous pressures that can worsen renal function. More informative than means and earlier mins, but still indirect and somewhat redundant with 25–36h peaks.

Peak diastolic PAP in 25–36h is the closest-window extreme and can proxy high right-sided pressures/congestion (cardiorenal physiology) preceding AKI. Among this batch it offers the strongest, least-smoothed signal and is most likely to survive sparse selection.

Minimum diastolic PAP in 0–12h is susceptible to transient post-op factors (ventilation, sedation, vasodilation) and may not reflect sustained hemodynamics. Weak, noisy, and dominated by later windows.

Low diastolic PAP in 13–24h can reflect low pulmonary flow/low output states relevant to renal perfusion. Indirect and likely redundant with other hemodynamic indicators, so limited sparse-model priority.

Minimum diastolic pulmonary artery pressure in the 25–36h window reflects RV/pulmonary vascular loading and venous congestion that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion/congestive AKI. Moderately useful but indirect and likely dominated by more direct renal perfusion/volume markers not shown here.

Count/frequency of diastolic PAP measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/line-presence proxy rather than physiology. Early window and measurement-frequency features are unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Frequency of diastolic PAP measurements in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and device availability more than renal pathophysiology. Adds little unique predictive value versus actual PAP values.

Measurement frequency of diastolic PAP at 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing hemodynamic instability/need for PA catheter monitoring closer to the outcome. Still largely a behavior proxy and inferior to true hemodynamic extremes.

Average heart rate in the immediate 0–12h post-op period is a nonspecific illness/severity signal and often noisy from anesthesia, pacing, and catecholamines. Likely redundant with later HR summaries and not kidney-specific.

Mean heart rate in 13–24h reflects sustained sympathetic tone/low output states that can relate to renal risk. However it is indirect and typically dominated by more proximal (25–36h) or extreme HR features.

Mean heart rate in 25–36h is closer to Hour-60 and can capture sustained tachycardia from low output, sepsis, or ongoing vasopressor support affecting renal perfusion. Moderate sparse-model candidate but still nonspecific.

Peak heart rate in 0–12h may capture acute stress/arrhythmia burden but is often transient and treatment-driven early after surgery. Some signal, but timing noise and indirectness limit sparse priority.

Maximum heart rate in 13–24h can reflect clinically meaningful tachyarrhythmias or persistent stress that may precede AKI via low output/hypoperfusion. More informative than mean, but still a general severity proxy.

Maximum heart rate in 25–36h is a near-term extreme that can capture ongoing instability (e.g., AF with RVR, shock physiology) relevant to renal hypoperfusion before Hour-60 creatinine. Among pulse features, this extreme and closest window is most likely to survive sparse selection.
Minimum heart rate in 0–12h is commonly influenced by pacing, sedation, hypothermia, or transient conduction issues immediately post-op. Weak and noisy for predicting later creatinine ratio.

Minimum heart rate in 13–24h may reflect bradyarrhythmia or heavy beta-blockade/pacing that could correlate with low output. Still indirect and usually less informative than tachycardic extremes for AKI risk.

Minimum heart rate in 25–36h could indicate bradycardia/over-sedation/pacing dependence with potential low cardiac output implications. However it is less consistently linked to renal injury than tachycardia extremes and is somewhat redundant with other hemodynamic markers.
Frequency of pulse measurements in 0–12h is largely routine monitoring intensity and does not represent a physiologic renal insult. Very low unique value for sparse modeling.
Pulse measurement frequency in 13–24h is a care-process proxy (nursing/telemetry intensity) rather than a biomarker. Unlikely to add predictive value beyond actual HR values.
Pulse measurement frequency in 25–36h remains primarily a monitoring proxy and is typically near-universal in ICU patients. Adds minimal unique information for predicting creatinine ratio.

Mean inspired O2 as an anesthetic/respiratory gas exposure in the immediate post-op window. Largely reflects ventilator/OR practice and is not a direct renal signal; likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/oxygenation variables not in this batch.
Peak inspired O2 early after surgery. High FiO2 is common and mainly a severity/management proxy; weak and indirect for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Lowest inspired O2 early after surgery. More reflective of weaning/ventilator strategy than kidney perfusion or injury, and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.
Count/frequency of O2 agent measurements in 0–12h. Primarily a monitoring-intensity/EMR artifact proxy rather than physiology, so low priority for sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of O2 agent in 13–24h. Still mainly clinician behavior/monitoring intensity and not a renal biomarker; unlikely to survive sparse screening.

Measurement frequency of O2 agent in 25–36h, closer to the outcome window. Could weakly proxy ongoing ventilatory support/illness complexity, but remains indirect and low-uniqueness for AKI prediction.
Mean nitrous oxide exposure in the immediate post-op period. N2O use is largely practice-pattern driven and not mechanistically tied to AKI risk, so minimal sparse-model value.
Peak nitrous oxide exposure early after surgery. Captures anesthetic technique rather than renal hypoperfusion/injury and is unlikely to add predictive signal for creatinine rise.
Lowest nitrous oxide exposure early after surgery. Essentially indicates whether/when N2O was used and offers little unique information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Frequency of N2O measurements in 0–12h. Mostly an EMR documentation/monitoring proxy with negligible direct relevance to renal dysfunction.
Frequency of N2O measurements in 13–24h. N2O is typically not a key ICU titration variable; measurement count is unlikely to contribute unique AKI prediction.
Frequency of N2O measurements in 25–36h. Still a weak clinician-behavior proxy and generally irrelevant to kidney injury trajectory, so very low priority.
Mean medical air as a carrier gas early post-op. Reflects ventilator gas blending rather than renal physiology and is dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers.
Peak air fraction early post-op. Essentially the complement of FiO2 management and not a direct AKI precursor; low unique predictive value.
Lowest air fraction early post-op (often when FiO2 is high). This is a ventilator-setting artifact and unlikely to help a sparse model predict creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of air agent in 0–12h. Primarily documentation/monitoring intensity with little physiologic specificity for renal outcomes.
Count/frequency of recorded “air” as an anesthetic agent is essentially a documentation/monitoring artifact. It has no direct renal physiology signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of “air” as an anesthetic agent is a clinician/documentation proxy rather than a biomarker. Even though closer to Hour 60, it adds negligible unique value for AKI prediction.

Average isoflurane exposure in the immediate post-op window mainly reflects anesthesia practice and early OR/ICU transition. It is indirect for renal injury and likely dominated by hemodynamic/renal-perfusion variables not in this family.
Peak isoflurane dose early post-op is more about anesthetic management than kidney insult. Any association with creatinine rise is likely confounded and not a priority in a sparse model.

Minimum isoflurane dose is not a meaningful physiologic extreme for renal risk and is largely noise/administration pattern. Redundant with other isoflurane summaries and low value for sparse selection.
Frequency of isoflurane documentation is a monitoring/clinical workflow proxy rather than a renal signal. Early timing further reduces relevance to Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of isoflurane in 13–24h reflects ongoing sedation/anesthesia documentation intensity. It is an indirect clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Isoflurane measurement frequency closest to the target window still primarily captures care patterns (e.g., continued sedation) rather than renal physiology. Low priority versus direct perfusion/renal markers.

Mean mixed/central venous O2 saturation reflects global oxygen delivery/consumption balance and can relate to renal hypoperfusion. However, early post-op values are noisy and the mean can mask brief low-flow episodes.

Mean venous O2 saturation in 13–24h captures sustained adequacy of cardiac output/oxygen delivery, which is relevant to evolving AKI risk. Still somewhat redundant with more informative extremes (especially minima).

Maximum venous O2 saturation is rarely the pathologic signal for renal injury and can be elevated with low extraction or high FiO2. Early timing and weak mechanistic link make it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Peak venous O2 saturation is not a strong indicator of renal hypoperfusion and is often clinically ambiguous. It is dominated by minima/trajectory measures for AKI prediction.

Minimum venous O2 saturation can capture low cardiac output/oxygen delivery episodes that predispose to renal ischemia. Despite early post-op noise, the extreme is more informative than the mean/max and may add some unique signal.

Minimum venous O2 saturation in 13–24h is a strong marker of low-flow physiology and inadequate oxygen delivery, a plausible precursor to AKI and creatinine rise. As an extreme in a more stable window, it is a high-priority sparse feature within this batch.

Measurement frequency of venous O2 saturation mainly reflects monitoring intensity (e.g., presence of PA catheter/acuity) rather than physiology. It is a proxy feature and low priority for sparse selection.

Frequency of venous O2 saturation measurements may weakly proxy illness severity or invasive monitoring, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still largely clinician-behavior driven and dominated by the actual SvO2 values (especially minima).

Indicates whether mixed/central venous O2 saturation was checked in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/clinical concern. As a clinician-behavior proxy (not the SvO2 value), it is unlikely to add unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Average maintenance IV fluid volume in the immediate 0–12h post-op window, reflecting early resuscitation/maintenance strategy. Early fluids are noisy and this mean is likely redundant with later (closer) epochs and with max values that better capture fluid loading.

Average maintenance IV fluid volume during 13–24h, reflecting ongoing fluid administration. Some relevance to renal perfusion/congestion, but the mean is a smoothed exposure and is typically dominated by the closer 25–36h window and/or extremes.

Average maintenance IV fluid volume during 25–36h, the closest window to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Could capture sustained fluid administration (illness severity/renal perfusion strategy), but remains indirect and partly redundant with max volume in the same epoch.

Peak maintenance IV fluid volume in 0–12h, capturing early high-rate fluid administration. Extremes are preferable to means, but this very early post-op period is confounded by bypass/anesthesia effects and may not uniquely predict Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peak maintenance IV fluid volume in 13–24h, reflecting episodes of higher fluid delivery beyond the immediate post-op noise. More informative than the mean and earlier window, but still an indirect proxy and somewhat redundant with the closer 25–36h max.

Peak maintenance IV fluid volume in 25–36h, closest to the outcome and best captures acute high fluid exposure that may reflect hemodynamic instability or evolving AKI management. Among the maintenance-IV summaries, this extreme in the latest epoch is the strongest sparse-model candidate.

Minimum maintenance IV fluid volume in 0–12h, often reflecting brief pauses/line changes rather than a renal-relevant physiologic insult. Highly noisy early post-op and dominated by max/mean measures for capturing clinically meaningful exposure.

Minimum maintenance IV fluid volume in 13–24h, which is usually not a meaningful renal signal compared with peaks or cumulative exposure. Likely reflects workflow/temporary holds and adds little unique predictive value.
Minimum maintenance IV fluid volume in 25–36h, again more indicative of interruptions than kidney-relevant physiology. Redundant and weak compared with the corresponding max/mean in the same epoch.

Count/frequency of recording maintenance IV volume in 0–12h, primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy. Measurement intensity is not a direct biomarker and is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus physiologic values.
Frequency of maintenance IV volume measurements in 13–24h, reflecting charting intensity and care complexity. As a proxy variable, it is low-yield and redundant with actual administered volumes.

Frequency of maintenance IV volume measurements in 25–36h, mainly indicating monitoring/documentation patterns. Even though temporally closer, it remains a clinician-behavior proxy and is dominated by the volume magnitude features.

Whether systolic pulmonary artery pressure was measured in 0–12h, indicating presence/use of a PA catheter and monitoring intensity. This is not the PAP value and is an indirect proxy unlikely to add unique predictive power for creatinine ratio.
Whether systolic PAP was measured in 13–24h, again reflecting invasive monitoring and clinician concern. As a binary measurement indicator, it is a weak, proxy-heavy feature for sparse modeling.

Whether systolic PAP was measured in 25–36h, which may weakly mark ongoing hemodynamic instability/complexity closer to the outcome. Still a measurement proxy (not physiology) and generally dominated by actual hemodynamic/renal variables, so only modest priority.

Indicates whether pulmonary artery diastolic pressure was measured in the first 12h (monitoring intensity/PA catheter presence). This is a clinician-behavior proxy and early post-op; low unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Measurement flag for PAP diastolic in 13–24h, reflecting invasive monitoring rather than renal physiology. Likely redundant with other severity/monitoring proxies and weak for sparse selection.

Measurement flag for PAP diastolic in 25–36h, a proxy for ongoing hemodynamic concern/complexity closer to the outcome. Still indirect and dominated by actual hemodynamic values if available, so only modest priority.
Whether CVP was measured in 0–12h, mainly indicating line presence/monitoring intensity. Early post-op and not a direct renal perfusion/AKI marker, so low sparse-model value.

CVP measurement indicator in 13–24h, reflecting clinician monitoring rather than congestion/hypoperfusion itself. Likely redundant with other care-intensity signals and not worth a sparse feature slot.
CVP measurement indicator in 25–36h may capture persistent instability/need for invasive monitoring closer to Hour 60. Still an indirect proxy and inferior to actual CVP values, so limited priority.

Mean end-tidal CO2 in 0–12h reflects ventilation/perfusion and cardiac output indirectly. Early window and mean smooths transient low-flow episodes; weak and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/acid-base markers.

Mean EtCO2 in 13–24h can reflect sustained low cardiac output or ventilation changes that may relate to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and averaged, so unlikely to survive sparse selection unless few perfusion variables exist.

Mean EtCO2 in 25–36h is closer to the 60h creatinine and may capture ongoing low-flow physiology affecting AKI risk. Indirect and potentially confounded by ventilator management, but could add some unique signal in a sparse model.
Maximum EtCO2 in 0–12h is usually driven by hypoventilation/ventilator settings rather than renal-relevant hypoperfusion. Early and not a clear AKI precursor, so low priority.

Peak EtCO2 in 13–24h more often reflects ventilation strategy or CO2 retention than kidney injury mechanisms. Provides little unique predictive value for creatinine ratio compared with low EtCO2 extremes or direct perfusion markers.

Peak EtCO2 in 25–36h is still primarily a ventilation/CO2 clearance signal and not a specific renal hypoperfusion marker. Likely noisy and dominated by more informative extremes like EtCO2 minima or hemodynamics.

Minimum EtCO2 in 0–12h can indicate episodes of low pulmonary blood flow/low cardiac output or severe hyperventilation, which may precede renal hypoperfusion. However, early post-op instability is common, so predictive specificity for Hour 60 is moderate.

Minimum EtCO2 in 13–24h captures acute low-flow episodes more directly than the mean and is less dominated by immediate post-bypass noise. This extreme is a stronger sparse-model candidate as a proxy for low cardiac output impacting AKI risk.

Minimum EtCO2 in 25–36h is the closest-window extreme and can reflect ongoing low cardiac output/shock physiology strongly linked to subsequent creatinine rise. Among EtCO2 summaries, this is the most pathophysiologically informative and least redundant for sparse selection.

Frequency/indicator of EtCO2 measurement in 0–12h reflects monitoring/ventilation status rather than a physiologic renal insult. As a clinician-behavior proxy in an early window, it has minimal unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Count of ETCO2 recordings in 13–24h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/ventilation management rather than renal physiology. Low unique value for AKI prediction and dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal markers not in this batch.

Count of ETCO2 recordings in 25–36h, a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than a kidney insult marker. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus true physiologic extremes.

Average FiO2 in the immediate post-op window reflects respiratory support/oxygenation needs and overall severity. Mean in 0–12h is noisy and indirect for renal injury, so low priority in a sparse model.
Peak FiO2 in 0–12h captures transient hypoxemia/instability requiring high oxygen. It can proxy severity but is early and indirect for AKI, so only a weak candidate.

Minimum FiO2 in 0–12h mostly reflects how quickly oxygen can be weaned and is heavily practice-dependent. Weak and indirect signal for future creatinine rise, and less informative than extremes of perfusion/renal markers.
Number of FiO2 measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/ventilator-management proxy. Adds little unique physiologic information for predicting Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Number of FiO2 measurements in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than oxygenation burden itself. Low sparse-model value and likely redundant with actual FiO2 levels (not measurement counts).
Number of FiO2 measurements in 25–36h is a late monitoring proxy but still not a direct renal-risk signal. Unlikely to be selected when feature budget is tight.

Mean tidal volume in 0–12h reflects ventilator strategy and patient size/effort; renal relevance is indirect (severity/ventilator-induced hemodynamic effects). Early window and mean aggregation reduce usefulness for sparse AKI prediction.

Mean tidal volume in 13–24h may reflect ongoing ventilation needs and sedation but remains an indirect severity marker for AKI. Mean is likely redundant with more informative extremes and later physiology.

Mean tidal volume in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may proxy persistent mechanical ventilation and cardiopulmonary instability that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect, but the later timing makes it a moderate sparse-model candidate within this batch.
Maximum tidal volume in 0–12h can capture brief high-pressure/high-volume ventilation episodes, but these are often transient and practice-driven post-op. Indirect for AKI and early, so low priority.

Maximum tidal volume in 13–24h may indicate intermittent ventilatory stress or patient-ventilator dyssynchrony, but renal linkage is weak and confounded by clinician settings. Likely not retained in a sparse model.

Maximum tidal volume in 25–36h may reflect ongoing ventilatory difficulty and higher intrathoracic pressures that can reduce venous return/cardiac output, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. Later timing and extreme aggregation make it one of the stronger signals in this ventilator-only batch.

Minimum tidal volume in 0–12h is usually driven by ventilator mode changes, spontaneous breathing, or measurement artifacts. Weak, non-specific, and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Minimum tidal volume in 13–24h is similarly mode/practice dependent and may reflect brief low-support periods rather than pathology. Low uniqueness and weak mechanistic link to AKI, so poor sparse-selection candidate.

Minimum tidal volume in the 25–36h window reflects ventilator strategy/respiratory mechanics rather than renal physiology. At best it is an indirect severity/ventilation proxy and is unlikely to add unique value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.

Whether tidal volume was recorded in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/ventilation-status proxy (often routine while intubated). Measurement frequency is weakly informative and early post-op timing is noisy for Hour-60 renal prediction.

Tidal volume measurement in 13–24h mostly indicates ongoing mechanical ventilation/monitoring intensity. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and typically dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features.

Tidal volume measurement in 25–36h suggests continued intubation/ICU complexity closer to the outcome window. Still largely a monitoring proxy and not a direct precursor of AKI, so low sparse-selection priority.

ABP pulse measurement in 0–12h reflects presence of an arterial line/monitoring rather than perfusion adequacy. Early post-op monitoring is near-universal and provides little unique signal for later creatinine rise.

ABP pulse measurement in 13–24h can indicate ongoing invasive monitoring and higher acuity. It remains an indirect clinician-behavior proxy and is redundant with actual BP/vasopressor features (not present here).

ABP pulse measurement in 25–36h suggests persistent need for invasive hemodynamic monitoring closer to Hour 60. Some weak association with illness severity/renal hypoperfusion risk, but still indirect and not a strong sparse-model keeper.
Heart rate measurement in 0–12h is routine ICU monitoring and mainly reflects data availability rather than physiology. As a measurement indicator it is a weak, noisy proxy for later AKI.

Heart rate measurement in 13–24h is typically ubiquitous and functions as a monitoring-intensity flag. It adds little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio compared with true hemodynamic extremes or renal labs.

Heart rate measurement in 25–36h remains a near-universal monitoring indicator rather than a physiologic summary. As a _measured feature it is unlikely to survive sparse selection for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Mean arterial pressure measurement in 0–12h indicates arterial-line monitoring and documentation, not hypotension burden. Early post-op measurement presence is common and not uniquely predictive of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

MAP measurement in 13–24h may weakly mark ongoing hemodynamic instability/monitoring needs. However it is still a measurement proxy and inferior to actual MAP minima/vasopressor dose features for sparse modeling.

MAP measurement in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect continued hemodynamic concern. Still indirect (presence of measurement rather than hypotension severity) and likely redundant with stronger perfusion variables.

Respirations measured/documented by anesthesia in 0–12h is largely a workflow/monitoring artifact around the OR-to-ICU transition. It is not a direct renal signal and early timing is particularly noisy.

Anesthesia respirations measurement in 13–24h is an atypical documentation/monitoring indicator rather than a physiologic exposure. It offers minimal unique information for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Anesthesia respirations measurement in 25–36h is primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy and not a renal-relevant biomarker. Even with closer timing, it is unlikely to contribute meaningfully in a sparse linear model.
Indicates whether pulse oximetry was recorded in the immediate post-op window. This is mainly a monitoring-intensity proxy with little direct renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement flag for pulse oximetry in 13–24h. Still a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy rather than physiology, offering minimal unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.
Measurement flag for pulse oximetry in the closest window (25–36h). Despite timing, it remains a weak proxy for acuity/monitoring and not a direct predictor of AKI trajectory.

Amount/recording of tube (enteral) intake early post-op, reflecting feeding tolerance and overall stability. It is indirect and confounded by practice patterns; early timing makes it a weak sparse-model candidate.
Tube intake in 13–24h may reflect recovery vs ongoing critical illness. Still an indirect severity/nutrition proxy with limited unique renal information compared with hemodynamics/renal labs.

Tube intake in 25–36h is a later marker of clinical trajectory (ability to feed) and may correlate with lower illness severity and less AKI risk. It’s still indirect but has more predictive value due to proximity to Hour 60.

Average maintenance/bolus IV volume in 0–12h reflects early resuscitation and fluid exposure. Mean smooths peaks and early post-bypass fluid shifts are noisy, so it’s likely redundant with more informative extremes/late-window fluid signals.

Peak IV bolus/maintenance volume in 0–12h can capture aggressive resuscitation episodes that may relate to shock/renal hypoperfusion. Extremes are more informative than means, but early timing and confounding by clinician behavior limit uniqueness.

Minimum IV bolus/maintenance volume in 0–12h is not a meaningful physiologic insult marker and is hard to interpret clinically. It is dominated by max/total exposure measures and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Count/frequency of IV bolus volume documentation in 0–12h. This is primarily a care-intensity proxy and not a direct renal-risk signal, so it should be deprioritized in a sparse model.
Measurement frequency of IV bolus volume in 13–24h. Still mainly reflects documentation/clinical attention rather than physiology and is redundant with actual volume features.

Measurement frequency of IV bolus volume in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability requiring titration/resuscitation. It remains a proxy variable, but later timing gives slightly more potential signal than earlier windows.

Average pacemaker use/setting early post-op, reflecting conduction issues and possibly low cardiac output states. It is an indirect hemodynamic proxy and early post-op pacing is common, reducing sparse-model priority.

Mean pacemaker use/setting in 13–24h may better reflect persistent need for pacing and ongoing cardiac dysfunction affecting renal perfusion. Moderately useful but still indirect and potentially redundant with direct hemodynamic/vasopressor variables (if present elsewhere).

Mean pacemaker use/setting in 25–36h captures persistent pacing dependence closest to the outcome window, a plausible marker of ongoing low-output physiology and renal hypoperfusion risk. Likely the strongest pacing-related feature in this batch for sparse selection.

Any/maximum pacemaker use in 0–12h captures whether pacing was needed at all immediately post-op. This is less informative than sustained later pacing (25–36h mean) and early transient pacing is common, so it’s lower priority.

Indicates presence/use of temporary pacing in the 13–24h window, a marker of conduction issues and overall post-op complexity. It is an indirect severity/procedure proxy with weak specific linkage to AKI and likely redundant with the later (25–36h) pacing indicator.

Presence/use of temporary pacing in the 25–36h window may reflect ongoing hemodynamic instability or low cardiac output closer to the creatinine outcome. Still indirect for renal injury, but the later timing makes it the best candidate among the pacemaker flags.
Early (0–12h) pacing use is common immediately post-cardiac surgery and often transient. As an indirect proxy and early/noisy window, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Temporary pacing in 13–24h suggests persistent conduction/hemodynamic issues beyond the immediate post-op period. Predictive value for creatinine ratio is still indirect and largely redundant with 25–36h pacing status.

Temporary pacing in 25–36h captures ongoing need for pacing closer to Hour 60 and may correlate with low output states that predispose to AKI. It remains a non-specific proxy but is the most temporally relevant pacemaker feature.

Measurement frequency/documentation of pacemaker parameters in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine post-op care. It is a clinician-behavior proxy and early/noisy, so low priority for a sparse model.

How often pacemaker data are recorded in 13–24h is primarily a proxy for attention/complexity rather than renal physiology. Likely redundant with simple pacing presence indicators and not a strong sparse-model keeper.

More frequent pacemaker documentation in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability and higher acuity closer to the outcome. Still largely a monitoring-intensity proxy, so only modest value beyond pacing presence.

Recording SLED/CRRT blood pump rate implies renal replacement therapy context, but the '_measured' field is mostly documentation/monitoring rather than the therapy itself. Early timing and proxy nature limit sparse-model priority despite potential AKI signal.

Measurement of CRRT/SLED pump rate in 13–24h suggests ongoing RRT management, but as a measurement-frequency variable it is an indirect proxy. It may add some AKI-related information yet is dominated by more direct RRT-on/off or creatinine/urine output features (if available).

Measurement of CRRT/SLED pump rate in 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and can indicate persistent severe AKI requiring RRT. Still a proxy variable, but among these it carries the strongest near-term renal dysfunction signal.

Sevoflurane measurement in 0–12h reflects anesthetic exposure/documentation around surgery. It has minimal unique predictive value for later creatinine ratio and is largely unrelated to evolving ICU AKI physiology.
Sevoflurane measurement in 13–24h is uncommon and mainly reflects documentation/continued sedation practices rather than renal injury. Low specificity and likely noise for sparse selection.

Sevoflurane measurement in 25–36h is a clinician-practice/documentation signal with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI compared with hemodynamics, nephrotoxins, or RRT indicators. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse linear model.

Frequency of IV intake measurement in 0–12h is mostly a monitoring/documentation proxy and early post-op care is routine. It may weakly correlate with acuity/fluid management but is not a direct renal signal.

IV intake measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects attention to fluid balance and illness complexity rather than actual fluid burden. Some indirect relevance to AKI risk, but as '_measured' it is low-yield for sparse selection.

Count/frequency of IV intake documentation in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and workflow. It is a weak, indirect proxy for illness/complexity and adds little unique renal signal for a sparse model.

Measurement frequency of right-sided drain output in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Mostly a care-process proxy and early post-op noise; unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of right drain output during 13–24h. Still primarily clinician behavior/monitoring intensity rather than physiology, and not a direct renal perfusion or function marker.

Measurement frequency of right drain output in 25–36h, closer to the outcome window and may weakly reflect ongoing bleeding/instability. However it remains a proxy variable and is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct hemodynamics/renal markers.
A binary indicator that a right-sided arrival-time-related field was recorded in 0–12h. This is essentially documentation/metadata with minimal physiologic content and low sparse-model priority.
Binary documentation indicator for the 13–24h window. It is not a renal biomarker and is dominated by true physiologic variables; keep out in a sparse model.
Binary documentation indicator in 25–36h. Despite temporal proximity, it remains a workflow proxy with negligible unique information about evolving AKI risk.

Proportion of time in normal sinus rhythm averaged over 0–12h, reflecting early rhythm stability. Rhythm can relate to cardiac output and renal perfusion, but this early mean is indirect and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/vasopressor features.

Average normal sinus rhythm presence over 13–24h, a marker of rhythm stability and possibly better cardiac output. Still an indirect severity signal and likely replaceable by direct BP/CO/vasopressor measures in sparse selection.

Average normal sinus rhythm presence over 25–36h, closer to Hour 60 and may capture sustained arrhythmia burden affecting renal perfusion. Among the rhythm summaries, this mean is the most temporally relevant and could add modest unique value if hemodynamic variables are limited.

Whether normal sinus rhythm occurred at any point in 0–12h (a very permissive indicator). This max is uninformative because most patients will have at least some NSR, making it redundant and low-yield for sparse modeling.
Any occurrence of NSR in 13–24h. The max aggregation is too non-specific and dominated by more discriminative rhythm burden measures (e.g., mean/min) and direct hemodynamics.
Any occurrence of NSR in 25–36h. Still too insensitive to capture clinically meaningful arrhythmia burden, so it is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

Whether NSR was continuously present throughout 0–12h (min), capturing any early arrhythmia. It is more informative than max, but early post-op rhythm disturbances are common/transient and only indirectly linked to AKI.

Whether NSR was maintained throughout 13–24h, reflecting sustained rhythm stability vs persistent arrhythmia. This can proxy for stable cardiac output and perfusion and is more discriminative than max/mean, though still indirect.

Whether NSR was maintained throughout 25–36h, capturing persistent arrhythmia burden closest to the prediction horizon. This is the strongest rhythm-derived feature here (extreme/min + best timing) and may add unique predictive value for renal hypoperfusion risk in a sparse model.

Count/frequency of documentation of normal sinus rhythm in the first 12h. This is mainly a monitoring/documentation proxy and early post-op rhythm documentation is noisy and not a direct renal signal.

Frequency of normal sinus rhythm documentation in hours 13–24. Still largely clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity and offers little unique value for AKI prediction versus hemodynamics/renal markers.

Frequency of normal sinus rhythm documentation in hours 25–36. Slightly more informative due to closer timing (may reflect stability/less arrhythmia burden), but remains an indirect proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Average burden of sinus rhythm variants (e.g., sinus brady/tachy/arrhythmia variants) in the first 12h. Early post-op rhythm variability is common and the mean smooths extremes, making it weak and redundant.

Mean burden of sinus rhythm variants in hours 13–24. Could weakly reflect ongoing instability affecting renal perfusion, but mean aggregation is diluted and likely dominated by a max/any-occurrence indicator.

Mean burden of sinus rhythm variants in hours 25–36. Closer to the outcome window but still an indirect severity marker and less informative than an extreme/any-occurrence feature for sparse modeling.

Indicator that any sinus rhythm variant occurred in the first 12h (max over window). Captures an extreme/occurrence better than the mean, but early timing and indirect linkage to AKI limit sparse-model priority.

Indicator of any sinus rhythm variant in hours 13–24. Occurrence of rhythm instability may correlate with hemodynamic stress and renal hypoperfusion, and this max is the best aggregation within this family for signal retention.
Indicator of any sinus rhythm variant in hours 25–36. Best-timed and best-aggregated (max/any) version here, but still a nonspecific illness/hemodynamic proxy rather than a direct renal predictor.
Minimum of a binary sinus-variant indicator over 0–12h (effectively whether it was absent at any time). This is poorly informative/redundant with max and adds little unique predictive value.
Minimum of the binary sinus-variant indicator over 13–24h. Min on a binary variable is generally unhelpful and dominated by the corresponding max/any-occurrence feature.
Minimum of the binary sinus-variant indicator over 25–36h. Redundant and low-signal compared with max/any-occurrence; unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Frequency of measurement/documentation of sinus rhythm variants in the first 12h. Primarily reflects monitoring intensity and documentation practices rather than physiology relevant to creatinine rise.
Frequency of measurement/documentation of sinus rhythm variants in hours 13–24. A clinician-behavior proxy with minimal unique renal predictive content.

Frequency of measurement/documentation of sinus rhythm variants in hours 25–36. Slightly more informative as a proxy for ongoing instability/attention, but still indirect and typically not worth a sparse feature slot.

Average burden of atrial arrhythmia (e.g., AF/flutter) in the first 12h. Atrial arrhythmias can impair cardiac output and renal perfusion, but early timing and mean aggregation reduce specificity; still potentially useful if arrhythmia features are otherwise absent.

Represents proportion of time in atrial arrhythmia during 13–24h, a marker of hemodynamic instability/illness severity. Likely redundant with a simpler presence/absence indicator and indirect for AKI risk, so low sparse-selection priority.

Proportion of time in atrial arrhythmia during 25–36h, closer to the creatinine outcome window and may reflect sustained low-output physiology. Still indirect and often captured by the binary max in the same epoch, but timing makes it a moderate candidate.
Indicates any atrial arrhythmia occurred in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-op arrhythmias are common/transient and are an indirect AKI signal, so it is a weak keeper.

Binary indicator of atrial arrhythmia presence in 13–24h, which can correlate with hemodynamic compromise and renal hypoperfusion risk. More useful than 0–12h and less redundant than mean/min, making it a moderate sparse-model feature.

Binary indicator of atrial arrhythmia presence in 25–36h, the closest epoch and a plausible marker of ongoing instability affecting renal perfusion. Among this batch it is the strongest atrial-arrhythmia summary and likely to dominate redundant aggregations.
Binary minimum over 0–12h effectively reflects whether arrhythmia was continuously present, which is rarely the key renal signal. Highly redundant with max and poorly timed, so very low priority.
Binary minimum over 13–24h captures continuous arrhythmia rather than occurrence, adding little beyond the max/mean features. Redundant and indirect for AKI, so lowest-tier.

Binary minimum over 25–36h suggests persistent arrhythmia throughout the window, which could imply sustained hemodynamic stress. Still largely redundant with the 25–36h max/mean and not a direct renal marker, so only weak-to-moderate.
Measurement frequency for atrial arrhythmia in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/EMR artifact rather than physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy and early window, it is not a sparse-model keeper.

Measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects monitoring intensity or documentation patterns more than renal pathophysiology. Low unique predictive value relative to actual arrhythmia presence, so score is minimal.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring. Still primarily clinician-behavior and dominated by actual rhythm indicators, so low priority.
Proportion of time paced in 0–12h, reflecting conduction issues and post-op support needs. Indirect for AKI and early/transient, so weak sparse-selection value.

Proportion of time paced in 13–24h, a marker of cardiac dysfunction/support but not a direct renal signal. Likely redundant with a binary paced indicator and other hemodynamic variables, so low priority.

Proportion of time paced in 25–36h may indicate persistent low-output/conduction problems closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and potentially redundant with paced max, but timing makes it a moderate candidate.
Binary indicator of any pacing in 0–12h, often routine immediately post-cardiac surgery. Early pacing is common and not specific for renal injury, so weak inclusion priority.

Binary indicator of any pacing in 13–24h, suggesting ongoing conduction/low-output issues that can relate to renal hypoperfusion. More informative than 0–12h and a cleaner sparse feature than the mean, so moderate priority.

Indicates any paced rhythm occurrence in the 25–36h window, reflecting conduction disease/need for pacing. Weak, indirect link to AKI (via hemodynamic instability) and largely a care/arrhythmia proxy rather than a renal signal, so low sparse-model priority.

Whether paced rhythm was present throughout (minimum) in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-op pacing is common/transient and not a direct renal marker; dominated by later-window rhythm/hemodynamic features.

Captures sustained paced rhythm during 13–24h, suggesting persistent conduction issues. Still an indirect severity/hemodynamics proxy and likely redundant with heart block indicators and later (25–36h) status.

Sustained paced rhythm in the closest available window (25–36h), which may reflect ongoing low-output/conduction problems affecting renal perfusion. Among paced-rhythm variants, this timing and ‘min’ (persistence) makes it the most informative, though still indirect.

Frequency/availability of paced rhythm documentation in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/EMR sampling. This is a clinician-behavior proxy with minimal unique physiologic information for creatinine at 60h.
Measurement count for paced rhythm in 13–24h, primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus actual rhythm presence/persistence variables.
Measurement count for paced rhythm in 25–36h, reflecting attention/monitoring rather than renal physiology. Low value and likely noisy compared with binary rhythm indicators.

Average heart block status in 0–12h, which can be influenced by transient post-bypass conduction changes. Mean aggregation blunts episodic instability and early timing is noisy, so low sparse-model utility.
Average heart block burden in 13–24h, reflecting ongoing conduction disturbance. Still a smoothed measure and likely redundant with max/min heart block indicators and paced rhythm persistence.

Average heart block burden in 25–36h, closest to the prediction horizon and may correlate with sustained hemodynamic compromise affecting renal perfusion. Mean is less sharp than extremes but could add some unique trajectory information in this batch.
Any heart block episode in 0–12h, capturing an acute conduction insult. Extremes are preferable to means, but early post-op episodes are often transient and less predictive for 60h creatinine.

Any heart block episode in 13–24h, suggesting clinically meaningful conduction instability beyond immediate post-op noise. More informative than mean and earlier min/max, but still indirect and partly overlaps with pacing variables.

Any heart block episode in 25–36h, the closest window and an extreme indicator of ongoing conduction/hemodynamic risk that can impair renal perfusion. Among heart-block features, this has the best timing and preserves the acute-insult signal, making it a strong sparse-model candidate.

Minimum heart block status in 0–12h (i.e., whether heart block was continuously present). Continuous heart block this early is uncommon and the window is noisy; likely redundant with later persistence measures.

Continuous heart block during 13–24h, indicating persistent conduction disease. Potentially relevant via sustained low-output risk, but overlaps with paced rhythm persistence and later (25–36h) indicators.

Continuous heart block in 25–36h, reflecting persistent conduction failure close to the outcome window and plausibly sustained renal hypoperfusion risk. High priority within this batch because persistence plus late timing is less redundant and more prognostic than early/mean versions.
Count/frequency of heart block documentation/monitoring in the first 12h. This is largely a monitoring-intensity proxy and early post-op conduction issues are common and indirect for AKI risk.
Frequency of recorded heart block in hours 13–24. Still mainly reflects surveillance/EMR recording rather than a direct renal perfusion or injury signal, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of recorded heart block in hours 25–36. Later timing is closer to the creatinine outcome and persistent conduction problems may correlate with low output, but it remains an indirect, behavior-influenced proxy.

Average presence of ischemic ST changes in the first 12h. Myocardial ischemia can contribute to low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion, but early post-op ECG noise and indirectness limit sparse-model value.

Average presence of ischemic ST changes in hours 13–24. Some signal for ongoing ischemia/low output, but the mean is less informative than an extreme/any-occurrence indicator and is redundant with max.

Average presence of ischemic ST changes in hours 25–36. Closest window to Hour 60 and may reflect sustained ischemia affecting renal perfusion, but still indirect and likely dominated by the max version.

Whether any ischemic ST change occurred (max) in the first 12h. Captures an acute ischemic insult better than the mean, but early post-op artifacts/transient changes reduce reliability for Hour 60 creatinine.
Whether any ischemic ST change occurred (max) in hours 13–24. A clearer marker of clinically meaningful ischemia than the mean and may add unique low-output risk information relevant to AKI.

Whether any ischemic ST change occurred (max) in hours 25–36. This is the strongest in this family due to temporal proximity and capturing acute ischemia that can drive renal hypoperfusion, making it a plausible sparse-model keeper.
Minimum value of a binary ischemic ST-change indicator in the first 12h (typically uninformative if any zeros exist). This aggregation is not clinically meaningful and is dominated by max/mean.
Minimum value of a binary ischemic ST-change indicator in hours 13–24. Provides little information beyond 'not always present' and is redundant with max/mean.
Minimum value of a binary ischemic ST-change indicator in hours 25–36. Not a useful summary for ischemia burden and adds negligible unique predictive value versus max.

Number of times ischemic ST change was assessed/recorded in the first 12h. Primarily a monitoring/concern proxy rather than physiology, and early post-op measurement intensity is highly protocol-driven.
Number of times ischemic ST change was assessed/recorded in hours 13–24. Still mainly reflects clinician behavior and telemetry documentation rather than a direct renal-risk signal.

Number of times ischemic ST change was assessed/recorded in hours 25–36. Later increased monitoring may indicate instability, but it remains an indirect proxy and is weaker than the actual ST-change max indicator.

Mean burden of 'other' cardiac rhythm category in the first 12h. This is heterogeneous and hard to interpret mechanistically, making it noisy and unlikely to add stable, unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Represents proportion of time in a non-standard/other rhythm during 13–24h. Rhythm category is an indirect severity/instability proxy for AKI risk and the mean is likely redundant with a simpler any-occurrence indicator, so low sparse-model priority.

Represents proportion of time in a non-standard/other rhythm during 25–36h (closest window). Still an indirect proxy and likely dominated by the binary any-occurrence feature in the same epoch, so only weak incremental value.

Indicates whether an 'other' rhythm occurred at any point in 0–12h. Early post-op rhythm abnormalities are common/transient and are indirect for renal hypoperfusion, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Indicates any occurrence of an 'other' rhythm in 13–24h, which may reflect hemodynamic instability affecting renal perfusion. More informative than 0–12h and more parsimonious than the mean, but still a nonspecific proxy.

Indicates any occurrence of an 'other' rhythm in 25–36h (most temporally proximate). Could capture ongoing instability relevant to evolving AKI, but remains indirect and likely secondary to direct hemodynamic/renal markers not in this batch.
Binary 'min' for a categorical rhythm flag is not clinically meaningful beyond presence/absence and is typically redundant with max. In the noisy 0–12h window it adds little unique predictive value.
Binary 'min' for rhythm category is largely uninformative/redundant (often reflects whether it was always present). Dominated by the corresponding max/mean summaries for sparse selection.
Binary 'min' for rhythm category is a poor aggregation and mostly redundant with max/mean. Even in the closest window it is unlikely to add unique signal for creatinine ratio.
Counts/reflects how often 'other rhythm' was recorded in 0–12h, mainly a monitoring/documentation intensity proxy. Weak and noisy early post-op, so very low sparse-model priority.

Measurement frequency for 'other rhythm' in 13–24h is primarily clinician behavior/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Unlikely to add stable, unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.
Measurement frequency for 'other rhythm' in 25–36h is a proxy for attention/complexity rather than a direct renal risk marker. Typically dominated by actual rhythm occurrence indicators.

Represents proportion of time on ASV ventilator mode in 0–12h, reflecting respiratory support strategy. This is an indirect care-pattern/severity proxy and early post-op practice-driven, so limited unique value for AKI prediction.

Represents proportion of time on ASV in 13–24h, which may correlate with ongoing respiratory failure and illness severity. Still largely clinician-choice driven and nonspecific for renal dysfunction, so low priority.
Indicates any ASV use in 0–12h. Binary any-use is more parsimonious than mean but remains a practice-pattern proxy in a noisy immediate post-op window, so weak sparse-model candidate.

Indicates any ASV use in 13–24h, potentially marking prolonged ventilatory support needs. Still indirect and likely redundant with stronger respiratory/hemodynamic variables (not in this batch), so modest importance.

Binary 'min' for ventilator mode is generally not informative (often reflects whether ASV was used continuously) and is redundant with max/mean. Early timing further reduces its utility for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Whether ASV was used at any point in 13–24h is mainly a ventilation-strategy marker. It is an indirect severity/management proxy with little unique renal signal and likely redundant with other ventilation-mode indicators.

Count/frequency of ASV documentation in 0–12h reflects monitoring/charting intensity more than physiology. Early post-op timing plus strong clinician-behavior proxy makes it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Measurement frequency of ASV in 13–24h is largely documentation/management intensity rather than a direct kidney-risk biomarker. Unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond general illness severity.

ASV measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory support needs closer to the outcome window. Still primarily clinician-behavior/vent-management and not a direct renal perfusion or injury signal.

Average time in controlled ventilation in 0–12h reflects immediate post-op ventilatory dependence. It is an indirect severity proxy and early-window noise limits its unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Sustained controlled ventilation in 13–24h suggests ongoing critical illness/sedation and potentially worse hemodynamics. Moderately useful as a severity trajectory marker, but still indirect for AKI and likely redundant with other support variables.

Any use of controlled ventilation in 0–12h is common after cardiac surgery and often routine. As a binary extreme it’s less redundant than mean, but early timing and low specificity for renal injury keep priority low.

Any controlled ventilation in 13–24h indicates failure to wean and ongoing physiologic stress, which can correlate with AKI risk. Useful but still a nonspecific severity proxy and not a direct renal signal.

Minimum controlled-vent indicator in 0–12h is essentially redundant with other controlled-mode summaries and offers little interpretable incremental information. Early post-op period further reduces its sparse-selection value.

Minimum controlled-vent indicator in 13–24h is a weak/awkward aggregation and largely redundant with max/mean indicators. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus clearer summaries of ventilatory dependence.

Frequency of controlled-mode documentation in 0–12h is mainly charting intensity and routine post-op management. It is not a physiologic renal-risk marker and is dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features (not in this batch).
Controlled-mode measurement frequency in 13–24h is primarily a monitoring/management proxy. Adds little unique information beyond whether controlled ventilation occurred and overall severity.

In 25–36h, frequent controlled-mode documentation may indicate persistent ventilatory dependence closer to the outcome window. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and weaker than a simple presence/absence indicator for sparse modeling.
Average SIMV use in 0–12h reflects early ventilator strategy and routine post-op transitions. It is indirect for kidney injury and likely redundant with other ventilation-mode variables.
SIMV mean in 13–24h may reflect partial weaning status and overall recovery trajectory. Mildly informative as a severity/weaning proxy but not a direct renal hypoperfusion/injury signal.
Any SIMV use in 0–12h is common and mainly indicates ventilator management rather than renal risk. Early timing and nonspecificity make it low priority for a sparse model.

SIMV use in 13–24h reflects ongoing ventilatory support/sedation and overall illness severity. It is an indirect proxy for AKI risk and likely redundant with other ventilation-mode indicators, so low sparse-model priority.

Any SIMV exposure in the immediate 0–12h post-op window is common and heavily influenced by routine post-bypass management. Early mode choice is noisy and indirect for renal outcomes, making it a weak sparse feature.

Presence of SIMV during 13–24h suggests delayed liberation from the ventilator and higher acuity. Still a clinician-behavior/illness-severity proxy and largely redundant with other vent-mode summaries.
Count/frequency of SIMV documentation in 0–12h mainly reflects charting/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. As a measurement-frequency proxy it is unlikely to add unique predictive value.
SIMV measurement frequency in 13–24h is primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy. It is not a direct renal signal and is dominated by more physiologic predictors in sparse selection.

Later (25–36h) SIMV measurement frequency may weakly capture prolonged mechanical ventilation/complexity closer to the outcome horizon. Still mostly a monitoring proxy and not a direct kidney-injury precursor.
Spontaneous mode in 0–12h reflects early weaning/readiness but is strongly driven by routine post-op practice. It is indirect for AKI and likely noisy in this immediate window.
Spontaneous mode during 13–24h suggests improved respiratory status and lower overall acuity. This is an indirect severity marker and redundant with SIMV indicators, so modest priority.
Any spontaneous mode exposure in 0–12h is common and influenced by sedation strategy rather than renal perfusion. As an early, indirect proxy it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Any spontaneous mode exposure in 13–24h may indicate earlier weaning and lower severity, which correlates with lower AKI risk. Still indirect and overlapping with other vent-mode features.
Absence/presence of spontaneous mode in 0–12h is largely determined by immediate post-op protocols and transient instability. It provides little unique information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Whether spontaneous mode occurred in 13–24h captures delayed vs earlier weaning, a general acuity signal. It is not kidney-specific and is redundant with other ventilation-mode summaries.
Measurement frequency of spontaneous mode in 0–12h is a charting/monitoring proxy rather than a physiologic exposure. Low likelihood of adding unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Spontaneous-mode measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly reflects documentation intensity and care complexity. It is an indirect proxy and typically dominated by physiologic variables.
Later measurement frequency (25–36h) may weakly encode ongoing ventilatory management and trajectory closer to Hour 60. Still primarily a monitoring/behavior proxy, so only modest priority.

Mean arterial pressure in 0–12h reflects systemic perfusion pressure, a key driver of renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk after cardiac surgery. Although a mean can miss brief hypotensive insults and the window is early, MAP is a relatively direct mechanistic predictor and likely to be retained if few hemodynamic features are available.
Average MAP over 13–24h reflects overall perfusion but smooths over short hypotensive insults most relevant to AKI. Likely redundant with MAP minima (and later-window MAP) for sparse selection.

Average MAP over 25–36h captures sustained perfusion closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Still partially redundant with MAP_min, but may add signal if prolonged low-normal MAP drives renal hypoperfusion.

Peak MAP in the immediate 0–12h post-op period is rarely a direct driver of AKI and is often influenced by transient vasoactive management. Dominated by hypotension-focused features (MAP_min) and later epochs.
Peak MAP over 13–24h mainly reflects treatment intensity rather than renal hypoperfusion risk. Adds little unique value compared with MAP_min/mean in later windows.
Peak MAP over 25–36h is more a clinician/vasopressor effect marker than a kidney-injury precursor. In a sparse model it is dominated by low-MAP extremes and does not directly encode hypoperfusion.

Minimum MAP in 0–12h captures early hypotensive episodes that can contribute to renal ischemia. However, early post-bypass instability is noisy and less predictive than later hypotension for Hour-60 creatinine.

Minimum MAP in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful hypotension after initial post-op noise has settled, a strong precursor of AKI. Likely to survive sparse selection as an extreme-value perfusion insult marker.

Minimum MAP in 25–36h is the closest hypotension-insult signal to the 60h creatinine and directly reflects renal hypoperfusion risk. Among MAP summaries, this extreme in the latest window is the highest-yield sparse predictor.

Count/frequency of MAP measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Highly confounded by routine ICU practice and redundant with having an arterial line.
Measurement frequency over 13–24h reflects clinician attention and workflow more than renal risk. Unlikely to add stable, unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.
Measurement frequency over 25–36h remains a proxy for monitoring intensity and care complexity. Typically dominated by the actual MAP values (especially MAP_min) for predicting AKI trajectory.
Proportion of time NPO in 0–12h is largely routine post-cardiac surgery practice and weakly related to kidney injury. Mostly a care-process proxy with little unique signal for creatinine rise.
NPO status in 13–24h may reflect delayed recovery but is nonspecific and confounded by extubation/aspiration risk decisions. Low priority versus direct perfusion/renal markers.

Persistently NPO by 25–36h can indicate ongoing critical illness or inability to extubate, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still a nonspecific clinician-behavior/trajectory proxy, so only modest sparse-model value.
Any NPO in 0–12h is nearly universal after surgery and provides minimal discrimination. Redundant with NPO_mean and largely non-informative for renal prediction.
Any NPO in 13–24h remains common and does not quantify severity or duration. Adds little beyond NPO_mean and is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

Whether the patient was ever NPO during 25–36h post-op, reflecting feeding status/clinical stability. It is a weak, indirect proxy for illness severity and care trajectory and is unlikely to add unique renal predictive value in a sparse model.
Whether the patient was continuously NPO in the immediate 0–12h post-op window. Early post-op NPO is routine and noisy, offering minimal unique information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Whether the patient remained NPO throughout 13–24h, which can reflect delayed recovery or ongoing instability. Still largely a clinician-behavior/trajectory proxy and redundant with later feeding status.

Whether the patient was persistently NPO across 25–36h, closer to the outcome window and may indicate ongoing critical illness. However it remains indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal labs if available.
Count/frequency of NPO diet-type documentation in 0–12h, mainly reflecting charting intensity. This is a monitoring/EMR artifact rather than a physiologic renal signal.
Frequency of NPO documentation in 13–24h, a proxy for care complexity and documentation patterns. Low likelihood of surviving sparse selection versus direct renal perfusion or kidney markers.
Frequency of NPO documentation in 25–36h, reflecting charting/monitoring rather than physiology. Even though temporally closer, it is still a weak proxy with little unique renal information.
Average diet status coding in 0–12h, essentially summarizing early feeding orders. Early feeding/NPO decisions are routine post-cardiac surgery and not a direct predictor of AKI trajectory.

Average diet status coding in 13–24h, reflecting progression toward enteral intake. It may weakly track recovery but is indirect and redundant with simpler binary indicators (diet vs NPO) in later windows.

Average diet status coding in 25–36h, capturing whether the patient is generally tolerating diet closer to the outcome. Still a nonspecific recovery proxy and unlikely to add unique value beyond stronger physiologic predictors.
Whether the patient ever had a diet order (not NPO) in 0–12h. This is uncommon/variable and heavily influenced by routine post-op protocols, offering little renal-specific signal.
Whether the patient ever had a diet order in 13–24h, indicating earlier feeding advancement. It is a weak proxy for stability and likely redundant with 25–36h feeding status.

Whether the patient ever had a diet order in 25–36h, suggesting clinical stability and recovery. Closer timing helps, but it remains indirect and not kidney-specific, so only low priority for sparse selection.
Whether the patient was continuously on a diet (never NPO) during 0–12h. This is largely protocol-driven and rare, and early window noise makes it a poor predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Whether the patient stayed on a diet throughout 13–24h, reflecting sustained feeding tolerance. It may correlate with lower severity but is indirect and replaceable by more proximal physiologic variables.

Whether the patient was continuously on a diet during 25–36h, a closer marker of recovery trajectory. Still a nonspecific proxy and unlikely to contribute unique predictive value for AKI compared with direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Count/frequency of diet-type documentation/orders in the first 12h post-op. Primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy with little direct renal signal and early timing noise, so low sparse-model priority.
Frequency of diet-type documentation/orders during 13–24h. Still a workflow proxy rather than physiology and unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI beyond hemodynamics/fluids/labs.

Frequency of diet-type documentation/orders during 25–36h. Slightly closer to the outcome and may weakly reflect recovery/ability to feed, but remains indirect and dominated by more direct renal/illness markers.

Average value of an 'other' diet category indicator in 0–12h (effectively proportion of time/orders labeled 'other'). Diet category is not a direct kidney injury marker and early post-op diet status is highly confounded, so minimal sparse value.
Mean presence/proportion of 'other' diet category in 13–24h. Largely administrative/ordering variation and unlikely to contribute unique signal for creatinine trajectory.

Mean presence/proportion of 'other' diet category in 25–36h. Later timing could weakly proxy ongoing critical illness or inability to advance diet, but it is nonspecific and redundant with stronger severity features.
Whether 'other' diet category occurred at any point in 0–12h. A binary administrative flag with high noise early after surgery and little mechanistic link to AKI.
Whether 'other' diet category occurred at any point in 13–24h. Still a nonspecific ordering artifact and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Whether 'other' diet category occurred at any point in 25–36h. Later occurrence may weakly reflect delayed recovery/continued ICU-level care, but remains an indirect proxy and low-yield compared with physiologic extremes.
Minimum of the 'other' diet indicator in 0–12h (typically uninformative for binary/indicator variables). Redundant with max/mean and provides no meaningful renal signal.
Minimum of the 'other' diet indicator in 13–24h. For an indicator-like variable, min is generally non-informative and redundant, so very low priority.
Minimum of the 'other' diet indicator in 25–36h. Min aggregation is not clinically interpretable here and adds no unique predictive value.
Frequency of recording the 'other' diet category in 0–12h. Measurement frequency is a documentation proxy and early post-op period is especially noisy, so not a sparse-model keeper.
Frequency of recording the 'other' diet category in 13–24h. Still primarily reflects ordering/documentation practices rather than kidney physiology, so low value.

Frequency of recording the 'other' diet category in 25–36h. Slightly more informative as a proxy for ongoing complexity, but remains indirect and likely dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic variables.

Mean proportion of time with regular cardiac rhythm in the first 12h post-op (proxy for arrhythmia burden). Arrhythmias can affect renal perfusion, but this is early and indirect; more direct hemodynamic/vasopressor features would dominate in a sparse model.

Proportion of time in a regular rhythm during 13–24h (inverse of arrhythmia burden). Indirect renal relevance and largely redundant with irregular-rhythm indicators; at best a weak hemodynamic-stability proxy.

Proportion of time in a regular rhythm during 25–36h. Closer to the outcome window but still an indirect proxy and mostly redundant with irregular rhythm features; unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Whether the patient ever had a regular rhythm in 0–12h. Near-universal/low-information and early post-op noise; dominated by more informative instability measures.
Whether the patient ever had a regular rhythm in 13–24h. Binary “ever regular” is typically non-discriminative and redundant with irregular rhythm presence.
Whether the patient ever had a regular rhythm in 25–36h. Still a coarse binary that adds little unique signal for AKI risk compared with arrhythmia burden or hemodynamic variables.
Whether regular rhythm was absent at any point in 0–12h (i.e., any irregularity). Captures early arrhythmia occurrence but timing is noisy and it is a crude proxy for perfusion risk.

Whether regular rhythm was absent at any point in 13–24h (any irregularity). A reasonable marker of arrhythmia burden/hemodynamic instability with moderate predictive value, but still indirect for renal injury.

Whether regular rhythm was absent at any point in 25–36h (any irregularity). Best-timed of the regular_min features and may reflect ongoing instability affecting renal perfusion, but remains nonspecific.
Count/frequency of documenting regular rhythm in 0–12h. Primarily a monitoring/EMR behavior proxy and early post-op documentation intensity, not a direct physiologic renal signal.
Count/frequency of documenting regular rhythm in 13–24h. Measurement frequency reflects care intensity rather than kidney-relevant physiology; low sparse-model priority.
Count/frequency of documenting regular rhythm in 25–36h. Still mainly a clinician-monitoring proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond actual rhythm status.

Proportion of time in an irregular rhythm during 0–12h (arrhythmia burden). Potentially relates to low cardiac output/hypoperfusion but early post-op arrhythmias are common/transient, limiting specificity for Hour-60 creatinine.
Proportion of time in an irregular rhythm during 13–24h. Better reflects sustained arrhythmia burden and downstream perfusion risk than 0–12h, but remains an indirect AKI predictor.

Proportion of time in an irregular rhythm during 25–36h. Closest window and a graded measure of ongoing arrhythmia burden that can uniquely proxy persistent hemodynamic compromise relevant to evolving AKI.
Whether any irregular rhythm occurred in 0–12h. Captures presence of arrhythmia but is a coarse binary and early timing is noisy; dominated by later/continuous burden measures.
Whether any irregular rhythm occurred in 13–24h. More informative than 0–12h for persistent instability, but still less rich than irregular_mean and may be redundant if burden is available.
