Indicates any occurrence of irregular cardiac rhythm (e.g., AF) in the 25–36h window. Later arrhythmia can proxy hemodynamic instability/low output affecting renal perfusion, but it is indirect and largely redundant with the corresponding irregular_min indicator.
Early (0–12h) indicator of irregular rhythm presence. Very proximal to surgery with high transient noise and weak direct linkage to Hour-60 creatinine compared with later windows.
Presence of irregular rhythm during 13–24h post-op. Some signal for evolving instability, but still indirect for AKI and dominated by the closer 25–36h rhythm features in a sparse model.

Presence of irregular rhythm in the 25–36h window. This is the most temporally relevant arrhythmia indicator here and may add modest unique value as a hemodynamic-stress proxy, though still not a direct renal marker.
Count/frequency of documenting irregular rhythm in 0–12h. Primarily reflects monitoring intensity/clinical attention rather than physiology, and early timing further reduces usefulness.
Measurement frequency of irregular rhythm in 13–24h. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus simple presence/absence indicators.
Measurement frequency of irregular rhythm in 25–36h. Even though later, it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy and adds little beyond whether irregular rhythm occurred.
Average burden of a non-sinus/non-irregular 'other' rhythm category in 0–12h. Early post-op rhythm labeling is noisy and this category is nonspecific, offering minimal unique renal predictive value.
Mean presence of 'other' rhythm in 13–24h. Nonspecific rhythm classification with unclear mechanistic tie to AKI and likely redundant with more interpretable irregular rhythm indicators.
Average burden of 'other' rhythm in 25–36h. Later timing helps slightly, but the category is heterogeneous and indirect, so it is a weak sparse-model candidate.
Any occurrence of 'other' rhythm in 0–12h. Early transient rhythm changes and nonspecific labeling make this unlikely to add stable predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Any occurrence of 'other' rhythm in 13–24h. Still nonspecific and generally dominated by clearer arrhythmia/hemodynamic features for sparse selection.
Any occurrence of 'other' rhythm in 25–36h. Could weakly proxy ongoing instability, but heterogeneity and redundancy with irregular rhythm features limit its priority.
Presence indicator for 'other' rhythm in 0–12h (binary extreme). Early timing and nonspecific rhythm category provide little unique information for predicting later creatinine rise.
Presence indicator for 'other' rhythm in 13–24h. Weak, indirect signal and likely redundant with other instability markers; low sparse-model priority.
Presence indicator for 'other' rhythm in 25–36h. The closest window makes it slightly more informative, but the rhythm category is vague and offers limited unique predictive value for AKI.

Count/frequency of documenting an 'other' cardiac rhythm/regularity in the first 12h, mainly reflecting monitoring/documentation behavior. Very indirect for AKI and likely noisy/redundant with hemodynamics/vasopressors, so low sparse-model priority.
Measurement frequency of an 'other' rhythm category in 13–24h, a clinician-behavior proxy rather than physiology. Adds little unique renal signal and is dominated by direct perfusion/renal markers.

Later (25–36h) frequency of 'other' rhythm documentation may weakly proxy ongoing instability closer to the creatinine outcome. Still largely a monitoring/documentation artifact and not a direct renal-perfusion signal, so only modest value.

Mean heart rate from pulse-ox monitor in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. HR is a nonspecific illness/adrenergic marker and early values are confounded by anesthesia/bypass effects, so weak unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean HR in 13–24h may reflect sustained stress/low output or sepsis physiology that can contribute to AKI. However it is nonspecific and likely redundant with BP/vasopressor/lactate signals, so low-moderate priority.

Mean HR in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and can capture sustained tachycardia from ongoing low cardiac output or shock, which relates to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect, but among HR summaries this timing makes it the most plausible keeper.

Peak HR (binary-encoded) in 0–12h captures transient tachyarrhythmia/stress early after surgery. Extremes can matter, but early post-op peaks are often nonpersistent and the binary encoding reduces information, so limited sparse-model value.

Peak HR (binary) in 13–24h may indicate ongoing adrenergic drive/arrhythmia associated with hemodynamic compromise. Still nonspecific and likely redundant with more direct perfusion markers; binary max further limits uniqueness.

Peak HR (binary) in 25–36h is the most temporally relevant extreme and may flag late tachyarrhythmia/instability that can precede AKI. Kept higher than earlier epochs, but still indirect and information-poor due to binary encoding.
Minimum HR (binary) in 0–12h often reflects sedation, pacing, or transient bradycardia rather than renal-relevant hypoperfusion. Early timing plus binary encoding makes it a weak and noisy predictor.
Minimum HR (binary) in 13–24h is usually less informative for AKI than hypotension/low MAP and may reflect pacing or medication effects. Adds little unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Minimum HR (binary) in 25–36h could weakly indicate conduction issues/over-sedation with potential low output, closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and dominated by direct perfusion measures, so modest priority.
Frequency of HR measurements from pulse-ox in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy. Routine in ICU and not a direct physiologic renal signal, so very low selection priority.
Measurement count of pulse-ox HR in 13–24h reflects documentation/monitoring rather than kidney-relevant physiology. Likely near-constant across patients and redundant with acuity indicators.
Measurement frequency of pulse-ox HR in 25–36h is still mainly clinician behavior/monitoring intensity. Unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond actual HR/hemodynamic values.

Mean apical HR in 0–12h is an early, intermittently measured vital often influenced by sedation/pacing and overlaps with monitor-derived HR. Redundant with stronger/denser monitor HR features and weak for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.

Mean apical heart rate in 13–24h reflects overall sympathetic tone/illness severity rather than a direct renal signal. Likely redundant with other hemodynamic variables and less informative than extremes or later-window measures for sparse selection.

Mean apical heart rate in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may capture sustained stress/low output states affecting renal perfusion. Still an indirect marker and typically dominated by BP/vasopressor/urine-output features, so moderate priority.

Indicator of a high apical heart-rate extreme in the immediate 0–12h post-op period, which is often driven by transient anesthesia/bypass effects. Early-window tachycardia is noisy and a weak, indirect proxy for AKI risk.

Indicator of tachycardic extreme in 13–24h may reflect pain, arrhythmia, or evolving shock that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. However it is indirect and likely redundant with stronger perfusion/vasopressor signals.

Indicator of tachycardic extreme in 25–36h is temporally closer and may capture ongoing hemodynamic stress relevant to subsequent creatinine rise. Still a nonspecific severity marker and usually secondary to direct renal/perfusion features.

Indicator of low apical heart-rate extreme in 0–12h is commonly influenced by pacing, sedation, and immediate post-op management. Weak and noisy for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Indicator of bradycardic extreme in 13–24h could reflect conduction issues/low output that may affect renal perfusion. Nonetheless it is indirect and often confounded by pacing/medications, limiting unique predictive value.

Indicator of bradycardic extreme in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and could signal persistent low cardiac output. Still largely a management/arrhythmia proxy and typically redundant with direct hemodynamic measures.

Count/frequency of apical HR measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and workflow rather than physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy it has low priority for a sparse predictive model.

Apical HR measurement frequency in 13–24h is primarily a proxy for nursing/monitoring patterns and perceived instability. Generally weak and non-specific for AKI prediction compared with physiologic extremes or renal markers.

Apical HR measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability closer to the outcome. Still largely a monitoring-intensity signal and unlikely to add unique value beyond actual hemodynamics.
Mean peripheral heart rate in 0–12h is an early, often noisy post-op summary and not a direct renal marker. Also redundant with apical HR summaries and other severity/hemodynamic features.

Indicator related to peripheral HR mean in 13–24h (likely presence/availability) is not a direct physiologic renal signal. Redundant with apical HR and offers little unique predictive value for sparse selection.

Indicator related to peripheral HR mean in 25–36h is closer to the outcome but remains indirect and likely reflects measurement/source availability. Generally dominated by apical HR and more direct perfusion/renal variables.
Indicator of peripheral HR maximum in 0–12h captures early tachycardia that is frequently transient and treatment-related. Weak, indirect, and redundant with apical HR extremes.

Indicator of peripheral HR maximum in 13–24h may reflect sustained stress or arrhythmia that can accompany renal hypoperfusion. Still nonspecific and redundant with apical HR max and other hemodynamic severity markers.

Indicates whether the heart rate signal source was peripheral (vs other source) in 25–36h. This is a monitoring/measurement artifact rather than physiology and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI beyond true hemodynamics.
Peripheral HR source indicator in the immediate 0–12h window. Early post-op monitoring choices are noisy and this is a clinician/telemetry artifact, not a renal-risk signal.
Peripheral HR source indicator during 13–24h. Still primarily reflects documentation/monitoring configuration and is dominated by actual HR/BP/vasopressor features (not present here).
Peripheral HR source indicator during 25–36h. Despite being closer to the outcome, it remains a proxy for monitoring setup and offers minimal unique information for creatinine trajectory.

Count/frequency of peripheral HR source measurements in 0–12h. Measurement frequency is mainly a monitoring-intensity proxy and early post-op frequency is especially routine/noisy for sparse selection.
Frequency of peripheral HR source measurements in 13–24h. This is still a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy and unlikely to outperform direct perfusion/renal markers in a sparse model.

Frequency of peripheral HR source measurements in 25–36h. Closer timing could weakly proxy ongoing instability/ICU complexity, but it remains indirect and low-priority versus physiologic renal-risk signals.

Proportion/mean time on mechanical ventilation in 0–12h. Mechanical ventilation reflects illness severity and hemodynamic stress but is early and largely expected post-cardiac surgery, so it is a weak, indirect AKI predictor.

Proportion/mean time on mechanical ventilation in 13–24h. Persistent ventilation beyond the immediate post-op period can signal ongoing shock/respiratory failure linked to AKI risk, making it a moderate sparse-model candidate.

Proportion/mean time on mechanical ventilation in 25–36h. Continued ventilation this late is a strong marker of ongoing critical illness/hemodynamic burden and is relatively close to Hour-60 creatinine, so it is a strong keeper within this batch.
Binary indicator of any mechanical ventilation in 0–12h. Nearly universal immediately post-op and therefore low-variance/noisy, offering little unique predictive value.
Binary indicator of any mechanical ventilation in 13–24h. More informative than 0–12h but still dominated by duration/mean exposure measures (e.g., mechanical_mean) for sparse selection.
Binary indicator of any mechanical ventilation in 25–36h. Useful as a coarse severity flag but redundant with the stronger continuous mean-duration signal in the same window.
Binary indicator of being mechanically ventilated throughout the 0–12h window (min). Early post-op ventilation is common and this adds little discriminative value for later creatinine ratio.
Binary indicator of continuous mechanical ventilation across 13–24h. Captures persistence (an extreme) better than a simple any-vent flag and can reflect sustained severity linked to AKI risk.

Binary indicator of continuous mechanical ventilation across 25–36h. This extreme/persistence measure is close to the outcome and signals ongoing critical illness; it is a strong sparse-model candidate, though somewhat redundant with 25–36h mechanical_mean.

Counts/flags documentation of mechanical oxygen/ventilation in the first 12h, mostly reflecting monitoring intensity and immediate post-op ventilation practices. Weak, indirect AKI signal and early window is noisy; likely redundant with later ventilation status.

Measurement frequency for mechanical oxygen/ventilation in 13–24h, a proxy for ongoing respiratory support and clinician attention. Still indirect for renal injury and dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal markers; modestly more informative than 0–12h.

Measurement frequency for mechanical oxygen/ventilation in 25–36h, closer to the 60h creatinine outcome and may capture persistent critical illness. Still a proxy variable, but timing makes it a somewhat better sparse-model candidate than earlier epochs.

Average proportion of time on no oxygen device in the first 12h, heavily influenced by routine extubation timing and immediate post-op workflow. Very indirect for AKI and likely redundant with later oxygen/ventilation status.
Mean time on room air/no device in 13–24h, reflecting recovery trajectory and lower illness severity. Indirect renal relevance and redundant with the more proximal 25–36h status.

Mean time on no oxygen device in 25–36h, a near-term marker of physiologic recovery vs ongoing support. Still indirect for kidney dysfunction but may add unique trajectory information in a sparse model compared with earlier windows.

Binary indicator that the patient was on no oxygen device at any point in 0–12h (max), largely capturing early extubation/room-air trial. Early post-op variability makes this a weak and noisy proxy for later AKI.

Whether the patient ever reached no oxygen device in 13–24h, reflecting partial respiratory recovery. Some prognostic value but indirect for renal outcomes and less informative than the more proximal 25–36h status.

Whether the patient achieved no oxygen device at any time in 25–36h, capturing recovery by the closest window to the outcome. Still a general severity proxy, but the extreme/any-achievement aggregation can be more informative than the mean for sparse selection.

Binary indicator that the patient was on no oxygen device for the entire 0–12h window (min), essentially immediate room-air status. Rare and dominated by perioperative practice patterns; minimal unique AKI signal.

Indicates sustained no-device status throughout 13–24h, suggesting stable respiratory recovery. Indirect for kidney injury and likely redundant with other severity/trajectory measures, but more meaningful than 0–12h.

Sustained no oxygen device throughout 25–36h, a strong marker of stability close to the 60h creatinine measurement. Still not kidney-specific, but may contribute unique severity/trajectory information in a sparse model.
Frequency of documenting no oxygen device in 0–12h, primarily a charting/monitoring artifact. Very weak and redundant proxy with little direct renal predictive value.
Measurement frequency for no-device status in 13–24h, reflecting documentation patterns more than physiology. Low sparse-model priority because it is a clinician-behavior proxy.

Measurement frequency for no-device status in 25–36h, still largely documentation intensity but closer to outcome timing. Slightly higher priority than earlier measured features, yet remains indirect and proxy-heavy.

Average time on low-flow oxygen in 0–12h, reflecting early respiratory support needs after surgery. Indirect for AKI and early window is noisy; may add limited severity signal but likely dominated by later respiratory/hemodynamic features.

Represents average use of low-flow oxygen in the 13–24h window, a rough marker of respiratory support needs. It is an indirect illness-severity proxy and likely redundant with higher-acuity oxygen/ventilation variables, so low sparse-model priority.

Average low-flow oxygen use closer to the outcome window, reflecting ongoing mild respiratory support. Still indirect for AKI and largely a care-pathway proxy (extubated vs not), so only weak incremental value.

Binary indicator that low-flow oxygen was used at any point in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-op oxygen device choice is noisy and not kidney-specific, making it a poor sparse-selection candidate.

Binary indicator of any low-flow oxygen use during 13–24h, mainly capturing extubation/step-down status. It is a weak, indirect proxy for overall recovery and is dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal signals.

Binary indicator of any low-flow oxygen use during 25–36h, reflecting being off higher-level respiratory support. Some prognostic value as recovery proxy, but still indirect and likely redundant with ventilation/vasopressor features.

Binary indicator of low-flow oxygen use at the minimum level in 0–12h (effectively another presence/absence proxy). Highly redundant with max/mean and heavily confounded by immediate post-op workflow, so minimal value.

Binary minimum indicator for low-flow oxygen in 13–24h, offering little beyond whether low-flow was used at all. Redundant with the corresponding max/mean and not mechanistically tied to creatinine rise.
Binary minimum indicator for low-flow oxygen in 25–36h, essentially duplicating other low-flow presence features. Adds negligible unique information for AKI prediction in a sparse linear model.

Count/frequency of documentation for low-flow oxygen in 0–12h, primarily reflecting monitoring/documentation intensity. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and early post-op charting is especially noisy, so very low priority.
Measurement frequency of low-flow oxygen in 13–24h, more about workflow and attention than physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.
Measurement frequency of low-flow oxygen in 25–36h, a proxy for care setting and documentation rather than renal risk. Provides little unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Binary indicator of high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h, suggesting early respiratory failure severity. It is still indirect for AKI and confounded by immediate post-op practice patterns, so modest at best.

Binary indicator of high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h, reflecting persistent respiratory support needs and higher illness severity. More informative than low-flow and less immediate-post-op noise, but remains an indirect proxy for renal dysfunction.

Binary indicator of high-flow oxygen use in 25–36h, closer to the outcome and signaling ongoing cardiopulmonary instability. Could add some unique severity information, though still not kidney-specific and may be redundant with ventilation/pressor variables.
Binary indicator of any high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h, capturing early need for escalated oxygen therapy. Early timing and overlap with the mean indicator limit unique value for sparse selection.

Binary indicator of any high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h, capturing an extreme (any escalation) that may better reflect clinically meaningful respiratory compromise. Among this batch it is one of the stronger severity proxies, though still indirect for AKI.

Represents need for high-flow oxygen support in the 25–36h window, a marker of ongoing respiratory/cardiac dysfunction that can correlate with renal hypoperfusion/venous congestion. Moderately useful as a late severity signal, but indirect for AKI and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal features not shown here.

Indicates whether high-flow oxygen was used at all early (0–12h), when post-bypass/anesthesia effects and extubation timing add noise. Weak and indirect for predicting 60h creatinine ratio and dominated by later oxygen-support indicators.

Captures presence of high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h, reflecting intermediate respiratory support needs. Some prognostic value as illness-severity proxy, but still indirect for renal injury and less informative than the closer 25–36h window.

Captures any high-flow oxygen use in the closest window (25–36h), suggesting persistent cardiopulmonary compromise that may track with AKI risk. Similar signal to the 25–36h max; keep one of these in a sparse model, with only moderate priority due to indirectness.

Measurement frequency of high-flow oxygen documentation in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/workflow proxy rather than physiology. Low sparse-model value and especially noisy in the immediate post-op period.

Counts/documentation frequency for high-flow oxygen in 13–24h, reflecting clinician behavior and charting intensity more than renal pathophysiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond the binary presence/absence features.

Documentation frequency of high-flow oxygen in 25–36h may weakly proxy persistent support needs and ICU complexity. Still largely a behavior/monitoring signal and typically dominated by the binary high-flow usage indicators.
Frequency of documented first-degree AV block in 0–12h is a monitoring/ECG-labeling proxy and often transient post-op. Minimal direct linkage to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of first-degree AV block in 13–24h reflects documentation intensity rather than a causal renal signal. Weak, indirect, and likely redundant with broader hemodynamic instability markers.

Measurement frequency of first-degree AV block in 25–36h remains primarily a charting proxy. Even though later timing is closer to outcome, the feature is not a strong or unique predictor of creatinine rise.

Frequency of documented high-grade AV block (2nd/3rd degree) in 0–12h can reflect significant conduction disease and potential low-output episodes. Still a measurement proxy and early post-op pacing/temporary block can be transient, limiting sparse-model priority.

Measurement frequency of high-grade AV block in 13–24h may correlate with ongoing cardiac instability and low cardiac output risk. Indirect for AKI and behavior-proxy heavy, so only a weak-to-moderate candidate.

Measurement frequency of high-grade AV block in 25–36h suggests persistent conduction instability/pacing needs, which can associate with renal hypoperfusion. Among the heart-block measurement features this is the most temporally relevant, but still indirect and proxy-like.

Frequency of documented bundle branch block in 0–12h is largely an ECG-labeling/monitoring proxy and often reflects baseline conduction disease. Weak connection to acute renal trajectory and early timing adds noise.

Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 13–24h is unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI beyond general cardiac comorbidity. Primarily a documentation proxy with limited renal specificity.

Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 25–36h remains a proxy for ECG monitoring rather than a direct renal insult marker. Low priority for sparse selection compared with more direct hemodynamic/renal features.

Average ionized calcium in the immediate post-op window reflects electrolyte management and bypass-related shifts more than evolving AKI. Likely redundant with later windows and extremes, so low sparse-selection priority.
Mean ionized calcium 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness or transfusion/citrate effects but is an indirect renal signal. Still largely replaceable by later (25–36h) values and min/max.

Mean ionized calcium closest to the outcome window can capture sustained hypocalcemia/hypercalcemia from ongoing instability or renal/metabolic dysfunction. Among means, this is the most temporally relevant and may add modest unique value.
Peak ionized calcium in the first 12h is often driven by supplementation and peri-bypass physiology rather than kidney injury trajectory. Early max is noisy and unlikely to survive sparse screening.
Maximum ionized calcium 13–24h is typically treatment-influenced and not a direct precursor of creatinine rise. Adds little unique predictive value versus minima or later-window summaries.

Later peak ionized calcium may reflect aggressive replacement or metabolic derangements closer to Hour 60, but remains indirect for AKI. Could weakly proxy severity/treatment intensity, so still low priority.

Early nadir ionized calcium can indicate citrate load/transfusion or hemodilution and may correlate with early shock, but is often transient post-CPB. Some physiologic signal, yet timing noise limits sparse-model value.

Nadir ionized calcium 13–24h can better reflect persistent metabolic stress and ongoing critical illness than the immediate post-op period. As an extreme, it is more informative than the mean/max but still indirect for renal injury.

Nadir ionized calcium in the closest window may capture ongoing instability (e.g., shock, transfusion/citrate effects) that can precede AKI. Among calcium features, this extreme in the latest epoch is the most plausible sparse keeper.

Count/frequency of ionized calcium measurements mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern in the immediate post-op period. This is a proxy variable and generally dominated by actual calcium values.
Measurement frequency 13–24h is primarily a care-pattern/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

More frequent calcium checks late (25–36h) may indicate ongoing instability or complications closer to the outcome window. Still a monitoring proxy and typically weaker than physiologic extremes, but could add small incremental signal.
Mean magnesium early post-op reflects replacement practices and CPB-related shifts more than kidney function trajectory. Early timing and treatment confounding reduce sparse-selection priority.
Average magnesium 13–24h may weakly reflect renal clearance and illness severity but is indirect and often protocol-driven. Likely redundant with later magnesium summaries if available.

Mean magnesium closest to Hour 60 can reflect reduced renal excretion (rising Mg) or ongoing replacement needs, providing a modest renal/metabolic signal. Among magnesium means, this is the most temporally informative and a potential sparse candidate.
Peak magnesium in the first 12h is commonly driven by intra/post-op supplementation and transient shifts rather than evolving AKI. High redundancy/noise makes it a poor sparse-model feature.
Peak magnesium in 13–24h reflects supplementation/renal handling but is usually a weak, indirect AKI signal. Likely redundant with closer 25–36h magnesium and more direct perfusion/acid–base markers.

Peak magnesium in 25–36h may capture reduced renal excretion or heavy replacement closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and treatment-influenced, but the late timing gives it more sparse-model value than earlier magnesium summaries.
Early nadir magnesium is commonly driven by bypass-related shifts and routine repletion rather than evolving AKI. High noise and temporally distant from Hour 60, so low priority for sparse selection.

Low magnesium in 13–24h can reflect ongoing losses or under-repletion, but it is not a specific renal dysfunction precursor. Some signal of illness/therapy, yet generally dominated by later physiologic markers.

Late low magnesium may indicate ongoing diuresis/renal wasting or inadequate replacement, but it is not a direct kidney injury marker. Useful mainly as a weak context variable and often redundant with stronger hemodynamic/acid–base features.
Measurement count is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy in the immediate post-op period. Weak direct physiologic content and likely noisy, so poor sparse-model candidate.

Frequency of magnesium checks reflects care intensity and electrolyte management rather than kidney function itself. Adds little unique predictive value versus actual magnesium levels or perfusion markers.
Late measurement frequency may weakly proxy evolving instability/AKI concern closer to Hour 60. Still a behavior proxy and typically inferior to physiologic extremes, so only modest priority.

Average base excess in 0–12h reflects early metabolic acidosis/alkalosis after surgery, often confounded by bypass and resuscitation. Mean smooths extremes and early timing is noisy, limiting sparse-model value.

Mean base excess in 13–24h captures sustained metabolic derangement that can track hypoperfusion and AKI risk. Moderately useful, but likely less informative than late-window or extreme (min) base excess.

Mean base excess in 25–36h reflects persistent metabolic acidosis/alkalosis close to the prediction horizon, correlating with ongoing shock, lactate burden, and renal hypoperfusion. Strong sparse-model candidate due to timing and sustained exposure signal.

Maximum (most alkalemic) base excess early post-op is often driven by ventilation/bicarbonate/transfusion practices rather than renal injury trajectory. Early and less pathophysiologically tied to AKI than acidotic extremes.
Higher (alkalemic) base excess in 13–24h is typically treatment-related and not a strong precursor of creatinine rise. Dominated by base excess minima/late-window summaries for AKI prediction.

Late alkalemia may reflect aggressive diuresis/ventilation or bicarbonate therapy and is only weakly linked to AKI risk. Some contextual value due to timing, but generally inferior to acidotic extremes or mean derangement.

Minimum base excess in 0–12h captures worst early metabolic acidosis, a marker of hypoperfusion that can precede AKI. However, immediate post-op acidosis can be transient/noisy, so not top-tier versus later windows.

Minimum base excess in 13–24h captures significant metabolic acidosis after initial stabilization, more likely reflecting true ongoing hypoperfusion/inflammation linked to AKI. Strong sparse-model feature, though potentially slightly less valuable than a 25–36h minimum if available.

Minimum base excess in the 25–36h window captures the worst metabolic acidosis, often reflecting hypoperfusion/shock that can precede AKI. As a late-window extreme it is a strong, relatively nonredundant severity-of-illness signal likely to add value in a sparse model.

Count/frequency of base excess measurements in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-concern proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is noisy and unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond physiologic extremes.

Measurement frequency of base excess at 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than a direct renal or perfusion biomarker. Typically redundant with actual acid-base values and other severity indicators.

Later (25–36h) base excess measurement frequency may weakly indicate ongoing instability prompting repeated labs. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and dominated by the actual base excess extreme (e.g., min).
Frequency of direct bilirubin checks in 0–12h is a monitoring proxy and not a direct kidney-function marker. Early post-op ordering patterns are unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Direct bilirubin measurement frequency at 13–24h mainly reflects clinician concern for hepatic/cholestatic issues or complexity of care. It is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with broader severity signals.

Later direct bilirubin measurement frequency can indicate persistent organ dysfunction prompting continued lab surveillance. Still indirect for creatinine rise and generally weaker than physiologic/hemodynamic extremes for sparse prediction.
Mean WBC in the first 12h reflects early inflammatory response to surgery/CPB and is often nonspecific. As an early mean it is noisy and unlikely to add unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Mean WBC at 13–24h may reflect evolving inflammation/infection risk that can contribute to AKI, but it is nonspecific. The mean is also typically dominated by later or extreme WBC summaries.
Mean WBC at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture sustained inflammation, but remains a generic severity marker. Likely redundant with WBC extremes and other organ dysfunction indicators.

Peak WBC in 0–12h captures the strongest early inflammatory response but is heavily influenced by immediate post-op stress and steroids. As an early extreme it is plausible yet not a high-priority sparse feature.

Peak WBC at 13–24h can signal infection/systemic inflammation that may precipitate AKI and is more informative than a mean. It is still indirect, but among WBC features this extreme in the mid window is a better sparse candidate.

Peak WBC at 25–36h is the closest extreme measure and may reflect ongoing inflammatory/infectious stress associated with renal dysfunction. Still nonspecific, but likely the best WBC-derived predictor in this batch.

Minimum WBC in 0–12h is rarely a direct driver of AKI and may reflect dilutional effects or lab variability early post-op. Low unique predictive value and dominated by more informative extremes (e.g., max) and later windows.
Minimum WBC at 13–24h is an indirect and generally weak signal for AKI risk compared with WBC max or perfusion markers. Unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Minimum WBC at 25–36h is not a strong mechanistic precursor of creatinine rise and is typically less informative than peak WBC or acid-base extremes. Low priority for a sparse linear model.
Count of WBC lab draws in the immediate post-op window, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity. This is a clinician-behavior proxy with weak, indirect linkage to AKI and low sparse-model priority.
Frequency of WBC checks in 13–24h, reflecting surveillance rather than physiology. Likely redundant with overall acuity/ICU workflow and not a direct renal signal.

Frequency of WBC checks closer to the outcome window, still primarily a monitoring/concern proxy. Could weakly tag evolving complications/sepsis that affect kidneys, but unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Average PT early after surgery, reflecting coagulation status/hepatic function and dilutional effects. Early means are noisy and less informative than later or extreme PT values for downstream AKI risk.

Average PT at 25–36h, capturing more stable coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction and illness severity closer to Hour 60. Useful but still indirect for renal injury and likely dominated by extreme PT if present.

Peak PT in the first 12h, potentially reflecting bleeding/transfusion, hepatic hypoperfusion, or anticoagulation effects. Timing is early and may be transient; provides limited unique renal prediction versus later PT abnormalities.

Peak PT at 25–36h, a strong marker of ongoing coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction and systemic derangement near the prediction horizon. The extreme (max) preserves acute pathology better than mean/min and is the best PT aggregation here for sparse selection.
Lowest PT early post-op, often reflecting normal/low values or assay variability rather than organ dysfunction. Provides little signal for AKI and is dominated by PT max/mean features.
Lowest PT at 25–36h is generally less pathophysiologically informative than elevated PT for shock/liver dysfunction. Likely redundant and low yield for predicting creatinine rise.
Number of PT measurements in 0–12h, mainly indicating monitoring/transfusion/bleeding management. Proxy-heavy and not a direct renal physiology marker, so low sparse-model priority.
PT measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician concern and care complexity. Weak indirect association with AKI and unlikely to add unique value beyond actual PT values.
PT measurement frequency closer to Hour 60 may tag ongoing instability/bleeding workup. Still a proxy and generally dominated by PT max/mean, but slightly more informative due to temporal proximity.
Mean NRBCs early post-op, a marker of severe physiologic stress/hypoxemia/inflammation. Early values can be transient and indirect for AKI, so modest priority.

Mean NRBCs at 13–24h, reflecting sustained stress/critical illness that can correlate with multiorgan dysfunction including AKI. Indirect but potentially additive if few stronger renal perfusion markers are available.

Mean NRBCs at 25–36h, closer to the outcome and suggestive of ongoing hypoxemia/shock burden associated with subsequent creatinine rise. Provides a relatively unique severity signal compared with coagulation metrics, so strong sparse-model candidate.
Peak NRBCs in the first 12h, capturing acute stress response but in a noisy immediate post-op period. Potentially informative yet early and likely less predictive than later NRBC burden.

Peak NRBCs reflect physiologic stress/hypoxemia/inflammation rather than a direct renal marker. Mid-window timing is reasonable but this is a nonspecific severity proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine_ratio.

Peak NRBCs in the closest window may correlate with ongoing critical illness and hypoxia. Still indirect for AKI and typically dominated by more kidney-specific/hemodynamic features, so low sparse-model priority.
Lowest NRBCs early post-op is largely uninformative and may reflect lab noise/absence of stress signal. Early window plus weak pathophysiologic link to AKI makes it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Minimum NRBCs is not a meaningful insult marker and mostly reflects baseline/measurement variability. Adds little unique predictive value for later creatinine rise compared with max or other organ-perfusion variables.
Minimum NRBCs in 25–36h is unlikely to encode renal risk and is dominated by more informative extremes (e.g., max) if NRBCs matter at all. Very low priority for sparse selection.
Measurement count is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op ordering patterns are noisy and unlikely to add unique value for creatinine_ratio.
NRBC measurement frequency reflects care intensity and lab ordering rather than kidney injury biology. Weak, indirect signal and typically redundant with overall acuity indicators.
Late-window NRBC measurement frequency may indicate ongoing concern but remains a proxy variable. Low priority versus physiologic extremes or direct renal/perfusion markers.

Average glucose early post-op reflects stress response and glycemic management but is not kidney-specific. Mean in 0–12h is prone to transient peri-bypass effects and is likely redundant with later glucose extremes.

Mean glucose in 13–24h captures sustained stress hyperglycemia, which can correlate with illness severity and AKI risk. However, it is indirect and often dominated by max (extreme dysglycemia) and closer-window measures.

Mean glucose in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect sustained metabolic stress/infection/vasopressor exposure linked to AKI risk. Still indirect, but among glucose summaries this has better timing and may add modest unique value.

Peak glucose early post-op reflects acute stress and catecholamine exposure but is influenced by perioperative factors and insulin protocols. As an early extreme it may carry some signal, yet it is indirect and less reliable than later peaks.

Peak glucose in 13–24h better reflects evolving stress response and treatment intensity beyond immediate bypass effects. As an extreme it can outperform the mean, but remains a nonspecific severity marker for sparse selection.

Peak glucose in the closest window may capture ongoing severe stress/catecholamine use associated with renal hypoperfusion risk. It is still indirect for AKI but is the most informative glucose aggregation here (extreme + best timing).

Minimum glucose can indicate hypoglycemia from insulin or poor intake, which may track instability. Early timing and protocol-driven effects limit specificity, but extremes can add some signal beyond means.

Minimum glucose in 13–24h may reflect overtreatment or reduced reserves and can correlate with higher acuity. Still indirect and typically less predictive than late-window glucose extremes for creatinine_ratio.

Lowest glucose in the 25–36h window reflects hypoglycemia/stress-dysglycemia and overall physiologic instability. It can add some predictive signal for AKI risk but is indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal-specific markers in a sparse model.

Count/frequency of glucose measurements in the immediate 0–12h period is mainly a monitoring/insulin-protocol intensity proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is noisy and not a direct renal physiology signal, so low sparse-selection priority.
Frequency of glucose checks at 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and ICU complexity more than kidney injury biology. Redundant as a generic monitoring proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of glucose measurements at 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability or insulin infusion needs closer to the outcome time. Still primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity feature, so only modest value.

Average platelet count in 0–12h reflects post-CPB consumption/hemodilution and bleeding/inflammation. Early means are noisy and largely redundant with later/extreme platelet summaries for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean platelets at 13–24h captures evolving thrombocytopenia/inflammation but smooths extremes. Provides limited unique AKI signal and is likely dominated by platelet minima and later windows in a sparse model.

Mean platelet count at 25–36h reflects sustained thrombocytopenia closer to the outcome and may correlate with ongoing inflammation/bleeding/organ dysfunction. Still indirect for renal injury and somewhat redundant with platelet minima.

Maximum platelet count in 0–12h is rarely the clinically informative direction for AKI risk and is heavily influenced by perioperative dilution/transfusion timing. Low unique predictive value and dominated by minima/later values.
Platelet maximum at 13–24h is not a strong pathophysiologic precursor of AKI compared with thrombocytopenia measures. Likely redundant/noisy and low priority for sparse selection.
Maximum platelets at 25–36h generally reflects recovery or transfusion rather than renal hypoperfusion/injury. Adds little unique information versus platelet minima/means, so low priority.

Lowest platelet count in 0–12h captures early thrombocytopenia from CPB/bleeding/inflammation, which can associate with later organ dysfunction. However, early post-op effects are transient/noisy, so moderate sparse value.

Platelet nadir at 13–24h better reflects true evolving thrombocytopenia (inflammation, bleeding, consumptive processes) beyond immediate CPB effects. As an extreme, it preserves insult signal and can add unique predictive value for AKI trajectory.

Platelet nadir at 25–36h is the closest extreme summary to Hour 60 and captures persistent/worsening thrombocytopenia linked to ongoing critical illness and organ dysfunction. Likely the most useful platelet-derived feature in this batch for sparse selection.
Platelet measurement frequency in 0–12h is largely routine post-op lab scheduling and a monitoring-intensity proxy. Not a direct renal signal and unlikely to add unique value.
Frequency of platelet checks at 13–24h mainly reflects clinician concern/bleeding workup rather than kidney physiology. Redundant proxy feature with low sparse-model priority.
Platelet measurement frequency at 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability or bleeding evaluation closer to the outcome. Still primarily a behavior/monitoring proxy, so only modest importance.

Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) reflects RBC indices/anemia phenotype rather than acute renal physiology. Early post-op mean values are unlikely to add unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and are dominated by hemodynamic/renal markers.

MCH mean in 13–24h is a stable hematologic index with weak mechanistic linkage to evolving AKI. Low sparse-model priority because it is indirect and largely redundant with broader anemia/bleeding/transfusion context not captured here.
MCH mean at 25–36h is temporally closer but still not a direct kidney function or perfusion marker. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given minimal unique predictive value for creatinine rise.
Maximum MCH in the immediate 0–12h window mainly reflects lab variability or baseline RBC characteristics. Extremes here do not plausibly encode renal insult and are low-value for sparse prediction.
Peak MCH over 13–24h is not a meaningful AKI precursor and is likely noise/measurement variation. Redundant within the MCH family and not competitive for a limited feature budget.

Peak MCH at 25–36h remains a hematologic index without near-direct information about renal hypoperfusion or injury. Very low priority versus physiologic/lab markers more proximal to creatinine trajectory.
Minimum MCH in 0–12h is unlikely to reflect an acute process relevant to AKI and is susceptible to perioperative dilution/transfusion effects. Weak and redundant signal for sparse modeling.
Low MCH over 13–24h reflects chronic microcytosis/iron status more than postoperative renal injury. Not expected to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Low MCH at 25–36h is still a chronic RBC characteristic and not a direct renal biomarker. Even with better timing, it is unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model.

Count/frequency of MCH measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/ordering proxy rather than physiology. In 0–12h it mostly reflects routine post-op labs and adds little unique predictive signal.
Measurement frequency of MCH in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior proxy and not a renal injury marker. Low sparse-model value unless no better acuity indicators exist.
MCH measurement frequency at 25–36h may weakly reflect complexity but is still indirect and noisy. Default low priority compared with physiologic extremes or direct renal labs.

Mean serum sodium reflects fluid balance, dilution, and neurohormonal stress, which can correlate with illness severity and renal handling. However 0–12h values are often confounded by bypass/fluids and are less reliable for Hour-60 prediction.

Mean sodium in 13–24h can capture evolving free-water balance and renal concentrating ability, indirectly relating to AKI risk. Moderate sparse priority as a general physiologic signal, though still indirect compared with creatinine/urine output/hemodynamics.

Mean sodium at 25–36h is the closest window and may reflect ongoing renal water handling and diuretic/fluid strategy associated with AKI trajectory. Useful but not top-tier because it is indirect and can be driven by treatment decisions.

Peak sodium in 0–12h may indicate hypernatremia from diuresis/insensible losses or sodium load, which can track severity and renal dysfunction. Early timing and potential iatrogenic drivers limit uniqueness, so it remains a weak-to-plausible candidate.

Peak sodium in the 13–24h window reflects hypernatremia/free-water deficit or diuretic effect. It is an indirect, often management-driven signal and likely redundant with the closer 25–36h sodium extremes for sparse selection.

Peak sodium in the 25–36h window can capture evolving dehydration/diuresis or osmotic load closer to the outcome. Still indirect for AKI, but the late-window extreme is the most potentially informative sodium summary in this batch.

Early postoperative nadir sodium is commonly influenced by bypass prime, fluids, and lab dilution. High noise and weak mechanistic linkage to later creatinine ratio make it a low-priority sparse feature.

Nadir sodium at 13–24h may reflect ongoing hypotonic fluid exposure or SIADH-like physiology. It is still a nonspecific illness/management marker and generally weaker than later (25–36h) extremes for predicting Hour-60 creatinine.

Late-window nadir sodium may indicate persistent fluid overload/hemodilution or impaired water handling nearer to the creatinine outcome. It remains indirect but is the best-timed sodium-min signal and could add modest unique value.
Count/indicator of sodium measurement in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy. Early postoperative labs are routine, so this adds little unique predictive information for sparse modeling.
Sodium measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior/acuity rather than renal physiology. Likely redundant with other severity indicators and low value for a sparse linear model.

More frequent sodium checks at 25–36h can signal evolving instability (fluid/diuretic titration) closer to the outcome. Still a proxy variable, but timing makes it slightly more informative than earlier measurement indicators.

Whether TEG platelet aggregation was measured early mainly reflects bleeding/antiplatelet management and monitoring intensity. It is not a direct renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine ratio.

TEG platelet aggregation measurement in 13–24h is a clinician-driven test for hemostasis concerns. It is an indirect proxy (bleeding/transfusion pathways) with weak, non-unique linkage to AKI prediction.

Late TEG platelet aggregation measurement still primarily indicates ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation. Any association with AKI would be mediated and better captured by hemodynamics/transfusion variables, so low sparse priority.

Mean MCHC in 0–12h reflects red cell indices/hemodilution and is typically stable with limited acute interpretability post-bypass. It is a weak, nonspecific correlate and dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers.
Mean MCHC at 13–24h remains a largely static hematologic index with minimal direct renal pathophysiology. Low expected incremental value in a sparse model.

Mean MCHC at 25–36h may weakly reflect ongoing hemodilution/transfusion patterns closer to outcome. Still indirect and likely redundant with hemoglobin/hematocrit or fluid balance features, so only modest priority.
Max MCHC in 0–12h is susceptible to lab artifact and perioperative shifts without clear renal linkage. Early timing and weak mechanistic connection make it a poor sparse feature.

Max MCHC in 13–24h is still a nonspecific red cell index and often reflects measurement variability rather than actionable physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peak MCHC in 25–36h is mainly an RBC index and not a direct renal perfusion/function marker. Likely redundant/noisy relative to hematocrit/hemoglobin signals and unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine rise.
Early (0–12h) low MCHC reflects RBC indices/hemodilution or lab variation more than kidney injury. Early window plus weak mechanistic link makes it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Minimum MCHC in 13–24h is still an indirect anemia/RBC metric with limited unique information for AKI prediction. Dominated by more interpretable oxygen-delivery/hemodilution measures like hematocrit extremes.
Later (25–36h) low MCHC could weakly proxy ongoing hemodilution/transfusion patterns that correlate with illness severity. Still indirect and largely redundant with hematocrit, so low priority.
Count of MCHC measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. In the immediate post-op window it is especially noisy and not a direct renal signal.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity rather than physiology and adds little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio. Unlikely to be retained in a sparse linear model.

More frequent labs in 25–36h may indicate evolving instability/bleeding prompting closer monitoring, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy feature and typically dominated by actual physiologic extremes.

Mean hematocrit in 0–12h reflects hemodilution/transfusion and oxygen-carrying capacity but is confounded by bypass/fluids early post-op. Mean also smooths extremes, so it’s a weaker sparse predictor than later minima.

Average hematocrit in 13–24h may capture sustained anemia/hemodilution affecting renal oxygen delivery. However it is still less informative than minima and somewhat redundant with other hematocrit summaries.

Mean hematocrit in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 outcome and can reflect sustained anemia/hemodilution relevant to renal hypoxia risk. Still likely secondary to the minimum hematocrit for capturing acute low oxygen-carrying capacity.

Early peak hematocrit is often driven by hemoconcentration/diuresis or measurement variability and is not a typical driver of AKI prediction. In sparse selection it is dominated by low hematocrit (oxygen delivery) and later windows.
Maximum hematocrit in 13–24h is an indirect signal and less pathophysiologically tied to AKI than low hematocrit. Adds little unique value compared with hematocrit minima/means.

Later high hematocrit could weakly reflect hemoconcentration from negative fluid balance, which can relate to renal perfusion, but directionality is inconsistent. Generally less informative than low hematocrit and thus lower sparse priority.

Minimum hematocrit in 0–12h captures acute anemia/hemodilution/transfusion needs that can affect renal oxygen delivery. Early timing is noisier post-bypass, but the extreme is still more informative than the mean/max.

Minimum hematocrit in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful anemia/hemodilution after initial post-op noise settles, impacting renal oxygen delivery and AKI risk. Stronger and less confounded than 0–12h and less redundant than mean/max.

Minimum hematocrit in 25–36h is the closest extreme oxygen-carrying-capacity signal to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and can reflect ongoing bleeding/hemodilution/transfusion burden. Among this batch it is the most plausible unique, high-yield predictor for sparse selection.

Count of hematocrit checks in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring/intensity proxy rather than renal physiology. Early post-op measurement frequency is noisy and unlikely to add unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of hematocrit in 13–24h reflects clinician concern/complexity more than kidney injury mechanisms. Low priority for sparse selection versus direct perfusion/renal markers.

Later (25–36h) monitoring intensity may weakly track ongoing bleeding/hemodilution or instability that can contribute to AKI risk. Still largely a behavior proxy and dominated by actual hemodynamics/renal labs if available.
Mean basophil count is a nonspecific differential component with little mechanistic link to postoperative AKI. Early window and mean aggregation make it unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Basophil mean in 13–24h is still a weak, nonspecific inflammatory marker and rarely informative for renal trajectory. Redundant with broader WBC/inflammation signals and low unique predictive value.
Peak basophil count in 0–12h is unlikely to capture a meaningful renal insult and is prone to lab noise. Even as an extreme, it is not a plausible sparse-model keeper for creatinine ratio.
Max basophil count in 13–24h remains a low-specificity signal and is unlikely to add incremental prediction beyond general illness severity markers. Low priority for sparse selection.
Minimum basophil count early post-op is typically clinically uninformative and may reflect measurement variability. Not a credible predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse linear model.
Minimum basophil count in 13–24h is nonspecific and unlikely to relate to renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. Strongly dominated by more direct renal/perfusion features.
Number of basophil measurements is essentially CBC frequency, a monitoring proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op frequency is especially noisy and low value for sparse selection.
CBC/basophil measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects care intensity and complexity more than kidney injury. Unlikely to add unique predictive signal for creatinine ratio.

Later CBC measurement frequency may weakly indicate ongoing complications (infection/bleeding) that correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy feature and should be deprioritized versus direct renal function/hemodynamic variables.

Mean lymphocyte percentage early post-op reflects stress response/inflammation but is indirect for AKI. Early timing and mean aggregation reduce its sparse-model priority, though it may weakly track systemic illness.

Mean lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h can reflect evolving systemic inflammatory/stress state that correlates with organ dysfunction risk. Moderately useful but still indirect and likely replaceable by stronger severity/perfusion markers.

Maximum lymphocyte percentage in 0–12h is an extreme but often driven by hemodilution/leukocyte shifts rather than kidney-specific injury. Early window limits reliability for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peak lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h may capture an atypical immune/stress trajectory (relative lymphocytosis/less stress leukogram) associated with overall recovery vs complications. Still indirect for AKI, but among this batch it is one of the more plausible non-proxy signals.

Early postoperative lymphopenia reflects stress/inflammation rather than a direct renal signal. In a sparse model it is indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers, plus 0–12h is noisy.

Minimum lymphocyte percent in 13–24h may track ongoing inflammatory response/illness severity. Still an indirect proxy for AKI risk and unlikely to add unique value versus more proximal perfusion/renal features.
Measurement frequency is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ordering behavior proxy. It is not a physiologic kidney injury marker and is especially uninformative in the immediate 0–12h window.
Counts how often lymphocyte percent was checked, reflecting clinician concern or lab panel frequency. This is a weak, indirect proxy and typically redundant with overall lab intensity/acuity.

Later measurement frequency still reflects monitoring behavior rather than renal physiology. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection compared with true renal/perfusion variables.

Having a random vancomycin level drawn suggests vancomycin exposure and/or concern for altered clearance. It is mostly a clinician-behavior/exposure proxy and early timing makes it less specific for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Random vancomycin level measurement indicates antibiotic use and possible concern for renal dysfunction affecting dosing. Still indirect (no level value) and likely redundant with other severity/medication features.

Closer-to-target timing makes a random level draw a stronger proxy for evolving impaired clearance/AKI prompting monitoring. Still indirect and behavior-driven, but may add some unique signal in a sparse model if few renal markers exist.
Trough measurement implies vancomycin therapy and dosing/clearance monitoring. As a binary “measured” flag it is a weak proxy and early postoperative period is confounded by routine protocols.

Trough monitoring in 13–24h may reflect concern for accumulation or protocolized monitoring. It remains an indirect clinician-behavior marker without the actual trough value, so limited sparse-model priority.

Trough measurement at 25–36h is nearer to Hour 60 and may reflect emerging reduced clearance prompting closer monitoring. Still a proxy (no concentration), but comparatively the most informative among the vanc “measured” flags.

Average oxygen saturation in 0–12h reflects early respiratory status and overall instability, which can contribute to renal hypoxia indirectly. Mean values smooth transient desaturations and early window is noisy, limiting unique predictive value.
Mean SpO2 in 13–24h captures sustained oxygenation adequacy and illness severity. It is still an indirect AKI predictor and likely weaker than hemodynamic/renal-specific features in sparse selection.
Mean SpO2 closer to Hour 60 better reflects ongoing hypoxemia that can worsen renal perfusion/oxygen delivery. Still indirect, but timing makes it the best of the SpO2 means for sparse inclusion.
Maximum SpO2 is usually near 100% and reflects supplemental oxygen/ventilator settings more than pathology. It is largely non-discriminative and dominated by mean/min oxygenation metrics.

Max SpO2 in 13–24h is typically ceiling-limited and driven by oxygen therapy rather than physiologic reserve. It adds little unique information and is redundant with more informative oxygenation summaries.
Represents best oxygenation achieved in the 25–36h window. Max SpO2 is rarely informative for AKI risk and is dominated by hypoxemia/hypoperfusion extremes and renal labs.
Captures early postoperative hypoxemia episodes. It is an indirect illness-severity signal and early (0–12h) values are noisy and less predictive of Hour-60 creatinine than later minima.
Represents hypoxemia in the 13–24h window. Still an indirect proxy for cardiopulmonary instability and likely redundant with the closer 25–36h minimum if selecting sparsely.

Captures the closest-window (25–36h) hypoxemia extreme, which can reflect ongoing low oxygen delivery contributing to renal injury. Among SpO2 features, this minimum is the most plausible sparse keeper, though still indirect vs renal markers.
Number/frequency of SpO2 measurements in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity rather than physiology. This is primarily a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency of SpO2 in 13–24h is a monitoring/acuity proxy. Typically redundant with other severity indicators and not a direct renal signal.
Measurement frequency of SpO2 in 25–36h mainly reflects care intensity and device use. It is not a physiologic biomarker and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Mean ALT in 0–12h reflects early hepatic injury/ischemia or baseline liver disease. It is an indirect marker of systemic hypoperfusion and early postoperative values are noisy for predicting Hour-60 creatinine.
Peak ALT in 0–12h can capture ischemic hepatitis from low-flow states, which correlates with AKI risk. The extreme is more informative than mean/min, but it remains indirect and early-timed.
Minimum ALT in 0–12h is generally not clinically meaningful for organ injury. It is dominated by ALT max (injury signal) and adds little unique predictive value.
ALT measurement count in 0–12h is a clinician-suspicion/monitoring proxy. It does not directly encode renal physiology and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
ALT measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects monitoring patterns rather than hepatic injury magnitude. Low priority for sparse selection due to proxy nature and redundancy.
ALT measurement frequency in 25–36h is primarily a care-intensity proxy. Without the ALT value itself, it offers little unique information for predicting creatinine ratio.

Mean BUN in 0–12h reflects baseline renal reserve, catabolism, and early perfusion/volume status. It is a direct-ish renal-related lab but early postoperative dilution/fluids can blunt its specificity.
Mean BUN in 13–24h better reflects evolving renal dysfunction and volume status after immediate post-op noise. Strong renal-adjacent signal likely to add value in sparse selection.

Mean BUN in 25–36h is the closest renal-related lab window to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and captures trajectory toward azotemia. High-priority sparse feature with direct relevance to impending creatinine rise.

Early peak BUN reflects azotemia from hypoperfusion/catabolism but is noisy immediately post-op. Useful renal-perfusion context, yet less predictive than later-window BUN and often redundant with creatinine-related signals.

Peak BUN in 13–24h captures evolving azotemia after initial bypass/anesthesia effects settle. Stronger sparse-model candidate than 0–12h and may add value beyond hemodynamics as an early renal dysfunction marker.

Closest-window peak BUN is a near-direct marker of evolving renal dysfunction/underperfusion heading toward Hour 60. The max aggregation preserves acute worsening and is likely to survive sparse selection within this batch.
Lowest BUN early post-op is usually driven by hemodilution and fluid shifts rather than kidney injury. Weak and potentially misleading for Hour 60 creatinine ratio, dominated by later BUN maxima.
Minimum BUN in this window mainly reflects dilution/nutrition rather than AKI trajectory. Adds little unique predictive value compared with BUN maxima in later epochs.

Later minimum BUN could reflect ongoing dilution or low protein load, occasionally informative about volume status. Still generally less aligned with AKI risk than BUN max and likely redundant in a sparse model.
Measurement count is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op labs are routine, so it adds minimal unique signal.
Frequency of BUN checks reflects care patterns and perceived acuity more than renal function itself. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when actual BUN values are available.
More frequent BUN measurement late may indicate concern for renal deterioration and could weakly proxy instability. Still indirect and dominated by the BUN level (especially max) for prediction.
Whether amylase was checked early is a clinician-suspicion proxy (e.g., pancreatitis/ischemia) and not a direct renal marker. Very low priority for predicting creatinine ratio.
Amylase testing reflects diagnostic workup rather than kidney physiology. Adds little unique predictive value for AKI compared with renal labs/hemodynamics.
Late amylase measurement (even if numeric) is not mechanistically tied to creatinine rise and is likely sparse/noisy. Low yield and largely a behavior proxy in this context.

Mean pH early reflects acid-base status during immediate post-op instability and ventilation changes. Some association with shock/poor perfusion, but timing is noisy and indirect for Hour 60 renal outcome.
Mean pH in 13–24h better reflects sustained metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/sepsis, which can precede AKI. Moderately useful but still a nonspecific severity marker.

Later mean pH captures ongoing acidosis close to the prediction horizon, signaling persistent hypoperfusion/critical illness that drives AKI. Stronger sparse-model candidate among pH features due to proximity and sustained exposure.
Early maximum pH often reflects transient respiratory alkalosis/ventilator management rather than renal-relevant hypoperfusion. Poor specificity and dominated by later pH mean as a severity signal.
Represents peak alkalemia in the 13–24h window. Max pH is usually less informative for AKI risk than acidemia/hypoperfusion signals and is largely redundant with pH_min for severity.
Represents peak alkalemia closest to the outcome window (25–36h). Still a weak/indirect renal signal and typically dominated by low pH (acidemia) or hemodynamic/renal markers in sparse selection.
Represents worst acidemia immediately post-op, which can reflect hypoperfusion and shock. Early (0–12h) values are noisier after bypass/anesthesia, so it’s a moderate but not top sparse-model keeper.

Represents worst acidemia in 13–24h, a more stable period where low pH can indicate ongoing hypoperfusion/lactate burden linked to evolving AKI. This extreme aggregation is likely to add unique severity/trajectory information.

Represents worst acidemia in the closest available window (25–36h), capturing near-term physiologic derangement (shock/respiratory failure) that strongly precedes AKI and creatinine rise. Among pH features, this extreme and timing make it the highest-priority sparse predictor.

Counts whether/ how often pH was measured in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and clinician concern. It is a proxy variable and early post-op practice-driven, so low priority for sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of pH in 13–24h is primarily a care-intensity proxy rather than physiology. Likely redundant with actual pH extremes and other severity markers.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability prompting repeated blood gases. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and dominated by pH_min itself, so only slight value.
Whether cholesterol was checked in 0–12h is unrelated to acute renal physiology and mostly reflects ordering habits. Very unlikely to survive sparse feature selection for creatinine_ratio.
Cholesterol measurement in 13–24h is a non-specific process-of-care indicator with minimal mechanistic link to AKI. Adds little unique predictive value.
Cholesterol measurement in 25–36h remains a weak clinician-ordering proxy without direct relevance to near-term creatinine rise. Not a sparse-model keeper.
Mean lymphocyte count early post-op reflects stress response/inflammation and hemodilution. It is indirect for AKI and early timing is noisy, so low-to-moderate priority.
Mean lymphocyte count in 13–24h is a nonspecific illness/inflammatory marker. Likely redundant with stronger severity/organ dysfunction signals and not a top sparse predictor.
Peak lymphocyte count in 0–12h is rarely a meaningful extreme for AKI risk and is heavily influenced by perioperative shifts. Redundant with mean/min and unlikely to add unique value.
Peak lymphocyte count in 13–24h is not a typical pathophysiologic driver or strong marker of AKI trajectory. Adds little beyond other inflammatory/severity variables.

Lowest lymphocyte count in 0–12h can capture stress leukogram/immune suppression and overall physiologic insult, which may correlate with complications including AKI. As an extreme it’s more informative than mean/max, but still indirect and early.

Minimum absolute lymphocyte count reflects stress/inflammation and possible infection risk rather than renal physiology. It is an indirect severity marker and unlikely to add unique value for creatinine_ratio versus hemodynamics/renal-function features.

Whether lymphocytes were measured is mainly a monitoring/ordering-intensity proxy in the immediate post-op window. This is weakly related to AKI risk and is dominated by actual renal function/hemodynamic signals.
Measurement frequency is a clinician-behavior proxy (concern/complexity) rather than a biomarker. Sparse models should generally drop these unless no physiologic data exist.

Later measurement may reflect evolving complications prompting labs, but it remains a proxy rather than a direct renal signal. Slightly more informative due to proximity to Hour 60, but still low priority for sparsity.

Glucose measured (binary) reflects monitoring intensity and routine ICU protocols early after surgery. It provides little unique predictive information for creatinine_ratio compared with actual glucose values or renal/hemodynamic variables.
This is a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy rather than a physiologic exposure. It is unlikely to survive sparse selection when direct renal function markers are available.

Later glucose measurement may indicate ongoing instability or insulin titration, but it is still indirect and protocol-driven. Proximity helps slightly, yet it remains low-value versus direct kidney function measures.

Whether CK was checked early is mostly driven by clinician suspicion (e.g., ischemia/rhabdo) rather than kidney injury itself. As a measurement proxy in a noisy immediate post-op window, it is low priority.
CK measurement may reflect concern for muscle injury/rhabdomyolysis which can contribute to AKI, but the feature is only an ordering proxy. Without CK values, it is a weak and indirect predictor.

Later CK measurement could signal evolving rhabdomyolysis/ischemia workup, indirectly related to AKI risk. Still, it is dominated by direct renal function markers and remains a low-priority sparse feature.

Mean eGFR summarizes early post-op renal function derived from creatinine, but this window is prone to dilutional/CPB-related noise. Useful but less reliable and less proximal than later eGFR summaries.

Mean eGFR in 13–24h captures early trajectory of renal function after initial post-op transients settle. It is a strong direct renal signal likely to add predictive value for Hour 60 creatinine_ratio.

Mean eGFR at 25–36h is the closest direct renal-function summary to the Hour 60 target and reflects evolving AKI. This is a top sparse-model candidate because it is near-directly linked to future creatinine changes.
Maximum eGFR largely reflects the lowest creatinine and can be inflated by hemodilution early after surgery. It is less informative for impending AKI than later eGFR and is redundant with eGFR_mean.

Max eGFR in this window captures best (lowest creatinine) rather than worst renal function, making it less sensitive to AKI evolution. It is also redundant with eGFR_mean and generally lower yield for sparse selection.

Later max eGFR may reflect recovery potential or absence of sustained dysfunction, but it still emphasizes the ‘best’ value rather than renal insult. Some complementary information to eGFR_mean, yet typically secondary in a sparse model.
Lowest early post-op eGFR reflects early renal dysfunction but can be confounded by immediate CPB/anesthesia fluid shifts. Still a strong direct renal signal, though less reliable than later windows.
Minimum eGFR in 13–24h captures evolving AKI after initial post-op noise settles. Direct kidney-function marker with good sparse-model value, but slightly dominated by the closer 25–36h window.

Minimum eGFR in 25–36h is the closest direct renal-function signal to the Hour-60 creatinine ratio and captures acute deterioration via an extreme. Highest-priority keeper in this batch for sparse selection.
Count/frequency of eGFR measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-concern proxy rather than physiology. Likely redundant with actual eGFR values and weak for sparse inclusion.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity and suspicion of AKI more than kidney function itself. Adds little unique predictive value beyond eGFR_min.
Later measurement frequency may weakly encode evolving instability/AKI workup closer to the outcome window. Still largely a behavior proxy and usually dominated by eGFR_min.
LDH measurement frequency is a nonspecific monitoring proxy (hemolysis/ischemia workup) rather than a renal biomarker. Unlikely to survive sparse screening.
LDH measured count mainly reflects clinician behavior and overall complexity. Provides minimal unique information for predicting creatinine ratio.
Later LDH measurement frequency remains a proxy for concern/complexity without direct renal signal. Low priority compared with direct kidney-function features.
Average INR early post-op reflects coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction/warfarin effect and overall illness severity. Indirect for AKI and likely less informative than later or extreme INR values.

Mean INR in 25–36h may capture sustained coagulopathy/organ dysfunction closer to the outcome window, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and somewhat redundant with INR_max for capturing acute derangements.
Peak INR in 0–12h captures early coagulopathy/bleeding risk and severity but is often transient post-CPB. Indirect renal relevance and less useful than later-window INR extremes.

Peak INR in 25–36h reflects significant ongoing coagulopathy/organ dysfunction and may track complications (bleeding/transfusion/hemodynamic stress) linked to AKI. Useful as a severity marker but not a direct renal signal.
Minimum INR early post-op is rarely pathophysiologically informative for AKI and may reflect baseline anticoagulation status. Dominated by INR_max/mean if INR is used at all.
Minimum INR in 25–36h is not a meaningful marker of renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Redundant and low yield for sparse selection.
INR measurement frequency is primarily a monitoring/bleeding-management proxy. Adds little unique predictive value beyond actual INR levels and other physiologic features.

Counts whether INR was checked in 13–24h, reflecting monitoring intensity/bleeding concern rather than renal physiology. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse AKI model.

Whether INR was checked in 25–36h; later timing may correlate with ongoing instability, hepatic dysfunction, or anticoagulation management. Still primarily a monitoring/complexity proxy, so low priority but slightly more informative due to proximity to Hour 60.

Average eosinophil percentage early post-op, often influenced by stress response and dilution rather than kidney injury. Early window and weak mechanistic link to AKI make it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Mean eosinophil percentage in 13–24h; may reflect inflammation/steroid exposure but is nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion/AKI. Likely redundant noise relative to stronger hemodynamic/renal markers not in this batch.

Peak eosinophil percentage in 0–12h; extremes here are still usually nonspecific and can be artifact of early post-bypass physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection for predicting creatinine ratio.

Maximum eosinophil percentage in 13–24h could weakly signal drug reaction/atheroembolic phenomena, which can affect kidneys. Still rare/nonspecific and generally dominated by more direct renal perfusion/function features, so low priority.
Minimum eosinophil percentage in 0–12h mainly captures stress-related eosinopenia and lab variability. Early timing and minimal specificity for AKI make it noncompetitive for sparse inclusion.

Minimum eosinophil percentage in 13–24h reflects eosinopenia from stress/catecholamines and is not a direct renal signal. Adds little unique predictive value and is redundant with overall illness severity.
Indicates whether eosinophils were measured in 0–12h, essentially a CBC differential ordering/availability proxy. Monitoring frequency is not a direct biomarker and is low value for sparse modeling.
Whether eosinophils were measured in 13–24h; reflects lab ordering patterns rather than physiology. Low unique predictive value for creatinine trajectory.
Whether eosinophils were measured in 25–36h, again a monitoring proxy. Even with closer timing, it is unlikely to add signal beyond true physiologic variables.

Whether LDL was checked in 0–12h, typically unrelated to acute post-op renal dysfunction and driven by baseline risk assessment or routine panels. Pure clinician-behavior proxy with negligible sparse-model value.
LDL measurement flag in 13–24h reflects ordering habits and chronic risk profiling, not evolving AKI. Not expected to contribute unique predictive information for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
LDL measurement flag in 25–36h remains a non-renal, non-acute lab ordering proxy. Very unlikely to survive feature selection for postoperative creatinine change.

Mean MCV in 0–12h reflects baseline anemia/macrocytosis, transfusion effects, or dilution; it is at best an indirect frailty/nutrition marker. Early timing and weak mechanistic link to AKI keep it low priority.
