Mean MCV in 13–24h may capture transfusion/hemodilution trajectory and chronic comorbidity burden, which can correlate weakly with complications. Still indirect and likely dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers, so low-to-modest priority.

Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) reflects RBC size and is mainly a chronic hematologic/nutrition signal rather than renal physiology. The 25–36h mean is the best-timed version but still low unique value for AKI prediction and likely redundant/noisy.

Peak MCV in the immediate 0–12h window is unlikely to reflect evolving kidney dysfunction and may be influenced by perioperative dilution/transfusion effects. Low priority and dominated by more proximal windows if any MCV signal exists.
Maximum MCV at 13–24h is a nonspecific hematologic marker with weak mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise. Adds little unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Maximum MCV at 25–36h is the most temporally proximal extreme, but MCV extremes rarely capture renal hypoperfusion/AKI risk directly. Could weakly proxy transfusion/hemolysis but is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Minimum MCV in 0–12h is largely perioperative noise and reflects hematologic shifts rather than kidney injury. Very weak candidate for inclusion.
Minimum MCV at 13–24h is nonspecific and not a direct precursor of AKI compared with hemodynamics, urine output, or renal labs. Low incremental value for sparse selection.

Minimum MCV at 25–36h is the closest window and could weakly reflect ongoing dilution/transfusion patterns, but remains indirect for renal dysfunction. Kept slightly higher than earlier epochs yet still low priority.
Count of MCV measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/ordering proxy rather than physiology. In 0–12h it is especially routine post-op and unlikely to add unique predictive signal.
MCV measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects lab ordering patterns and illness complexity more than kidney-specific risk. Low value and likely redundant with other measured-frequency features.

MCV measurement frequency at 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing concern/complications, but it is still a clinician-behavior signal rather than a renal biomarker. Slightly higher due to temporal proximity but not a strong sparse-model keeper.

Mean platelet volume (MPV) can reflect platelet activation/inflammation and post-bypass effects, which are only indirectly related to AKI. Early 0–12h mean is noisy and unlikely to be selected over direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Average MPV at 13–24h may capture ongoing inflammatory/consumptive physiology but remains nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion. Moderate-low priority and likely replaceable by stronger severity/renal markers.

Mean MPV at 25–36h is the most proximal sustained platelet-activation signal and may correlate with systemic inflammation/microvascular dysfunction relevant to AKI. Still indirect, but this is the best MPV aggregation for sparse selection in this batch.
Peak MPV in 0–12h may reflect acute platelet activation from surgery/CPB but is temporally early and often transient. Adds limited unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Maximum MPV at 13–24h captures extremes of platelet activation but remains a nonspecific illness/inflammation marker. Likely redundant with the 25–36h MPV signal and not top-tier for sparse inclusion.

Maximum MPV at 25–36h captures late extremes of platelet activation/inflammation, which may better track evolving organ dysfunction risk. It is still indirect for AKI but is the strongest MPV extreme in the most relevant window.

Minimum mean platelet volume (MPV) in the immediate post-op window; MPV is a nonspecific inflammation/platelet activation marker. Weak and indirect for AKI prediction and likely dominated by hemodynamics/lactate/renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.
Minimum MPV during 13–24h reflects nonspecific platelet/inflammatory changes. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Minimum MPV closer to the outcome window may weakly track ongoing systemic stress/inflammation. However it remains a low-specificity signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection unless few stronger predictors are available.
Count/frequency of MPV measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Not a physiologic renal signal and should be deprioritized in sparse modeling.
MPV measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects lab ordering patterns rather than kidney physiology. Adds little unique predictive value beyond illness severity proxies.
MPV measurement frequency in 25–36h is still mainly a proxy for care intensity. Unlikely to contribute stable, transportable signal for creatinine ratio prediction.
Average lactate in 0–12h reflects global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output after surgery, a plausible precursor to AKI. Mean smooths peaks and early post-op lactate can be noisy, so moderate priority.
Mean lactate in 13–24h captures sustained tissue hypoperfusion beyond immediate bypass effects. This is a strong, relatively proximal severity/perfusion signal that can add unique value for AKI risk.

Peak lactate in 0–12h captures acute hypoperfusion/shock burden, which can precipitate renal injury. Despite early-window noise, the extreme is more informative than the mean and may survive sparse selection.

Peak lactate in 13–24h is a high-salience marker of ongoing/worsening hypoperfusion and is strongly linked to subsequent AKI. As an extreme in a more informative window, it is a top sparse-model candidate in this batch.
Minimum lactate in 0–12h largely reflects periods of recovery/normalization and is less tied to renal insult than peaks. Redundant once max/mean lactate are available.
Minimum lactate in 13–24h may indicate clearance/recovery but is generally less predictive of AKI than sustained elevation or peaks. Likely redundant with lactate mean/max for sparse selection.
Lactate measurement frequency is a proxy for clinician concern and instability rather than a direct biomarker. Could weakly add severity information, but generally dominated by actual lactate values.
More frequent lactate checks in 13–24h suggests ongoing hemodynamic concern. Still a behavior/monitoring proxy and usually inferior to lactate max/mean for sparse models.
Lactate measurement frequency closer to the outcome may reflect persistent instability prompting monitoring. However it remains an indirect proxy and is unlikely to outperform lactate level features.
Mean alkaline phosphatase early post-op reflects hepatobiliary/bone sources and is not a direct renal perfusion or filtration marker. Likely low signal-to-noise and low priority for sparse selection.

Peak alkaline phosphatase early post-op reflects hepatobiliary/cholestatic signal rather than renal function. In a sparse AKI model it is indirect and likely noise/redundant with stronger perfusion/inflammation markers.
Minimum alkaline phosphatase in the first 12h has little physiologic linkage to evolving AKI. Low priority and dominated by more kidney-proximal features.
Count of ALP measurements is mainly a monitoring/ordering-intensity proxy. It adds little unique physiologic information for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
ALP measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior/complexity rather than renal injury. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct renal/hemodynamic variables.
Even though closer to the outcome window, ALP measurement count remains a weak proxy signal. Low unique value for AKI prediction compared with true renal/perfusion markers.

Mean RDW is a nonspecific marker of chronic illness/inflammation and perioperative transfusion effects. Early (0–12h) values are less informative for 60h AKI and are redundant with later RDW summaries.
RDW mean in 13–24h captures general physiologic stress/anemia rather than kidney-specific injury. Modest, indirect predictive value and likely dominated by the later (25–36h) window.

Later RDW mean may reflect sustained inflammation/hemolysis/transfusion burden that correlates with AKI risk. Still indirect, but this is the most temporally relevant RDW summary and could add modest unique signal.
Max RDW early post-op is largely driven by baseline anisocytosis and transfusion-related shifts, not renal physiology. Redundant with later RDW extremes and weak for sparse selection.
RDW max in 13–24h is a nonspecific severity marker and not kidney-proximal. Some association with outcomes is possible, but it is replaceable and likely collinear with RDW mean/max later.

Peak RDW in 25–36h may better capture ongoing transfusion/hemolysis/inflammatory stress closer to the 60h creatinine measurement. Indirect but potentially the strongest RDW aggregation in this batch, so moderate priority.
Minimum RDW is not a meaningful insult marker and is unlikely to relate to AKI trajectory. Clearly dominated by later RDW summaries and other physiologic predictors.
RDW minimum in 13–24h provides little interpretable signal and is typically redundant with mean/max. Very low sparse-model value.
Even in the closest window, RDW minimum is not a plausible driver or marker of renal hypoperfusion/injury. Low priority and dominated by RDW max/mean if RDW is used at all.
RDW measurement count is essentially CBC ordering frequency and reflects monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Weak proxy and not a good sparse-model feature.
CBC/RDW measurement frequency in 13–24h is clinician-behavior driven and adds minimal unique information. Low likelihood of surviving sparse selection.

Counts how often RDW was checked in the 25–36h window, reflecting monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy it is unlikely to add unique value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.

Average chloride early post-op reflects fluid/acid-base management (e.g., saline load) but is noisy immediately after bypass. Likely redundant with later chloride summaries that are closer to Hour 60.
Mean chloride at 13–24h captures ongoing hyperchloremia exposure but smooths extremes and is not the closest window. Adds limited unique signal compared with 25–36h chloride features.

Mean chloride at 25–36h reflects sustained hyperchloremia/acid-base status near the prediction horizon, which can correlate with AKI risk and resuscitation burden. Still somewhat redundant with chloride extremes, so moderate priority.
Peak chloride in the immediate 0–12h period may indicate large chloride-rich fluid exposure but is prone to transient post-op shifts. Lower priority given timing and overlap with later peaks.

Peak chloride at 13–24h better reflects clinically meaningful hyperchloremia after initial stabilization and may relate to renal vasoconstriction/AKI risk. Some redundancy with 25–36h peak chloride keeps it from top-tier.

Peak chloride in the 25–36h window is the most temporally proximate extreme and best captures significant hyperchloremia from ongoing resuscitation/acid-base derangements. Among chloride features, this is most likely to survive sparse selection.
Lowest chloride early post-op is often driven by dilutional effects and rapid peri-bypass shifts rather than evolving kidney dysfunction. Weak and noisy signal for Hour 60 creatinine_ratio.
Minimum chloride at 13–24h may reflect dilution or free-water balance but is an indirect and less plausible AKI precursor than hyperchloremia extremes. Likely dominated by chloride_max/mean features.

Minimum chloride at 25–36h is closer to the outcome but still an indirect marker and typically less informative than peak chloride for renal risk. Kept low due to redundancy and weaker pathophysiologic linkage.
Number of chloride measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects ICU workflow/acuity and routine post-op labs. As a monitoring proxy it offers little unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency of chloride at 13–24h is primarily clinician behavior and care intensity rather than a biomarker. Unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model.
More frequent chloride checks at 25–36h may weakly proxy instability or acid-base management closer to the outcome. Still largely a monitoring-intensity signal, so low priority.
Whether HDL was measured early post-op reflects ordering patterns (often non-ICU routine panels) rather than renal physiology. Very unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
HDL measurement at 13–24h is a clinician/workflow proxy with minimal mechanistic link to acute creatinine rise. Not a sparse-model keeper.
HDL measurement at 25–36h remains a low-information ordering proxy and not a direct AKI signal. Should be excluded under a limited feature budget.

Mean neutrophil percentage reflects early postoperative inflammatory/stress response. It is an indirect, nonspecific severity marker and likely redundant with later values and other inflammation markers, so low sparse-model priority.

Mean neutrophil percentage in 13–24h captures ongoing inflammation/infection risk. Still indirect for AKI and typically dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Peak neutrophil percentage early post-op may reflect acute stress or early infection signal. It is nonspecific and early-window noise is common, so only weak incremental value for creatinine ratio.

A higher peak neutrophil percentage at 13–24h may better indicate evolving systemic inflammation/sepsis that can contribute to AKI. Among neutrophil summaries, the max is the most plausible signal, but it remains indirect and likely replaceable.

Minimum neutrophil percentage is rarely a meaningful renal-risk signal in this context and can reflect dilution/measurement variability. It is dominated by other aggregations and has minimal sparse-model value.
Minimum neutrophil percentage in 13–24h is not a strong mechanistic precursor of AKI and is hard to interpret clinically. Redundant/noisy compared with max/mean, so very low priority.
Count of measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op labs are routine, so it adds little unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity and suspicion rather than a direct biomarker. It is unlikely to add stable, unique signal beyond actual lab values.

Later measurement frequency (25–36h) may weakly proxy clinical concern for infection/instability closer to the outcome window. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by physiologic features, so modest at best.

Mean albumin reflects oncotic status, inflammation, and dilution from fluids/transfusion, which relate to AKI risk and fluid balance. However it is early and can be confounded by perioperative hemodilution, limiting sparse-model priority.
Maximum albumin in 0–12h is less informative than low values for capillary leak/dilution and is sensitive to timing of draws. Likely redundant with mean/min and not a strong AKI precursor.

Minimum albumin early post-op captures worst hemodilution/capillary leak and low oncotic pressure, which correlate with illness severity, fluid overload, and AKI risk. Within the albumin family, the min best preserves the pathologic signal, making it the strongest keeper here despite early timing.
Albumin measurement count is mainly a clinician-ordering/monitoring proxy and often routine post-op. It provides little unique physiologic information for a sparse model.
Measurement frequency is a weak proxy for complexity rather than renal physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection compared with actual albumin values and other direct AKI predictors.
Later albumin measurement frequency may reflect ongoing instability or nutritional/volume management closer to Hour 60. Still largely a behavior proxy, so only modest incremental value.

Whether triglycerides were measured is almost entirely an ordering-practice proxy (e.g., propofol monitoring) and not a direct renal-risk biomarker. Very unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Whether triglycerides were checked in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring/TPN/pancreatitis workup proxy rather than a renal physiology signal. Unlikely to add unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Whether triglycerides were checked in 25–36h reflects clinician behavior/complexity (e.g., nutrition or propofol monitoring) more than AKI trajectory. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is still a weak, indirect proxy.

Average hemoglobin in the immediate post-op window reflects hemodilution/bleeding/transfusion and oxygen delivery but is noisy early after bypass. Likely redundant with later hemoglobin and with min/max summaries that better capture clinically relevant extremes.

Mean hemoglobin at 13–24h captures overall anemia burden but smooths short low-Hb episodes that may matter for renal hypoxia. Some predictive value, but typically dominated by hemoglobin_min and by the later (25–36h) window.

Mean hemoglobin at 25–36h reflects sustained anemia closer to the outcome window and may relate to renal oxygen delivery and ongoing bleeding/transfusion needs. Still somewhat redundant with hemoglobin_min, which better captures the insult signal for sparse selection.

Peak hemoglobin in 0–12h is often driven by transfusion/hemoconcentration and is less directly tied to AKI risk than low hemoglobin. Early timing and limited mechanistic link make it a low-priority sparse feature.

Maximum hemoglobin in 13–24h is a weak and indirect marker (often reflecting transfusion or diuresis) and not a primary driver of creatinine rise. Redundant with other hemoglobin summaries and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Maximum hemoglobin in 25–36h mainly reflects treatment effects/volume status rather than renal hypoperfusion or injury. Compared with hemoglobin_min/mean, it adds little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Lowest hemoglobin in 0–12h can capture acute bleeding/hemodilution and reduced oxygen delivery, which can contribute to renal injury. However, immediate post-op values are noisy and may be less predictive than later minima.

Lowest hemoglobin in 13–24h better reflects persistent or clinically meaningful anemia after initial bypass-related shifts. As an extreme, it preserves the insult signal and is a strong sparse-model candidate for AKI risk.

Lowest hemoglobin in 25–36h is the closest extreme anemia signal to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and can reflect ongoing bleeding, inadequate oxygen delivery, or high illness severity. Among hemoglobin variants, this is the most likely to add unique predictive value and survive sparse selection.

Frequency of hemoglobin checks in 0–12h is largely routine post-cardiac surgery monitoring and a clinician-behavior proxy. It provides little direct physiologic information and is dominated by actual hemoglobin values.

Hemoglobin measurement count in 13–24h mainly reflects monitoring intensity/complexity rather than kidney injury biology. Low incremental value compared with hemoglobin minima/means in a sparse model.

More frequent hemoglobin checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing bleeding/transfusion concern and higher acuity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by hemoglobin_min and other direct physiologic features.

Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was measured early reflects bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and clinician behavior. It is not a direct renal signal and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

TEG fibrinogen activity measurement in 13–24h is a proxy for ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy workup rather than kidney function. Compared with direct hemodynamic/renal markers, it is low priority for sparse selection.

Indicates whether TEG fibrinogen activity was checked (monitoring/bleeding-workup intensity) rather than a renal physiology signal. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy with weak, indirect linkage to AKI risk, so low sparse-model priority.

Average ACT in the immediate post-op window reflects anticoagulation/heparin reversal and bypass-related management. It is early, indirect for renal injury, and dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Peak ACT may capture extreme anticoagulation/prolonged bypass/heparin effect, which can correlate with operative complexity and downstream AKI risk. Still indirect and early (0–12h), so only a weak-to-modest sparse candidate.

Minimum ACT mainly reflects periods of lower anticoagulation after reversal and is not a clear mechanistic driver of AKI. Redundant within the ACT family and weakly connected to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Number/frequency of ACT measurements is primarily a proxy for intra/post-op monitoring intensity and procedural workflow. As a clinician-behavior feature with high confounding and low physiologic specificity, it has low sparse-model value.

ACT measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects ongoing anticoagulation monitoring (e.g., bleeding/ECMO concerns) rather than kidney function. Still a proxy variable and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Later ACT measurement frequency (25–36h) may flag complicated courses (re-exploration, mechanical support) that increase AKI risk. However it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy, so only modest priority despite better timing.

Mean AST early post-op reflects hepatic/ischemic injury burden and overall hypoperfusion severity. It is indirect for renal dysfunction and early-window noise is common, so limited sparse-model priority.

Peak AST in 0–12h can capture ischemic hepatitis/shock or major operative insult, which often co-tracks with renal hypoperfusion and later AKI. Among AST summaries, the extreme is most informative, but timing is still early so not top-tier.
Minimum AST is not clinically meaningful for organ injury and mostly reflects baseline/measurement noise. It is dominated by AST max as the relevant insult signal.

AST measurement frequency is largely a proxy for clinician concern/monitoring rather than a biomarker of renal trajectory. High confounding and redundancy with actual AST values make it a poor sparse-model feature.
Frequency of AST checks in 13–24h reflects care intensity (e.g., concern for liver injury/hemolysis) more than kidney physiology. As a proxy variable, it is unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Later AST measurement frequency may indicate ongoing instability or complications that correlate with AKI risk, and it is closer to the 60h target. Still primarily a monitoring proxy, so only modest importance.

Mean total protein reflects hemodilution, capillary leak, and nutritional/inflammatory status, which can relate to fluid balance and renal perfusion. However it is nonspecific and early, so only a weak-to-moderate sparse candidate.

Maximum total protein in 0–12h is rarely a meaningful renal-risk signal and can be driven by lab/volume status variability. It is less informative than low protein (hemodilution) for post-op physiology, so low priority.

Minimum total protein early post-op can capture marked hemodilution/capillary leak and large-volume resuscitation, which are associated with AKI risk via congestion and reduced effective perfusion. Within the total-protein family, the low extreme is the most informative, though still indirect and early.
Count/frequency of total protein checks in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It has little direct renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of total protein in 13–24h reflects testing patterns rather than kidney physiology. Adds minimal unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Later (25–36h) testing frequency may weakly proxy evolving complications prompting labs, but remains indirect. Still low priority versus physiologic/lab extremes directly tied to AKI.

Whether HbA1c was measured early post-op is a documentation/testing artifact (chronic diabetes assessment), not an acute renal trajectory marker. Highly redundant with known comorbidity data and low sparse-model value.
HbA1c measurement flag in 13–24h is a clinician-ordering proxy and does not capture acute kidney stress. Unlikely to add unique predictive information for 60h creatinine ratio.
HbA1c measurement in 25–36h remains a testing-pattern indicator rather than a physiologic AKI precursor. Very low priority for sparse selection.
Mean serum CO2 (bicarbonate) reflects acid–base status but is averaged over an early, noisy post-bypass window. Likely weaker and more redundant than later or extreme CO2 values for AKI prediction.
Sustained lower bicarbonate in 13–24h can reflect ongoing metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/early AKI. Mean is still somewhat smoothing/redundant, but this window is more informative than 0–12h.

Mean CO2 in 25–36h captures persistent metabolic acidosis closer to the 60h outcome, which can track evolving renal dysfunction and shock. Stronger sparse-model candidate due to timing and sustained exposure signal.
Peak CO2 early post-op often reflects transient ventilatory/alkalosis management rather than kidney injury. Early timing and limited mechanistic link make it low priority.
Higher CO2 maxima are generally less informative for AKI than low minima (acidosis) and can be driven by ventilation/diuretics. Redundant and weak for sparse selection.
Later CO2 maxima may reflect contraction alkalosis/ventilation effects and is not a direct AKI signal. Kept low because minima/mean in this window would dominate acid–base information.

Low CO2 minimum captures acute metabolic acidosis episodes that can accompany hypoperfusion and predict AKI, but early post-op values can be transient/noisy. Moderate priority, dominated by later minima if available.

CO2 minimum in 13–24h captures clinically important acidosis extremes after initial post-op stabilization, aligning with hypoperfusion/early AKI physiology. Strong sparse-model candidate and more informative than mean/max.

CO2 minimum in 25–36h is the closest extreme acid–base marker to the 60h creatinine outcome and can reflect ongoing shock/renal acid retention. High unique predictive value and likely to survive sparse selection among CO2 aggregations.

Frequency of CO2 measurements in 0–12h is mostly a monitoring/acuity proxy (more ABGs/BMPs), not a biomarker itself. May weakly indicate instability but is redundant with actual CO2 values and other severity features.

Count/frequency of CO2 (chemistry) measurements in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Weak, indirect link to AKI and likely dominated by physiologic/hemodynamic and renal markers.

Measurement frequency of CO2 in 25–36h may reflect evolving metabolic issues prompting closer monitoring. Still largely a proxy variable and unlikely to survive sparse selection unless few other severity markers exist.

Whether BNP was checked in 0–12h reflects clinician concern for cardiac dysfunction/volume status rather than kidney injury itself. Early post-op ordering behavior is noisy and not a strong unique predictor of 60h creatinine ratio.

BNP measurement in 13–24h can indicate concern for heart failure/venous congestion, which relates to renal dysfunction. However it is still an ordering proxy (not the BNP value) and is likely redundant with direct hemodynamics/diuretic/fluids features.

BNP checked in 25–36h may signal persistent congestion or low-output concerns closer to the outcome window. As a binary 'measured' feature it remains indirect and typically dominated by actual vitals/pressors/urine output and lab values.
Mean PaCO2 in 0–12h reflects ventilation/anesthesia effects and immediate post-bypass physiology. Early averages are noisy and only indirectly related to AKI risk, so low sparse-model priority.

Mean PaCO2 in 13–24h captures sustained ventilatory status and may correlate with severity of illness. Still an indirect renal signal and likely redundant with stronger perfusion/acid-base markers (e.g., lactate, pH, vasopressors) if available.

Mean PaCO2 in 25–36h is temporally closer and could reflect ongoing respiratory failure/sedation affecting hemodynamics. However it remains a weak, indirect predictor of creatinine rise and is usually not a top sparse feature.

Peak PaCO2 in 0–12h often reflects transient hypoventilation immediately post-op. Early extremes are frequently procedural/ventilator-related noise and not uniquely predictive of 60h creatinine ratio.

Max PaCO2 in 13–24h may capture episodes of hypoventilation/hypercapnia associated with instability. It is still an indirect AKI signal and likely secondary to more direct perfusion/congestion variables.

Max PaCO2 in 25–36h captures late hypercapnic episodes closer to the outcome and may reflect significant respiratory failure with hemodynamic consequences. Among PaCO2 summaries, this extreme is more likely to add signal than means/mins, though still indirect for AKI.
Minimum PaCO2 in 0–12h often reflects hyperventilation/ventilator settings during immediate post-op management. This is largely iatrogenic and weakly connected to renal outcomes.

Minimum PaCO2 in 13–24h is typically driven by ventilator management rather than kidney pathophysiology. Provides little unique predictive value versus other PaCO2 aggregates and broader severity markers.
Minimum PaCO2 in 25–36h reflects episodes of hyperventilation and is not a direct renal hypoperfusion marker. Likely redundant and low yield for sparse selection.
Frequency of PaCO2 measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects routine post-op ABG monitoring and ventilation management. Strongly proxy-like and noisy early, so minimal sparse-model value.

PaCO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h may indicate ongoing respiratory/acid-base instability requiring repeated ABGs. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy and generally dominated by actual physiologic values and renal-relevant features.

Count/frequency of pCO2 measurements in 25–36h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ventilation-management proxy. It is indirect for AKI and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model versus true perfusion/renal markers.

Whether indirect bilirubin was checked in 0–12h reflects clinician behavior/concern rather than a renal physiology signal. Early post-op timing is noisy and this is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement indicator for indirect bilirubin in 13–24h is still primarily a monitoring proxy (hemolysis/liver workup) rather than kidney injury. Adds little unique predictive information for creatinine ratio.

Indirect bilirubin being measured at 25–36h may proxy evolving hemolysis/hepatic dysfunction and overall complexity closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and behavior-driven, so only a weak sparse-model candidate.
Average calcium in 0–12h is influenced by bypass, transfusion/citrate, and replacement protocols, making it noisy early post-op. Mean is also dominated by extremes and is not a direct renal signal.

Mean calcium in 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness and replacement needs, but it is an indirect correlate of AKI risk. Likely redundant with more informative extremes (min/max) and later window values.

Mean calcium in 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may capture sustained derangements, but calcium is still a weak/indirect predictor of creatinine rise. In sparse selection it is usually dominated by calcium_min (hypocalcemia) or other renal/perfusion features.
Peak calcium in 0–12h often reflects supplementation and peri-bypass shifts rather than kidney dysfunction. Early timing and treatment-driven nature make it low-yield for sparse prediction.

Peak calcium in 13–24h can indicate aggressive replacement or metabolic disturbance, but it is not a direct AKI precursor. Likely redundant and less informative than hypocalcemia (min) or later-window signals.

Peak calcium in 25–36h is temporally closer and may reflect ongoing supplementation/metabolic issues, but remains indirect for renal injury. Typically not a top sparse-model keeper compared with calcium_min or hemodynamic/renal markers.

Minimum calcium in 0–12h captures hypocalcemia from citrate load/transfusion and early instability, which can correlate with severity. However early post-op noise and protocolized replacement limit unique predictive value for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum calcium in 13–24h may better reflect persistent hypocalcemia and ongoing critical illness/organ dysfunction, which can track with AKI risk. Among calcium summaries, the minimum is the most plausible sparse-model candidate in this mid window.

Minimum calcium in 25–36h is the closest window and an extreme that can capture ongoing metabolic derangement and severity associated with evolving AKI. Within this batch it is the strongest calcium-derived signal and most likely to survive sparse selection.

Number/frequency of calcium measurements in 0–12h is largely a monitoring/protocol proxy (post-op labs, transfusion, replacement). It is not a physiologic renal marker and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Calcium measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects care intensity and lab ordering patterns more than kidney function. Generally redundant with actual calcium values and weak for sparse selection.

Calcium measurement frequency in 25–36h may proxy ongoing instability/repletion needs closer to the outcome. Still primarily clinician-behavior driven and typically dominated by calcium_min/max themselves.

Early post-op mean PTT reflects anticoagulation/coagulopathy and heparin exposure more than renal physiology. Likely redundant with later PTT and with extremes; limited unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean PTT at 25–36h captures ongoing anticoagulation intensity/bleeding risk and overall illness trajectory closer to the outcome. Still an indirect renal signal and often dominated by extreme PTT or hemodynamic/renal markers, so moderate sparse priority.

Peak PTT in the first 12h often reflects intra/post-op heparin effect or early coagulopathy. It is temporally distant and noisy for AKI prediction, and largely a treatment/management proxy rather than a direct kidney insult marker.

Late peak PTT may indicate supratherapeutic anticoagulation, hepatic dysfunction, or ongoing critical illness nearer to Hour 60. It can add some unique severity/complication signal versus the mean, but remains indirect for renal dysfunction.
Low PTT early post-op is usually uninformative (often normal) and not a plausible driver of AKI. Strongly dominated by later values and by high/extreme abnormalities if present.
Minimum PTT at 25–36h is typically near-normal and provides little incremental information beyond mean/max. Weak and redundant for sparse selection.

Count of PTT measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-concern proxy in the immediate post-op period. High risk of confounding by care patterns and low direct physiologic specificity for AKI.
Measurement frequency reflects anticoagulation management complexity and surveillance rather than kidney function. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when physiologic variables are available.
Later PTT measurement frequency may proxy ongoing instability (bleeding, ECMO/IABP, heparin titration) closer to the outcome. Still primarily a behavior/monitoring signal, so only modest priority.

Early mean monocyte count reflects inflammatory response/stress leukogram but is nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Likely noisy post-bypass and dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Mean monocyte count at 13–24h may capture sustained inflammation/infection risk that can correlate with AKI. However it is indirect and typically weaker than other inflammatory markers (e.g., WBC, lactate) for sparse selection.
Peak monocyte count in the first 12h is a nonspecific inflammatory signal and often reflects perioperative stress. Low unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

A higher monocyte peak at 13–24h could indicate evolving inflammation or infection, which can contribute to AKI risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with broader leukocyte/infection severity signals.
Minimum monocyte count early post-op is rarely mechanistically linked to AKI and is often within normal variation. Redundant and low signal-to-noise.
Minimum monocyte count at 13–24h provides little interpretable renal-risk information and is dominated by other inflammatory/clinical severity measures. Weak candidate for sparse inclusion.
Measurement count is a proxy for lab ordering frequency rather than a physiologic biomarker. In the early window it mainly reflects routine CBC cadence and adds minimal unique predictive value.

Count of how often absolute monocytes were checked (monitoring intensity) in 13–24h. This is largely a clinician-behavior proxy with weak direct linkage to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Frequency of absolute monocyte measurements in 25–36h. Despite closer timing, it remains a monitoring proxy rather than a renal physiology signal, so low priority.

Average serum phosphorus in the immediate 0–12h post-op window. Phosphorus can rise with reduced renal clearance, but early post-bypass shifts and dilution make this a weaker, noisier predictor than later/extreme summaries.

Mean phosphorus at 13–24h, reflecting early trajectory of renal clearance and catabolism. Moderately useful, but likely dominated by later (25–36h) and/or extreme (max) phosphorus signals for sparse selection.

Mean phosphorus at 25–36h, closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and more reflective of evolving renal dysfunction. Still somewhat redundant with phosphorus extremes (max) which better capture acute impaired clearance.

Peak phosphorus in 0–12h, which can indicate early impaired renal excretion or cell breakdown. Extremes are preferable to means, but this early window is prone to transient perioperative effects, limiting sparse-model priority.
Peak phosphorus in 13–24h, a stronger marker of reduced renal clearance and evolving AKI than the mean. Good sparse candidate, though still slightly less proximal than 25–36h.

Peak phosphorus in 25–36h, the most proximal and extreme-based summary in this batch, capturing impaired renal clearance and catabolic stress preceding creatinine rise. High unique predictive value and likely to survive sparse selection among phosphorus features.

Lowest phosphorus in 0–12h, often reflecting dilution, insulin/glucose shifts, or refeeding rather than renal dysfunction. Low relevance to predicting later creatinine ratio and dominated by max/late values.
Minimum phosphorus in 13–24h. Hypophosphatemia is not a direct AKI signal and is less informative than phosphorus maxima for renal clearance, so low priority.
Minimum phosphorus in 25–36h. Even with better timing, low phosphorus is typically not a precursor of creatinine rise and is redundant/less pathophysiologic than peak phosphorus.
Number of phosphorus measurements in 0–12h, reflecting monitoring intensity and care patterns. This is a weak, indirect proxy and not a physiologic renal marker.
Frequency of phosphorus checks in 13–24h. Primarily a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy with limited unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Frequency of phosphorus measurements in 25–36h. Slightly more informative as a proxy for concern/instability near the outcome, but still indirect and generally dominated by actual phosphorus values.

Mean absolute neutrophil count in 0–12h, reflecting inflammation/stress response after surgery. This is a nonspecific illness-severity marker and early post-op leukocytosis is common, so limited sparse-model value for AKI prediction.

Mean absolute neutrophil count in 13–24h, capturing ongoing inflammatory response that can correlate with complications. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and likely weaker than direct renal clearance markers (e.g., phosphorus max), so low-to-moderate priority.
Peak ANC in the immediate post-op window reflects inflammatory/stress response. It is an indirect AKI signal and early post-CPB leukocytosis is common, so it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Peak ANC at 13–24h may reflect ongoing inflammation/infection risk. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and largely a generic severity marker, so low priority in a sparse model.
Nadir ANC early post-op is usually not mechanistically tied to AKI and may reflect dilution/lab variability. Weak and likely noisy for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Nadir ANC at 13–24h is not a strong precursor of renal hypoperfusion/AKI. Adds little unique predictive value versus other physiologic signals.
Frequency of ANC measurement is mainly a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy. It is not a direct physiologic renal signal and is low value for sparse selection.
ANC measurement count reflects intensity of lab monitoring rather than kidney physiology. Likely redundant with overall acuity and should be deprioritized.

Later ANC measurement frequency may indicate ongoing concern for complications (infection/instability). Still a proxy variable, but closer timing to outcome gives it slightly more potential signal than earlier epochs.
Troponin measurement frequency early post-op reflects routine protocols and monitoring intensity. It is a weak proxy and not a direct renal-risk biomarker.
Troponin measurement count at 13–24h is largely clinician-driven and protocolized. Provides little unique information for AKI prediction in a sparse linear model.

Troponin measurement frequency at 25–36h may signal ongoing hemodynamic/cardiac concern that could secondarily affect renal perfusion. Still a proxy and inferior to actual troponin values or hemodynamic extremes, so modest priority.

Average PaO2 early post-op reflects oxygenation/ventilation status but is often influenced by high FiO2 and transient instability. Indirect for AKI and likely dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers.
Mean PaO2 at 13–24h captures sustained oxygenation but remains an indirect illness-severity signal. Mean aggregation may miss brief hypoxemic insults and is not a top sparse-model feature.

Mean PaO2 at 25–36h is closer to the 60h outcome and may reflect persistent respiratory failure affecting renal oxygen delivery and overall severity. Still indirect, but timing makes it the best of the PaO2 means.
Maximum PaO2 early post-op mostly reflects administered FiO2/ventilator strategy rather than physiology. It is a poor and potentially misleading proxy for renal risk.
Peak PaO2 is largely driven by oxygen supplementation and does not capture hypoxemic injury. Low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Maximum PaO2 at 25–36h remains primarily a treatment/ventilator-setting artifact rather than a renal-relevant physiologic insult. Unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Lowest arterial oxygen tension in the immediate post-op window; severe hypoxemia can contribute to global hypoperfusion risk. However it is an indirect AKI signal and early (0–12h) values are often transient/noisy, so lower sparse-model priority.
Minimum PaO2 in the 13–24h window, reflecting ongoing hypoxemia severity. Still an indirect illness-severity marker for AKI and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers not in this batch.

Minimum PaO2 closest to the prediction horizon; persistent or late hypoxemia may track evolving cardiopulmonary failure that can worsen renal perfusion. Among PaO2 summaries this is the most temporally relevant, but remains indirect for creatinine rise.

Count/frequency of PaO2 measurements in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and clinician concern. This is a proxy variable and early post-op practice patterns add noise, so very low priority.
Frequency of PaO2 checks in 13–24h, largely a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.
Frequency of PaO2 measurements in 25–36h may reflect ongoing respiratory instability/ICU complexity closer to Hour 60. Still primarily a monitoring proxy, so only modest value.

Average potassium in the immediate post-op period; abnormalities can reflect renal handling, transfusion/hemolysis, and acid-base shifts. Mean in 0–12h is often diluted by transient peri-bypass effects and is redundant with later/extreme potassium summaries.
Average potassium in 13–24h, which may begin to reflect renal dysfunction trajectory. Mean is still less sensitive than extremes and likely redundant with potassium_max/min in the same epoch.

Average potassium in 25–36h, closest window and may capture sustained impaired renal excretion or ongoing shifts. Still somewhat redundant with potassium_max (more insult-sensitive), but could add signal if dyskalemia is persistent.

Peak potassium in 0–12h; hyperkalemia can be an early marker of reduced renal clearance or major tissue injury. Early timing is noisier post-CPB, but the extreme is more informative than the mean/min in this window.

Peak potassium in 13–24h, a stronger indicator of evolving impaired renal excretion and severity than mean values. This extreme in a mid window is a good sparse-model candidate within the potassium family.

Peak potassium in 25–36h is the most temporally proximate and insult-sensitive potassium summary, often reflecting evolving AKI/low clearance near the outcome window. Highest priority within this batch due to strong physiologic linkage and reduced redundancy versus means.

Lowest potassium in 0–12h, often driven by diuresis, insulin therapy, alkalosis, or dilution rather than kidney injury per se. Early and less directly tied to creatinine rise than hyperkalemia, so lower priority.
Minimum potassium in 13–24h; hypokalemia is more treatment/shift-related and less specific for AKI than hyperkalemia. Likely redundant and weaker for sparse selection.
Minimum potassium in 25–36h, still more reflective of therapy/redistribution than renal failure. Adds limited unique value compared with potassium_max in the same window.

Frequency of potassium measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/acuity proxy and influenced by routine post-cardiac surgery protocols. Unlikely to contribute unique predictive signal for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Count/frequency of potassium checks in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-concern proxy rather than a renal physiology signal. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model versus actual potassium/creatinine/urine output features.

Frequency of potassium measurement in 25–36h may weakly reflect evolving AKI/arrhythmia risk prompting closer monitoring. Still largely a behavior proxy and typically dominated by physiologic variables, so low sparse-selection priority.

Early mean bilirubin reflects hepatic function/hemolysis after surgery but is indirect for kidney injury and noisy immediately post-op. Mean is also less informative than extremes and this early window is least proximal to Hour 60.

Peak bilirubin in 0–12h can capture acute hepatic hypoperfusion/hemolysis and overall shock burden, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it is indirect and early-postop, so only a weak-to-moderate sparse candidate.
Minimum bilirubin in 0–12h is not a meaningful insult marker and mostly reflects baseline/measurement noise. Provides little unique predictive value for later creatinine ratio.
Number of bilirubin measurements in 0–12h is a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy and not a direct biomarker. Early timing further reduces usefulness for Hour 60 creatinine prediction.
Measurement frequency of bilirubin in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than hepatic/renal physiology. Typically redundant with severity markers and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

More frequent bilirubin checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing concern for organ dysfunction, closer to the outcome window. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by actual lab values/hemodynamics.

Mean RBC count in 0–12h reflects anemia/hemodilution/transfusion effects, which can relate to renal hypoxia risk but is indirect. Early postoperative fluid shifts make it noisy and less likely to be selected sparsely.
Mean RBC count in 13–24h captures sustained anemia/hemodilution that may contribute to renal hypoxia. Still indirect and often redundant with hemoglobin/hematocrit or transfusion variables.

Mean RBC count in 25–36h is the most temporally relevant summary of ongoing anemia/hemodilution near the prediction horizon. Moderately useful as a sustained oxygen-delivery proxy, though still indirect for AKI.
Maximum RBC count in 0–12h is rarely a pathologic extreme relevant to AKI and may reflect transfusion timing or lab variability. Adds little unique predictive signal for creatinine ratio.
Peak RBC count in 13–24h is not a clear renal-insult marker and is typically driven by transfusion/measurement variation. Low likelihood of surviving sparse feature selection.
Maximum RBC count in 25–36h may weakly encode recent transfusion/hemoconcentration status, but higher RBC is not a direct AKI precursor. Likely redundant with mean/min RBC or transfusion variables.

Minimum RBC count in 0–12h captures nadir anemia/hemodilution, which can reflect bleeding/transfusion needs and reduced oxygen delivery linked to AKI risk. Despite early timing, the nadir is the most informative aggregation within this RBC family.

Minimum RBC count in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful nadir anemia after initial stabilization, a stronger and less noisy signal than early 0–12h. This extreme is the best RBC-derived candidate for sparse selection as an oxygen-delivery/bleeding severity proxy relevant to AKI risk.

Lowest RBC count in the 25–36h window reflects anemia/hemodilution or bleeding/transfusion burden, which can contribute to renal hypoxia. Moderately useful as a late physiologic severity signal, but indirect for AKI and often redundant with hemodynamics/transfusion variables not shown here.

Indicates whether/how often RBC count was checked in the first 12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and routine post-op labs. Weak direct renal signal and early timing is noisy, so low sparse-selection priority.

Measurement frequency of RBC count in 13–24h is largely a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Adds little unique value versus actual RBC values and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

RBC measurement frequency at 25–36h may reflect ongoing bleeding/anemia evaluation or instability closer to the outcome window. Still primarily a monitoring proxy and dominated by actual RBC level summaries, so only a weak candidate.

Whether D-dimer was measured early post-op reflects clinician concern for thrombosis/DIC rather than kidney function. Early window and proxy nature make it low value for sparse prediction of creatinine ratio.

D-dimer measurement in 13–24h is a workup/monitoring indicator (e.g., thrombosis/inflammation concern) more than a direct renal biomarker. Likely redundant with overall illness severity and not a strong sparse-model feature.

Late D-dimer measurement may mark evolving complications (thrombosis, DIC, inflammation) that can associate with AKI risk. Still a clinician-behavior proxy without the actual D-dimer value, so limited unique predictive value.

TEG ACT being measured in 0–12h mainly indicates perioperative bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and protocolized care. It is an indirect proxy and early post-op noise limits usefulness for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.
TEG ACT measurement in 13–24h reflects clinician concern for coagulopathy/bleeding rather than renal physiology. Without the ACT value, it is unlikely to add unique signal in a sparse linear model.

Late TEG ACT measurement can indicate ongoing bleeding/transfusion/coagulopathy issues that may correlate with renal hypoperfusion risk. However it remains a monitoring proxy and is weaker than physiologic extremes, so low-moderate priority.

Mean immature granulocyte percentage early post-op reflects inflammatory/stress response and possible infection risk. It is indirect for AKI and early values are often nonspecific after surgery, so limited sparse-model value.

Average immature granulocyte percentage in 13–24h may better reflect evolving inflammation than the immediate post-op period. Still a nonspecific severity marker and likely replaceable by stronger renal-perfusion or renal-function features.

Peak immature granulocyte percentage in 0–12h captures an extreme inflammatory/stress signal. Extremes are preferable to means, but early post-op inflammation is common and nonspecific, so only weak-moderate priority.

Maximum immature granulocyte percentage in 13–24h may indicate evolving infection/systemic inflammation, which can contribute to AKI risk. Among IG summaries here it best preserves a potentially meaningful extreme signal, though still indirect.
Minimum immature granulocyte percentage is not clinically informative for AKI risk and mainly reflects baseline/measurement noise. Dominated by max/mean summaries and low sparse-selection priority.

Minimum immature granulocyte percentage in 13–24h provides little pathophysiologic information compared with peaks or sustained elevation. Redundant and unlikely to contribute unique predictive value.

Measurement frequency of immature granulocyte percent is mainly a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy rather than a renal physiology signal. Low unique value for AKI prediction and likely dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Counts how often immature granulocyte percent was checked in 13–24h, reflecting suspicion/complexity more than kidney injury. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct creatinine trajectory features.
Later measurement frequency may correlate with illness severity, but it is still an indirect proxy and not a biomarker of renal dysfunction. Redundant with better severity/renal trajectory variables.

Whether TEG fibrinolysis was measured early post-op reflects bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and practice patterns. Very indirect for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and likely noise/redundant with stronger physiologic predictors.

TEG fibrinolysis measurement in 13–24h is a clinician-action proxy tied to coagulation concerns, not kidney function. Low incremental predictive value for AKI compared with creatinine trajectory features.
Late TEG measurement may mark complicated course, but remains an indirect monitoring/procedure proxy. Unlikely to add unique signal beyond direct renal markers available in this batch.

Average early post-op creatinine ratio captures initial renal response but is susceptible to immediate post-bypass dilution/shift effects. Useful but dominated by later-window and/or extreme summaries for sparse selection.

Mean creatinine ratio in 13–24h reflects early trajectory toward AKI and is closer to the outcome than 0–12h. Still somewhat redundant with max in the same/later window but often carries sustained-change information.
Mean creatinine ratio in 25–36h is the closest available direct renal function trajectory to Hour 60. High unique predictive value in a sparse model as a near-direct precursor of the target.
Peak creatinine ratio in 0–12h can capture an early renal insult, but early peaks may be transient/noisy post-op. Likely less stable than later-window peaks for predicting Hour-60 ratio.

Peak creatinine ratio in 13–24h captures worsening renal function and acute rises that are prognostic for later creatinine. Some redundancy with mean and later-window measures, but extremes often add value.

Peak creatinine ratio in 25–36h is a strong near-term indicator of evolving AKI and closely tracks the eventual Hour-60 ratio. Among the highest-priority sparse predictors due to directness and timing.

Minimum early creatinine ratio often reflects hemodilution or lab variability rather than kidney injury. Provides limited unique predictive value and is dominated by later and/or peak creatinine summaries.
Minimum creatinine ratio in 13–24h is less pathophysiologically aligned with AKI risk than rising/peak values. Likely redundant and lower yield for sparse selection.

Minimum creatinine ratio in 25–36h may reflect recovery/hemodilution but is generally less informative than mean/max for predicting subsequent elevation. Lower priority given redundancy with stronger creatinine trajectory features.
Number/frequency of creatinine measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy. Adds little unique physiologic information beyond the creatinine values themselves and is early/noisy.

Counts how often creatinine ratio was measured in 13–24h, reflecting monitoring intensity/clinician concern rather than renal physiology. Weak, indirect proxy and likely redundant with actual creatinine values if available, so low sparse-selection priority.

Measurement frequency of creatinine ratio in the closest window (25–36h) may weakly proxy evolving AKI prompting repeat labs. Still primarily clinician-behavior/acuity signal and dominated by true renal biomarkers, so only modest value.

Binary indicator of hyperoncotic (25%) albumin use early post-op, often reflecting hypotension/third spacing or attempts to limit volume. Indirect and confounded by practice patterns; early window is noisy, so weak sparse-model candidate.

Binary 25% albumin use in 13–24h can indicate ongoing hemodynamic instability or fluid management complexity linked to renal hypoperfusion risk. Still a treatment/proxy variable and less informative than direct hemodynamics/urine output, but may add some unique signal.

Binary 25% albumin use in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture persistent shock/volume management issues associated with AKI trajectory. Remains clinician-driven and potentially redundant with other severity markers, so moderate priority.

Amount of 5% albumin given in 0–12h mainly reflects routine post-bypass volume resuscitation and institutional practice. Highly confounded and early/noisy, offering little unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.

5% albumin dose in 13–24h may reflect ongoing volume needs and capillary leak, indirectly related to renal perfusion. Still largely a treatment proxy and likely redundant with fluid balance/pressors, so low priority.

5% albumin dose in 25–36h could indicate continued resuscitation needs closer to the outcome window. However it is clinician-driven and nonspecific, so unlikely to survive sparse selection unless other perfusion markers are absent.

Analgesic dosing (acetaminophen-codeine) reflects pain control/sedation strategy rather than kidney injury biology. Minimal direct link to AKI and high practice-pattern noise, so very low sparse-model value.

Acetaminophen-codeine dose in 13–24h is primarily a comfort/analgesia marker and may correlate weakly with extubation status. Not a meaningful renal predictor and likely pure noise for sparse selection.
Later acetaminophen-codeine dosing mainly indicates ongoing analgesia needs and care pathway differences. No direct renal signal and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Acetaminophen dose early post-op is routine and reflects analgesia/fever management rather than renal perfusion or injury. Very indirect and dominated by true renal/hemodynamic features.
Acetaminophen dosing in 13–24h is a nonspecific medication-use variable with little mechanistic connection to AKI. Likely redundant with general care intensity and not worth sparse feature budget.
Acetaminophen dose in 25–36h remains a nonspecific treatment marker; any association with creatinine is likely confounded by care pathway. Very low priority for sparse linear modeling.

Allopurinol use reflects chronic gout/hyperuricemia management and is unlikely to change acutely post-op. Weak, mostly comorbidity/practice signal with little incremental value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Continuation of allopurinol in 13–24h is largely medication reconciliation and not a near-term AKI biomarker. Minimal unique predictive information for sparse selection.

Allopurinol use in this window mainly reflects chronic gout/urate management rather than acute renal perfusion or evolving AKI. Likely low prevalence and weak, indirect signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Aminocaproic acid is an antifibrinolytic given perioperatively to reduce bleeding. It is mostly a surgical practice/bleeding proxy and early (0–12h) exposure is unlikely to add unique predictive value for later creatinine rise.

Continued/recorded aminocaproic acid use reflects ongoing bleeding management or institutional protocol rather than kidney physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Late aminocaproic acid use may indicate persistent bleeding/re-exploration risk and associated hemodynamic stress, which can relate to AKI. Still indirect and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal markers, so low priority.

Amiodarone dosing reflects postoperative atrial/ventricular arrhythmia treatment and illness complexity. Early (0–12h) dosing is a weak, indirect proxy for renal outcomes and is likely dominated by direct perfusion/renal features.

Amiodarone in 13–24h suggests ongoing arrhythmia burden that can correlate with low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. However it remains an indirect treatment proxy and usually redundant with direct hemodynamic variables.

Amiodarone use closest to the target window can mark persistent arrhythmias/low-output physiology that may precede AKI. Among the amiodarone epochs this is the most temporally informative, but still indirect versus direct renal/perfusion markers.

Amlodipine administration typically reflects resumption of chronic antihypertensive therapy rather than acute ICU physiology. Early post-op dosing is uncommon and not a direct renal signal, so very low sparse-model priority.

Amlodipine in this period mainly indicates stable blood pressure allowing oral meds, a weak proxy for recovery rather than a driver of creatinine rise. Likely redundant with vitals/vasopressor needs and low value for sparse selection.

Later amlodipine use may signal hemodynamic stability and transition to oral regimen, which can inversely correlate with AKI risk. Still a clinician-behavior/stability proxy and not a direct renal marker, so only modest value.

Ampicillin exposure reflects antibiotic choice/prophylaxis or infection concern rather than kidney function. In 0–12h it is largely protocol-driven and unlikely to add unique predictive information for creatinine_ratio.

Antibiotic use in 13–24h may indicate suspected infection/sepsis, which can contribute to AKI risk. Still indirect and confounded by practice patterns; weaker than direct hemodynamic/renal trajectory features.

Continued ampicillin at 25–36h can proxy evolving infection/inflammation closer to the outcome window. However it remains a treatment/indication proxy and is unlikely to outperform direct physiologic predictors in a sparse model.

Angiotensin II is a rescue vasopressor indicating severe vasodilatory shock and renal hypoperfusion risk. Early use is informative for severity but 0–12h can be noisy post-bypass and may be redundant with other vasopressor/hemodynamic measures.

Angiotensin II requirement in 13–24h signals persistent vasoplegia/shock beyond immediate post-op effects, a strong precursor to AKI. This is a relatively unique marker of refractory shock likely to survive sparse screening.

Angiotensin II dose at 25–36h is a near-term indicator of ongoing refractory shock and renal hypoperfusion closest to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Within this batch it provides the strongest, most unique severity/perfusion signal for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Early postoperative apixaban exposure mainly reflects anticoagulation strategy/bleeding risk rather than renal physiology. Likely weak and confounded, with little unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Apixaban dosing in this window is largely a clinician-behavior proxy (AF/VTE prophylaxis decisions) and not a direct AKI precursor. Any association with renal function is indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics/diuretics/labs not in this batch.
Later apixaban use may weakly proxy recovery/stability and renal clearance considerations closer to the target time. Still indirect and highly confounded, so low priority for sparse selection.

Immediate post-op aspirin reflects antiplatelet protocol and surgical bleeding considerations, not kidney injury trajectory. Early timing and high practice-pattern confounding make it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Aspirin administration in this period is mostly routine post-cardiac surgery care and offers minimal unique signal about evolving AKI. Likely redundant with overall care pathways rather than renal pathophysiology.

Later aspirin dosing still primarily captures protocol adherence and bleeding risk rather than renal hypoperfusion or injury. Unlikely to survive sparse feature selection given weak mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise.
Early statin dosing reflects chronic medication continuation and perioperative practice patterns. It is not a near-term marker of renal dysfunction and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Atorvastatin in this window is largely a continuation-of-care signal and not sensitive to acute renal trajectory. Any effect on creatinine is small and indirect, so low sparse-model priority.
Later statin dosing remains a weak proxy for general stability/med reconciliation rather than AKI evolution. Minimal unique information for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Bisacodyl use indicates bowel regimen/constipation management and is not physiologically linked to AKI prediction. Strongly a care-pattern proxy with negligible unique renal signal.
Bowel regimen administration reflects feeding status and routine ICU care rather than renal perfusion or injury. Unlikely to contribute meaningfully in a sparse linear model.
Later bisacodyl may weakly correlate with recovery/enteral tolerance but remains an indirect, noisy proxy. Very low priority compared with direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Bivalirudin use early post-op can indicate HIT concern/ECMO/complex anticoagulation, which correlates with severity and potential AKI risk. Still mostly a treatment-choice proxy and early timing reduces specificity for Hour-60 creatinine.

Bivalirudin in this window may mark ongoing high-acuity support or HIT management, indirectly associated with renal dysfunction risk. However it is confounded by indication and not a direct renal marker, so modest priority.

Continued bivalirudin closer to the target time can proxy persistent critical illness/advanced support where AKI is common. Among anticoagulants here it may carry the most unique severity signal, but remains indirect.

Early bumetanide dose reflects diuretic requirement/diuretic responsiveness and attempts to manage volume overload, which are closely tied to renal perfusion and evolving AKI. Despite early-window noise, diuretic need is a strong, relatively unique predictor within this medication-heavy batch.

Loop diuretic exposure in the 13–24h window reflects oliguria/volume overload management and evolving renal/cardiac dysfunction. Moderately predictive but partly a clinician-behavior proxy and dominated by the closer 25–36h dose.

Loop diuretic requirement closest to the target time can capture persistent low urine output/volume overload and worsening cardiorenal physiology. Stronger sparse-model candidate than earlier bumetanide due to temporal proximity and trajectory signal.

Early use of a phosphate binder mainly reflects pre-existing CKD/ESRD medication reconciliation rather than acute post-op renal trajectory. Weak and likely redundant with baseline creatinine/comorbidity features.

Calcium acetate use is largely a chronic CKD/ESRD marker and not a direct driver of short-term creatinine rise. Limited unique value for sparse selection versus stronger renal perfusion/diuresis signals.

Later phosphate-binder administration may indicate recognized renal dysfunction or hyperphosphatemia, but remains mostly a treatment/CKD proxy. Slightly higher than earlier epochs due to timing, yet still low priority.

Calcium carbonate is commonly given for supplementation/antacid or chronic CKD-mineral bone disease and is not a specific AKI signal. Early post-op dosing is noisy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Represents calcium carbonate administration, which is weakly related to acute kidney injury and often reflects routine care. Provides little unique predictive information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Later calcium carbonate use could correlate with CKD management or electrolyte abnormalities, but is still indirect and clinician-driven. Low-to-modest priority compared with diuretic requirement.

IV calcium chloride early post-op often reflects transfusion/citrate load, hemodynamic instability, or electrolyte correction rather than kidney-specific injury. Some association with severity but early timing and nonspecificity limit sparse-model value.

Continued need for IV calcium chloride may indicate ongoing instability or massive transfusion effects, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a nonspecific treatment proxy and typically redundant with direct hemodynamic/lab features.

Later IV calcium chloride suggests persistent electrolyte derangements or ongoing critical illness closer to the outcome window. More temporally informative than earlier epochs, but remains indirect and treatment-driven.

IV calcium gluconate early post-op is usually given for hypocalcemia/hyperkalemia management and reflects general instability. Early window is noisy and the signal is indirect for predicting creatinine rise.

Calcium gluconate use in 13–24h may track electrolyte issues (e.g., hyperkalemia) that accompany AKI, but it is still a clinician-response proxy. Moderate redundancy with direct potassium/acid-base measures if available.

Later calcium gluconate administration can be a near-term marker of hyperkalemia or worsening renal handling of electrolytes closer to Hour 60. Useful but indirect, so below top-tier renal predictors.
Early carvedilol dosing mainly reflects home medication continuation and clinician preference, not acute renal physiology. Likely redundant with baseline comorbidity and hemodynamic variables.
Carvedilol administration in 13–24h is a weak proxy for cardiovascular history/stability and not a direct AKI precursor. Low unique predictive value for sparse selection.

Beta-blocker exposure in the 25–36h window reflects hemodynamic management and underlying cardiac status. It is an indirect proxy for stability and is likely dominated by direct perfusion/renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.

Early postoperative cefepime use mainly reflects empiric antibiotic choice and clinician concern for infection. It is a weak, behavior-driven proxy and the 0–12h window is noisy for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Cefepime in 13–24h may indicate evolving infection/sepsis risk, which can contribute to AKI. Still largely a treatment/indication proxy and not a direct renal signal, so only modest value.

Later cefepime use (25–36h) is closer to the outcome and may track ongoing infection severity that can worsen renal function. However it remains indirect and confounded by prescribing practices, limiting sparse-model usefulness.

Binary ceftriaxone exposure early post-op reflects prophylaxis/empiric coverage rather than kidney physiology. Strongly clinician-driven and early-window noise makes it unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Ceftriaxone use in 13–24h is still mostly an indication/prescribing proxy with limited direct linkage to creatinine rise. Redundant with broader infection/severity features that would typically outperform it.

Ceftriaxone exposure at 25–36h could mark ongoing infection management closer to Hour 60. Nonetheless it is a weak indirect predictor and likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/lab indicators of AKI risk.

Clonidine dosing early post-op reflects sedation/analgesia and blood pressure control practices. It is an indirect, highly confounded signal and the immediate post-op period is particularly noisy for AKI prediction.

Clonidine in 13–24h may correlate with ongoing sedation needs and hemodynamic lability, which can relate to renal hypoperfusion. Still largely a management proxy and likely dominated by direct BP/vasopressor/urine output features.
Later clonidine use is temporally closer and may reflect persistent ICU course and hemodynamic management. However it remains indirect and confounded, so low-to-moderate sparse selection priority.

Early clopidogrel reflects antiplatelet strategy (e.g., CABG/PCI context) rather than renal physiology. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio compared with direct renal/perfusion markers.
Clopidogrel in 13–24h is mainly a procedural/indication marker and not a near-direct AKI precursor. Any association with creatinine is likely weak and confounded, so poor sparse-model candidate.
Later clopidogrel use continues to reflect antiplatelet management rather than kidney injury trajectory. It is unlikely to contribute incremental predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Colchicine early post-op typically indicates pericarditis prophylaxis/treatment or inflammatory management. It has minimal direct relationship to AKI risk and is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Colchicine in 13–24h remains an indication-driven medication feature with weak mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise. Any signal is likely redundant with broader inflammation/severity markers.
Colchicine use at 25–36h reflects ongoing inflammatory/pericardial management rather than renal perfusion or function. It is a low-yield predictor for creatinine ratio in a sparse linear model.

D10 in lactated Ringer’s given in the immediate post-op window, mainly reflecting dextrose/maintenance fluid choice. Weak, highly practice-driven signal and early (0–12h) timing makes it unlikely to add unique AKI prediction value in a sparse model.

D10 LR use in 13–24h may reflect ongoing need for dextrose-containing fluids (e.g., hypoglycemia risk, nutrition delays) and some fluid exposure. Still largely clinician-behavior/maintenance-fluid selection and redundant with broader fluid balance/hemodynamics not present here.

D10 LR use closest to the target window could correlate with continued ICU complexity and fluid administration. However it remains an indirect proxy and not a direct renal perfusion/toxin signal, so low priority for sparse selection.

Binary indicator of D10W administration early after surgery, typically for glucose management or maintenance fluids. Mostly practice/monitoring driven and early transient, offering little unique information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Amount of D10W in 13–24h captures some ongoing dextrose/IV fluid exposure and illness-management context. Still indirect for kidney injury and likely redundant with overall fluid strategy, so only a weak candidate.

Binary D10W use in 25–36h may mark persistent need for IV dextrose/maintenance fluids closer to outcome. It is still a nonspecific clinician-behavior proxy rather than a renal insult marker, so low sparse-model priority.

Volume of D5 0.45% NS early post-op reflects hypotonic maintenance fluid exposure and overall fluid administration. Some relevance via dilutional effects/volume status, but early timing and nonspecificity limit unique predictive value.
Binary use of D5 0.45% NS in 13–24h indicates ongoing maintenance fluid strategy. Indirect and practice-pattern driven, with limited specificity for renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI.

Binary D5 LR administration in the first 12 hours mainly reflects maintenance fluid choice during immediate post-op instability. Highly protocol-driven and early, so minimal unique signal for later creatinine rise.

Binary D5 LR use in 13–24h may correlate with ongoing fluid needs and clinician preference for balanced crystalloids. Still indirect for AKI and redundant with overall fluid exposure, so low priority.

Binary D5 NS use in 13–24h may weakly relate to chloride-rich fluid strategy and ongoing IV fluid dependence. Still largely clinician-choice driven and not a direct renal dysfunction marker.

Binary D5 NS use in 25–36h is closer to the target and could reflect continued fluid administration with potential hyperchloremia risk. However, without dose magnitude it is a weak proxy and likely redundant with other fluid variables.

Binary D50W administration in 0–12h typically indicates treatment of hypoglycemia or insulin-related management. It can mark metabolic instability/diabetes management complexity but is indirect and early, so only modest sparse-model value.

Dialysate infusion implies renal replacement therapy (e.g., CRRT) initiation/continuation, which is near-direct evidence of severe renal dysfunction. Even early, this is a strong unique indicator for elevated future creatinine ratio.

Hydromorphone PCA dosing reflects analgesia delivery and patient interaction rather than renal physiology. Early post-op dosing is highly practice-dependent and offers minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Dobutamine use indicates low cardiac output support, a plausible driver of renal hypoperfusion. However, 0–12h is confounded by immediate post-bypass instability and may be redundant with MAP/pressors/lactate features.

Dobutamine requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent low-output state, a strong precursor to AKI via renal hypoperfusion. This window is more prognostic than 0–12h and may add unique value beyond generic severity markers.

Docusate is a bowel regimen medication and does not directly reflect renal physiology. Early post-op use is largely routine care and a weak clinician-behavior proxy, unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Docusate dosing mainly indicates bowel regimen ordering rather than kidney injury risk. Adds little unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and is dominated by true hemodynamic/renal markers.
Later docusate use still reflects routine constipation prophylaxis and care patterns more than renal trajectory. Even with closer timing, it is not a meaningful AKI precursor for sparse modeling.

Enoxaparin dosing is primarily VTE prophylaxis/anticoagulation practice and is often withheld/adjusted for bleeding risk rather than kidney trajectory. In the immediate 0–12h period it is especially protocol-driven and weakly informative for creatinine ratio.

Enoxaparin administration may indirectly reflect renal function (dose adjustments/withholding) and overall stability, but it is a clinician-behavior proxy with many confounders. Provides limited unique value compared with direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Later enoxaparin use can signal recovery/stability and sometimes adequate renal function for standard dosing, but remains indirect and confounded by bleeding/procedure considerations. Likely low yield for sparse selection despite closer timing.

Ephedrine use indicates transient hypotension treatment, often peri-anesthetic and short-lived. In 0–12h it is noisy and less predictive of sustained renal hypoperfusion leading to Hour-60 creatinine rise.

Ephedrine use at 25–36h may capture ongoing episodic hypotension closer to the outcome window, which can contribute to renal insult. Still a coarse binary/bolus proxy and likely weaker than continuous inotrope/pressor dose signals.

Esmolol use reflects rate control/arrhythmia management rather than renal perfusion, and early post-op administration is often situational. It is unlikely to provide unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
