Later esmolol may indicate persistent atrial fibrillation/tachycardia management and could correlate with cardiac dysfunction, but remains an indirect proxy. Closer timing modestly increases potential signal, yet it is still low priority versus inotrope/pressor doses.

Etomidate exposure early post-op reflects induction/sedation choice and may proxy hemodynamic instability. It is an indirect, clinician-behavior signal and early (0–12h) noise, so low sparse-model priority.

Etomidate use in 13–24h suggests ongoing sedation needs/instability rather than a direct renal insult. Still largely a treatment proxy and uncommon beyond induction, so unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Late etomidate use (25–36h) could indicate prolonged critical illness or procedures, indirectly associated with AKI risk. However it remains a nonspecific clinician-driven marker and is dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal features (not in this batch).

Ezetimibe is a chronic lipid-lowering medication and perioperative dosing mainly reflects home-med continuation. It has minimal mechanistic or near-term linkage to 60h creatinine changes and is low-value for sparse selection.

Ezetimibe dosing at 25–36h remains a chronic-therapy proxy rather than physiology. Any association would be confounded by care patterns and offers little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Famotidine is stress-ulcer prophylaxis and dosing reflects routine ICU protocols. It is a weak, nonspecific care-pattern marker and early timing further reduces usefulness for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Famotidine in 13–24h is still largely protocol-driven and not a renal biomarker. Any signal is indirect (severity/ICU stay) and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Fentanyl PCA dose reflects analgesia needs and patient interaction/ventilation status early post-op. It is an indirect severity/trajectory proxy and early window is noisy, so low priority.

Fentanyl dose in 13–24h may proxy ongoing ventilation, delirium, or instability, which can relate to AKI risk. Still largely redundant with other severity markers and not kidney-specific.

Later fentanyl requirement (25–36h) is closer to the 60h target and may reflect persistent critical illness/ventilation and hypotension risk. It remains indirect but is the most temporally relevant fentanyl aggregate in this batch.

Intranasal fluticasone is a chronic rhinitis medication and perioperative administration reflects home-med continuation. It has no meaningful short-term relationship to postoperative creatinine trajectory and is not a sparse-model keeper.

Intranasal fluticasone dosing is a low-acuity outpatient-type medication signal. Even though closer to Hour 60, it provides negligible unique information about creatinine rise.

Folic acid administration is typically a nutrition/supplementation marker, not a driver of acute kidney injury. Early post-op timing further increases noise and makes it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Folic acid use mainly proxies baseline supplementation or anemia workup rather than evolving renal dysfunction. Adds little unique predictive value for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Later folic acid dosing still reflects supplementation/med reconciliation rather than hemodynamics or nephrotoxic exposure. Unlikely to survive feature budget constraints.

Loop diuretic dosing reflects attempts to manage volume status/oliguria and can correlate with early renal stress. However 0–12h is confounded by bypass-related shifts and practice variation, so it’s less reliable than later dosing.

Furosemide requirement in 13–24h often signals persistent congestion, low urine output, or clinician response to evolving AKI/volume overload. This window is more prognostic than 0–12h and may add unique value in a sparse model.

Furosemide dosing closest to the outcome captures ongoing diuretic resistance/volume management and is a strong proxy for evolving renal dysfunction and fluid status. Among this batch, it is the most directly informative and least dominated by earlier epochs.
Gabapentin use is primarily an analgesia regimen marker and not a direct renal injury signal. Early dosing is routine and unlikely to add unique predictive information for creatinine ratio.
Gabapentin dosing reflects pain-control strategy and may be held/adjusted for sedation rather than kidney function. Weak, indirect proxy and not competitive for sparse selection.
Later gabapentin may be reduced in renal impairment (dose adjustment), giving a small indirect signal. Still heavily confounded by pain/sedation practices, so low priority versus diuretic/fluids.
Guaifenesin is a symptomatic respiratory medication and not linked to renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. Likely noise for predicting Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Guaifenesin use mainly reflects cough/secretions management and general care patterns. Provides minimal unique renal prognostic information.
Even in the closest window, guaifenesin dosing is a weak clinician-behavior proxy unrelated to AKI biology. Should be excluded under a sparse feature budget.

Half-normal saline volume reflects early fluid administration which can affect renal perfusion and dilution, but 0–12h is highly confounded by operative resuscitation and routine protocols. Likely redundant with better hemodynamic/volume balance features not shown here.

Ongoing hypotonic crystalloid administration in 13–24h may indicate continued resuscitation/maintenance needs and can relate to perfusion and fluid overload risk. Moderately useful, but still an indirect and practice-dependent proxy compared with furosemide dosing.

Represents hypotonic IV fluid administration in the closest (25–36h) window, which can reflect ongoing fluid management and volume status. Moderately informative as a proxy for fluid strategy/overload risk, but indirect for AKI and often redundant with stronger hemodynamic/urine output/diuretic signals (not in this batch).

Early postoperative anticoagulation dose mainly reflects protocolized care (e.g., line patency/bridging) rather than renal physiology. In 0–12h it is especially noisy and unlikely to add unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.

Anticoagulation dosing in 13–24h is largely clinician/protocol driven and not a direct kidney injury marker. Weak sparse-model candidate unless capturing rare complications, which is not specific here.

Heparin dose at 25–36h reflects ongoing anticoagulation decisions and device/arrhythmia management more than renal perfusion or injury. Despite closer timing, it remains a low-specificity proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Hydralazine use indicates treatment of hypertension/afterload in the immediate post-op period. It is an indirect hemodynamic-management proxy and early window effects are noisy, so it has limited unique value for predicting AKI trajectory.

Opioid/acetaminophen analgesic dosing reflects pain control and extubation/comfort practices rather than renal pathophysiology. Early post-op dosing is highly practice-driven and unlikely to add unique predictive signal.

Hydrocortisone exposure early post-op can indicate vasoplegia/shock treatment or adrenal support, which correlates with illness severity. However 0–12h is noisy and steroid use is a treatment proxy rather than a direct renal signal.

Hypertonic saline use early post-op may reflect hyponatremia management or intracranial/volume considerations, often rare and protocol-driven. It is not a direct AKI marker and early timing reduces reliability for 60h creatinine prediction.

Hypertonic saline in 13–24h indicates specific electrolyte/osmolality management and may correlate with complexity of care. Still indirect for renal injury and likely too sparse/heterogeneous to be a high-priority predictor.

Hypertonic saline use at 25–36h can signal ongoing dysnatremia or aggressive osmotic/volume management closer to the outcome window. Moderately informative as a marker of complexity and fluid/electrolyte derangement, but indirect and not a primary renal biomarker.

Early postoperative NSAID exposure can contribute to renal hypoperfusion via prostaglandin inhibition. However 0–12h dosing is often minimal/avoided after cardiac surgery and is an indirect, clinician-behavior signal with limited unique value for Hour-60 creatinine.

NSAID dosing in this window may better reflect ongoing analgesic strategy and potential nephrotoxic exposure once hemodynamics stabilize. Compared with 0–12h it is more temporally informative and could add some unique signal, though still indirect.

Later NSAID exposure is closest to the outcome window and may capture sustained nephrotoxic/renal-perfusion risk when AKI is evolving. Among the ibuprofen epochs this is the strongest sparse-model candidate due to temporal proximity and plausible causal link.

Inhaled acetylcysteine is typically used as a mucolytic for airway secretion management, not a renal-directed therapy. It is largely a proxy for pulmonary care intensity and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Represents ongoing mucolytic use for respiratory secretions. This is an indirect severity/airway-management marker and is unlikely to survive sparse selection for predicting renal dysfunction.

Later mucolytic use may correlate with prolonged ventilation/secretions but remains non-specific and clinician-driven. Even with better timing, it is a weak and redundant proxy for overall illness rather than kidney injury.

Inhaled long-acting bronchodilator use reflects COPD/asthma management rather than renal physiology. Early post-op administration is routine/variable and offers little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine.
Continued bronchodilator dosing mainly indicates chronic lung disease or ventilator/airway management. It is an indirect comorbidity/treatment marker with low priority for sparse renal prediction.

Later bronchodilator use may track prolonged respiratory support but remains non-specific and treatment-pattern driven. Unlikely to add incremental predictive value for AKI beyond stronger hemodynamic/lab features (not in this batch).
Inhaled corticosteroid use reflects airway inflammation management and chronic pulmonary disease. It is not a direct renal insult/biomarker and is low-yield for sparse selection.
Ongoing inhaled steroid dosing is largely a comorbidity/treatment proxy. It provides minimal unique information about evolving postoperative kidney dysfunction.
Later inhaled steroid use may correlate with prolonged ventilation but remains non-specific and clinician-driven. Even with closer timing, it is unlikely to be selected in a sparse renal model.
Short-acting bronchodilator use indicates bronchospasm/airway management and is common perioperatively. It is an indirect severity/treatment marker with little direct linkage to creatinine rise.
Represents continued need for bronchodilation, often reflecting pulmonary comorbidity or ventilator course. Low specificity for renal outcomes and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Later bronchodilator requirement may proxy prolonged respiratory issues but is still far from a renal signal. In sparse selection it would be dominated by more direct kidney/hemodynamic predictors.

Nebulized saline use reflects airway secretion management rather than renal physiology. Likely a weak, clinician-practice proxy with little unique signal for AKI/creatinine trajectory.
Later nebulized saline still primarily indicates pulmonary toilet/ventilator care needs. Not a direct kidney marker and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics/renal labs.

Early inhaled bronchodilator dosing reflects bronchospasm/ventilator management in the immediate post-op period. It is indirect and noisy for Hour-60 creatinine prediction and dominated by more renal-relevant features.
Levalbuterol dose is mainly a pulmonary severity/therapy marker rather than renal perfusion or injury. Provides minimal unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse linear model.

Later bronchodilator requirement may correlate with ongoing respiratory failure and overall illness severity. Still an indirect proxy and likely redundant with stronger severity/hemodynamic variables, so low priority.

Basal insulin use reflects diabetes status and glycemic management early after surgery. Diabetes is a renal-risk context, but this dosing is treatment-dependent and early/noisy, so weak sparse-model value.
Basal insulin in this window continues to proxy underlying diabetes and clinician glycemic strategy. It is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with comorbidity indicators or glucose measurements.
Later basal insulin may better reflect persistent hyperglycemia/diabetes burden closer to the target time. Still largely a treatment proxy with limited unique renal signal, so low-moderate priority.
Detemir dosing is another basal insulin marker of diabetes/glycemic control. Highly redundant with glargine and influenced by prescribing patterns, limiting sparse selection usefulness.
Basal insulin requirement in 13–24h reflects diabetes management rather than kidney injury. Redundant with other insulin features and weaker than direct renal perfusion/renal function markers.
Later detemir use is temporally closer and may track ongoing metabolic stress. However it remains a clinician-behavior proxy and redundant with other basal insulin variables.

Short-acting insulin dose reflects early hyperglycemia and stress response. Immediate post-op dosing is noisy and treatment-driven, so it is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Lispro requirement in 13–24h can capture sustained stress hyperglycemia, which correlates with illness severity and AKI risk. Still indirect and therapy-dependent, but may add some signal beyond basal insulin.

Later short-acting insulin needs are closer to Hour 60 and may reflect ongoing metabolic stress/inflammation associated with evolving AKI. Among insulin features, this timing is most likely to retain modest unique predictive value.

Regular insulin infusion dose indicates ICU-level glycemic control needs and physiologic stress early post-op. It is still treatment-driven and early, but may proxy severity more strongly than subcutaneous dosing.

Ongoing insulin infusion in 13–24h suggests persistent stress hyperglycemia/critical illness and often co-occurs with vasopressors, infection, or organ dysfunction linked to AKI. Within this batch it is one of the stronger, less-redundant severity proxies likely to survive sparse selection.

Total insulin infusion dose reflects stress hyperglycemia/insulin resistance and overall illness severity in the closest window to the outcome. It can add some predictive value for AKI risk but is indirect and often redundant with stronger hemodynamic/lab renal signals.

Exposure to iodinated contrast is a direct nephrotoxic risk factor, but this early 0–12h window can be noisy and may precede later, more relevant exposures. Useful if contrast use is uncommon/variable, but not the strongest timing for sparse selection.

Contrast exposure in 13–24h is a meaningful nephrotoxic insult with plausible temporal linkage to creatinine rise by hour 60. Likely to provide unique signal beyond generic severity markers if contrast is administered post-op.

Ipratropium dosing mainly reflects bronchospasm/COPD management and respiratory care patterns rather than renal physiology. In the early post-op window it is a weak, behavior-driven proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Continued ipratropium use indicates ongoing respiratory symptoms/therapy intensity but has little direct connection to kidney injury. Adds minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Late ipratropium dosing remains a respiratory-treatment proxy with no clear mechanistic link to AKI. Even with closer timing, it is dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.
Combination bronchodilator dosing reflects airway disease/ventilator weaning issues and clinician practice, not kidney function. Early dosing is especially nonspecific for hour-60 creatinine changes.
Ongoing bronchodilator requirement is an indirect marker of pulmonary status and care intensity. It is unlikely to add unique renal predictive signal in a sparse linear model.
Late bronchodilator dosing may correlate with severity but remains a weak, indirect proxy for AKI. Low priority compared with direct nephrotoxic exposure (contrast) and physiologic renal precursors.
Iron administration reflects anemia management/transfusion strategy rather than renal perfusion or injury. Early post-op dosing is unlikely to provide unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Iron dosing in 13–24h is still primarily a treatment-choice proxy for anemia and does not directly inform evolving AKI. Low sparse-selection priority.
Late iron administration remains disconnected from kidney injury mechanisms and is likely rare/noisy. Even near the outcome window, it is not a meaningful predictor of creatinine ratio.

Nitrate use indicates ischemic heart disease/afterload management and can relate to hemodynamics, but it is largely a clinician-behavior proxy. Early timing and indirect linkage make it a weak sparse-model candidate.

Nitrate administration may reflect ongoing cardiac ischemia or blood pressure management that could correlate with renal perfusion. Still indirect and likely redundant with direct BP/vasopressor features, so low priority.

Late nitrate use is closer to the outcome and may track persistent cardiac dysfunction affecting renal perfusion. However it remains a treatment proxy and is typically dominated by direct hemodynamic and renal markers.

Potassium phosphate repletion in the immediate postop window, often reflecting hypophosphatemia/shift or clinician practice rather than kidney injury. Early timing and strong confounding by nutrition/insulin/diuresis make it a weak, indirect AKI signal.

K-phos use in 13–24h may reflect ongoing electrolyte derangements and ICU intensity, sometimes seen with renal dysfunction but also with refeeding/diuretics. Some trajectory information, but still mostly a treatment proxy and likely redundant with direct renal/hemodynamic features.

K-phos repletion closer to the outcome can indicate evolving renal dysfunction (or aggressive diuresis) affecting phosphate handling and replacement needs. Later timing gives more predictive value, but it remains an indirect clinician-driven proxy rather than a direct renal biomarker.

Ketamine dose reflects sedation/analgesia strategy and agitation/hemodynamic tolerance in the immediate postop period. It has little direct mechanistic link to AKI and is largely clinician-behavior noise for sparse selection.

Ketamine dose at 25–36h still primarily captures sedation practice and delirium/agitation management. Not a unique or reliable predictor of creatinine rise compared with direct perfusion/renal markers.

Early LR volume reflects resuscitation and fluid shifts immediately after surgery/CPB. It may correlate with hemodynamic instability but is noisy and confounded by routine postop protocols, so low priority in a sparse model.

Levothyroxine dosing reflects chronic hypothyroidism replacement and medication reconciliation. It is not a meaningful short-term predictor of postoperative creatinine changes.

Topical lidocaine patch use reflects pain-control preference and is unlikely to affect renal perfusion or creatinine. Pure clinician-behavior signal with negligible predictive value.
Topical lidocaine patch use is primarily an analgesia marker with minimal direct renal physiologic signal. Likely reflects routine pain management and is low-yield for sparse AKI prediction.
Topical lidocaine patch dose in the closest window still mainly captures pain-control practice rather than kidney perfusion or injury. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Systemic lidocaine early post-op is usually for analgesia/antiarrhythmic protocols and is not a direct AKI precursor. The 0–12h timing is also noisy and dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features.

Systemic lidocaine dose may weakly proxy arrhythmia management or analgesia intensity but has little mechanistic link to subsequent creatinine rise. Low priority for sparse selection given indirectness.

Later systemic lidocaine remains a clinician-behavior/arrhythmia-treatment proxy rather than a renal biomarker. Even in the closest window, it is unlikely to survive sparse screening versus direct perfusion/nephrotoxin signals.

ACE-inhibitor exposure can reduce glomerular filtration pressure and may worsen creatinine in vulnerable patients, but immediate post-op dosing is uncommon and confounded by selection (given to stable patients). Early window and treatment-selection bias limit sparse-model value.
